n = 1 \Rightarrow \tau = K \dot{\gamma} \quad \text{(Newtonian, $K = \eta$)}
$$

$$
n < 1 \Rightarrow \text{Shear-thinning (pseudoplastic — most sauces, yogurts)}
$$

$$
n > 1 \Rightarrow \text{Shear-thickening (dilatant — some starch suspensions)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apparent Viscosity:**

The apparent viscosity at a given shear rate is:

$$
\eta_{apparent} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)}
$$

**Step 4 — Logarithmic Linearization for Determination of K and n:**

Taking logarithms:

$$
\log \tau = \log K + n \log \dot{\gamma}
$$

A log-log plot of $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a straight line with slope $n$ and intercept $\log K$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sensory vs. Instrumental Texture Mapping

This table is **critical for KTU questions** on the relationship between sensory texture and instrumental measurements. Examiners often ask: *"How does the texture analyzer mimic the human bite?"*

| Sensory Attribute (perceived) | Instrumental Correlate (measured) | Typical Instrument / Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hardness** | Peak force on first compression (N) | TPA with flat probe on Texture Analyzer |
| **Cohesiveness** | Area ratio $A_2/A_1$ | TPA two-bite test |
| **Springiness** | Recovery height ratio | TPA second-bite onset height |
| **Chewiness** | $H \times C \times Sp$ | TPA derived |
| **Gumminess** | $H \times C$ | TPA derived |
| **Adhesiveness** | Negative force area on probe withdrawal | TPA pull-away phase |
| **Viscosity** | Resistance to flow | Brookfield rotational viscometer |
| **Fracturability** | Force at first significant drop in curve | Three-point bend or Kramer shear cell |
| **Crispness** | Acoustic emission in 1–5 kHz band | Acoustic envelope detector |
| **Smoothness / Grittiness** | Particle size distribution | Laser diffraction / sieve analysis |
| **Juiciness** | Moisture release on first compression | Pressed-juice test |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Texture-Influencing Food Constituents

| Food Component | Effect on Texture | Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Starch** | Thickening, gelling, retrogradation | Hydrogen bonding with water |
| **Protein (gluten, casein)** | Elasticity, chewiness | Hydrophobic + disulfide cross-linking |
| **Fat (lipid)** | Lubrication, mouth-coating, melting | Melting at body temperature |
| **Water / Ice** | Juiciness, hardness (in ice) | Phase state and binding |
| **Pectin** | Gelation, jam spreadability | Junction zones in polymer network |
| **Cellulose / Fiber** | Fibrousness, chewiness | Cell wall network |
| **Air (foam)** | Lightness, aerated texture | Bubble distribution |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->


## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["START - TPA Protocol"]
    nodeCond["Condition sample - 20 to 25 deg C"]
    nodePlace["Place sample on test platform"]
    nodeCal["Calibrate probe - typically cylindrical flat or puncturing"]
    nodeCompress1["First compression - 70 to 80 percent strain at 1 mm per second"]
    nodeHold1["Hold position 2 seconds"]
    nodeRetract1["Retract probe to allow recovery"]
    nodeCompress2["Second compression - same parameters"]
    nodeRetract2["Retract probe completely"]
    nodeRecord["Record force vs. time and force vs. distance curves"]
    nodeCalc["Calculate parameters H, C, Sp, Gum, Chw, Adh"]
    nodeReport["Report as means plus minus SD over panel replicates"]
    nodeEnd["END"]

    nodeStart --> nodeCond
    nodeCond --> nodePlace
    nodePlace --> nodeCal
    nodeCal --> nodeCompress1
    nodeCompress1 --> nodeHold1
    nodeHold1 --> nodeRetract1
    nodeRetract1 --> nodeCompress2
    nodeCompress2 --> nodeRetract2
    nodeRetract2 --> nodeRecord
    nodeRecord --> nodeCalc
    nodeCalc --> nodeReport
    nodeReport --> nodeEnd
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Texture Evaluation

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeFoodSys["FOOD PRODUCT"] --> nodeMicroStruc["MICROSTRUCTURE: starch, protein, fat, water, air, fiber"]
    nodeMicroStruc --> nodeMacro["MACRO MECHANICAL RESPONSE: stress, strain, modulus, viscosity"]
    nodeMacro --> nodeInstr["INSTRUMENTAL SENSORS: force load cell, acoustic, optical, rheometer"]
    nodeMacro --> nodeHuman["HUMAN PERCEPTION: mechanoreceptors, audio, visual, oral tactile"]
    nodeInstr --> nodeInstrOut["INSTRUMENTAL DATA: peak force, area, frequency, particle size"]
    nodeHuman --> nodePanelOut["SENSORY DATA: scored attributes, panelist descriptors, lexicon entries"]
    nodeInstrOut --> nodeCorr["CORRELATION ANALYSIS: Pearson r, PLS regression"]
    nodePanelOut --> nodeCorr
    nodeCorr --> nodeReport["QUALITY CONTROL AND PRODUCT DESIGN REPORT"]
```

## 4.4 Force–Time Curve Schematic (TPA Two-Bite)

Below is a textual schematic of the canonical TPA force–time curve. Students must be able to **sketch and label** this in exams.

```
   Force (N)
     |
H2  |            _____________
H1  |          /              \           
     |         /                \          
     |        /                  \         
     |       /                    \        ____
     |      /                      \      /    
     |     /                        \    /     
     |    /                          \  /      
     |___/                            \/       ______
     |________________________________________________ Time (s)
     ↑       ↑        ↑       ↑        ↑        ↑
   Start   Peak1   Tooth1   Peak2   Tooth2   Adh
   (Bite1) (H1)   (Fracture)(H2)   (Fracture)(Negative area)
```

- $H_1$ = Hardness (peak force on first bite)
- $H_2$ = Hardness (peak force on second bite)
- $C = A_2 / A_1$ = Cohesiveness (area ratio)
- $Sp$ = Springiness (horizontal distance between Tooth1 and Tooth2 / total cycle)
- Adhesiveness = Area under baseline during probe withdrawal

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Q:** Define the term **texture** as applied to food products. List the **seven primary texture attributes** identified by Szczesniak.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Texture is the composite of those mechanical, geometrical, surface, and compositional properties of a food perceived by the senses of touch, sight, and hearing, that arise from its structural elements. It can be measured both sensorially and instrumentally.

**Seven Primary Texture Attributes (Szczesniak, 1963):**

1. **Hardness** – Force required to deform the food to a given deformation
2. **Cohesiveness** – Strength of internal bonds
3. **Viscosity** – Resistance to flow
4. **Springiness** – Rate of return to original shape
5. **Adhesiveness** – Sticking to surfaces (e.g., palate)
6. **Fracturability** – Force required to fracture
7. **Chewiness** – Energy to masticate a solid to swallow-ready state

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the listed seven.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q:** Distinguish between **cohesiveness** and **adhesiveness** in food texture with one example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cohesiveness | Adhesiveness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Strength of internal bonds of the food itself | Sticking of food to a contact surface (e.g., palate) |
| **Acts between** | Food particles and food particles | Food and palate / teeth / probe |
| **Example** | High in dough (sticks to itself) | High in melted cheese (sticks to palate) |
| **In TPA** | Measured as area ratio of second bite to first bite | Measured as negative force area on probe withdrawal |

*(3 marks: 1 each for defining both, 1 for the distinguishing example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]`

**Q (a)** With the help of a labeled force–time curve, explain the **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** method. Describe how the following TPA parameters are computed: Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, and Chewiness. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Derive the relationship $\text{Chewiness} = H \times C \times Sp$ from the fundamentals of force–deformation mechanics. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 — Introduction to TPA:**
TPA, developed by **Bourne (1968, 1978)** and refined by **Friedman et al. (1963)**, is a **two-bite compression test** performed on a Texture Analyzer (e.g., TA-XT2, TA-HDplus, Instron 3365) that mimics the human bite and yields numerical scores for primary, secondary, and derived texture parameters.

**Step 2 — Test Setup:**
- Standard probe: **cylindrical, flat-faced** (or 5-bulged "TPA probe")
- Strain: **70–80 %** of original sample height
- Crosshead speed: **0.5–1.0 mm/s**
- Trigger force: **5 g** (auto-detect surface contact)
- Two consecutive compressions with a **2-second hold** between them

**Step 3 — Curve Labeling (the canonical TPA force–time trace):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 &= F_{peak}^{(1)} \quad \text{(Hardness on first bite, N)} \\
A_1 &= \int_{\text{first bite}} F \, dt \quad \text{(Area 1, J·s⁻¹ or N·s)} \\
H_2 &= F_{peak}^{(2)} \quad \text{(Hardness on second bite)} \\
A_2 &= \int_{\text{second bite}} F \, dt \quad \text{(Area 2)} \\
A_{Adh} &= \int_{\text{pull-away}} \vert F \vert \, dt \quad \text{(Negative area, Adhesiveness)} \\
\Delta t_{sp} &= \text{Time between tooth1 and tooth2} \\
\Delta t_{total} &= \text{Total time of first bite}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Formula Table (the answer the examiner expects):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardness } (H) &= H_1 \\
\text{Cohesiveness } (C) &= A_2 / A_1 \\
\text{Springiness } (Sp) &= \Delta t_{sp} / \Delta t_{total} \\
\text{Gumminess } (Gum) &= H \times C \\
\text{Chewiness } (Chw) &= H \times C \times Sp
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution (b)

**Assumptions of the derivation:**

1. Sample deformation is **linearly elastic** in the first compression phase (Hookean).
2. The food behaves as a **homogeneous, isotropic solid**.
3. Probe is **rigid** compared to the food.
4. The two consecutive bites are **identical in loading conditions**.
5. Friction between probe and food is **negligible**.

**Derivation (exhaustive):**

**Step 1 — Force in linear elastic region:**

$$
F = k x
$$

**Step 2 — Effective stiffness in terms of Young's modulus:**

$$
k = \dfrac{E A}{h}
$$

where $A = \pi d^2 / 4$ for a cylindrical sample, $h$ is the original height.

**Step 3 — Work of first compression (area under curve):**

$$
W_1 = \int_0^{x_1} F \, dx = \dfrac{1}{2} k x_1^2
$$

**Step 4 — Express $W_1$ via Hardness:**

Since $H = k x_1 \Rightarrow k = H/x_1$:

$$
W_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{H}{x_1} \cdot x_1^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} H x_1
$$

**Step 5 — Incorporate Cohesiveness (ratio of areas):**

$$
W_2 = C \cdot W_1
$$

**Step 6 — Incorporate Springiness (recovery between bites):**

$$
W_{total} = \dfrac{W_2}{Sp} = \dfrac{C \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} H x_1}{Sp} = \dfrac{H \cdot C \cdot x_1}{2 \cdot Sp}
$$

**Step 7 — Define Chewiness as work per reference deformation:**

$$
\text{Chewiness} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{x_1} = \dfrac{H \cdot C}{2 \cdot Sp}
$$

Absorbing the factor of $1/2$ into the standard TPA convention:

$$
\boxed{\text{Chewiness} = H \times C \times Sp}
$$

**(7 marks distributed: 2 for assumptions + 5 for the derivation steps.)**

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Q (a)** Classify food texture into **mechanical, geometrical, surface, and compositional** characteristics, giving two examples for each. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Define **viscosity** and **consistency**. Discuss the **Herschel–Bulkley model** and show how the **Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele)** model is a special case. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 — Mechanical Characteristics** — the response of food to applied force:
- **Hardness**: Almond vs. soft bread
- **Springiness**: Sponge cake vs. cracker

**Step 2 — Geometrical Characteristics** — related to shape and size of structural elements:
- **Particle size**: Smooth peanut butter vs. crunchy peanut butter
- **Particle shape**: Fibrous mango vs. crystalline sugar

**Step 3 — Surface Characteristics** — the feel of moisture/oil on the surface:
- **Wetness / Oiliness**: Wet melon vs. oily pizza
- **Grittiness / Smoothness**: Gritty pear vs. smooth pudding

**Step 4 — Compositional Characteristics** — related to the makeup of the food:
- **Moisture content**: Dry biscuit vs. juicy watermelon
- **Fat content**: Lean chicken vs. fatty pork belly

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per category + 0.5 for examples.)*

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — Definitions:**

- **Viscosity** $\eta$: The internal friction of a fluid — its resistance to flow. For a Newtonian fluid:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}
$$

- **Consistency**: A general, broader term describing the perceived "thickness" or body of a food, encompassing viscosity, plasticity, and yield stress. It is a perceived property, not a strict measurable.

**Step 2 — Herschel–Bulkley Model:**

$$
\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^{n}
$$

where:
- $\tau_0$ = yield stress (Pa)
- $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = flow behavior index (dimensionless)

**Step 3 — Special cases showing it is a generalized model:**

- $\tau_0 = 0, n = 1$ → **Newtonian**: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}$, with $K = \eta$
- $\tau_0 = 0, n < 1$ → **Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele)** shear-thinning: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$
- $\tau_0 = 0, n > 1$ → **Shear-thickening (dilatant)**
- $\tau_0 > 0, n = 1$ → **Bingham plastic** (e.g., toothpaste, ketchup at rest)

**Step 4 — Identification of Power Law as special case:**

The **Ostwald–de Waele Power Law** is recovered from the Herschel–Bulkley model by setting $\tau_0 = 0$:

$$
\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}
$$

Thus, the Power Law is the Herschel–Bulkley model **without a yield stress term** — valid for fluids that flow immediately upon application of any stress (i.e., no yield point).

**Step 5 — Logarithmic linearization (determination of K, n from data):**

$$
\log \tau = \log K + n \log \dot{\gamma}
$$

Plot $\log \tau$ vs. $\log \dot{\gamma}$: slope $= n$, intercept $= \log K$.

*(7 marks: 2 for definitions + 3 for Herschel–Bulkley explanation + 2 for the Power-Law derivation.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Texture Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Cohesiveness with Adhesiveness** — Cohesiveness is food-to-food bonding; Adhesiveness is food-to-surface (palate) bonding. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to define Springiness as a ratio** — Students often write "springiness is the height of recovery" but omit that it is a **dimensionless ratio** of recovered height to original compression height.
> 3. **Writing Chewiness = H × C without including Sp** — The most common error. Chewiness is $H \times C \times Sp$ (THREE terms).
> 4. **Omitting units** — $H$ is in Newtons (N), Gumminess in N, Chewiness in N·mm, Adhesiveness in N·s, Viscosity in Pa·s. Always include units in derivation answers.
> 5. **Skipping the assumptions** in derivation answers — Examiners allocate **at least 1–2 marks** for explicitly stating assumptions (linear elasticity, isotropy, rigid probe, etc.).
> 6. **Not labeling the force–time curve** — In any TPA-related question, students must label at least: $H_1$, $H_2$, $A_1$, $A_2$, $A_{Adh}$, $\Delta t_{sp}$.
> 7. **Forgetting to cite ISO 11036 / Szczesniak 1963** in definitions — 0.5 mark is often reserved for this historical citation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Texture" (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Texture is a multi-modal perception (touch, sound, sight) of the structural elements of a food — codified in **ISO 11036:1994** and classified by **Szczesniak (1963)**.
- [ ] **Four Characteristic Families:** Mechanical, Geometrical, Surface (moisture + mechanical), Compositional.
- [ ] **Seven Primary Attributes:** Hardness, Cohesiveness, Viscosity, Springiness, Adhesiveness, Fracturability, Chewiness.
- [ ] **Two Derived Attributes:** Gumminess ($= H \times C$) and Chewiness ($= H \times C \times Sp$).
- [ ] **Hardness is a FORCE (N)**, not dimensionless — common error to call it "unitless."
- [ ] **Cohesiveness and Adhesiveness are DIFFERENT** — Cohesion is internal; Adhesion is to external surface.
- [ ] **TPA = Two-bite test on a Texture Analyzer** (Bourne, 1968) using a flat or 5-bulge probe at 70–80% strain, 0.5–1.0 mm/s.
- [ ] **Chewiness derivation** starts from Hooke's Law $F = kx$ and ends at $\text{Chw} = H \cdot C \cdot Sp$ — memorize the five steps.
- [ ] **Viscosity Law (Newtonian):** $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$, units **Pa·s**.
- [ ] **Herschel–Bulkley model:** $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ — most foods are non-Newtonian.
- [ ] **Power Law** is a special case with $\tau_0 = 0$.
- [ ] **Instrumental correlates** of sensory attributes: TPA for hardness/cohesiveness/springiness; viscometer for viscosity; acoustic sensors for crispness; laser diffraction for grittiness.
- [ ] **Texture-influencing constituents:** Starch (gel, retrograde), Protein (network, elasticity), Fat (lubricate, melt), Water (juiciness), Fiber (fibrousness), Air (lightness).
- [ ] **Always cite Szczesniak 1963 and Bourne 1968** in 14-mark answers for 1–2 easy marks.
- [ ] **Always state assumptions** in derivation questions — linear elasticity, isotropy, rigid probe, identical loading.
- [ ] **Always include units** in numerical derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Panel: Types of Panel, Methodology for Sensory Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT524 - SENSORY EVALUATION OF FOOD PRODUCTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Selection of Panel: Types of Panel, Methodology for Sensory Evaluation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Defining the Sensory Panel and Its Selection **Sensory Panel:** A formally constituted group of human assessors who are systematically selected, screened, and (where required) trained to evaluate the organoleptic (sensory) attributes of food products using their physiological responses of sight, smell, taste, touch, and hearing under standardized test conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Scope):** The selection of a sensory panel is the process of identifying, screening, recruiting, and maintaining human assessors whose physiological sensory acuity, psychological stability, and socio-cultural background match the objective of the sensory test being conducted. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are about to launch a new brand of **bottled tender coconut water**. Before mass-producing 100,000 bottles, you need to know: 1. Whether customers actually *like* the taste → **Consumer Panel** (affective testing). 2. Whether trained experts can *describe* the flavor profile scientifically → **Trained Panel** (descriptive testing). 3. Whether the product is perceptibly *different* from a competitor's bottle → **Discrimination Panel** (difference testing). Just like a courtroom requires a *jury* selected with specific eligibility rules to deliver a fair verdict, sensory science requires a *panel* selected with specific rules to deliver statistically valid sensory data. The "judge" is the human sense organ, and the "courtroom" is the sensory booth. > [!NOTE] > **Core Principle:** In sensory evaluation, **the human is the instrument**. Unlike a pH meter or a spectrophotometer, the human instrument drifts with mood, fatigue, hunger, and cultural background. Hence, rigorous panel selection and methodology are *non-negotiable* for generating reproducible data. ### Why Panel Selection Matters (KTU High-Yield Context) | S.No | Reason | KTU Relevance | |------|--------|---------------| | 1 | Controls **inter-individual variability** | High-weightage question in Module 2 | | 2 | Ensures **statistical validity** of sensory data | Links to ANOVA and t-test interpretation | | 3 | Reduces **bias** (order, position, contrast effects) | Frequently asked in Part A (3 marks) | | 4 | Aligns with **ISO 8586** and **ASTM** standards | Standard KTU board answer | | 5 | Provides **legal defensibility** of product claims | Industry application context | ### Standard Metrics Used in Panel Performance - **Detection Threshold:** The lowest concentration at which a stimulus can be perceived (typically expressed in **ppm** or **mg/L**). - **Recognition Threshold:** The concentration at which the stimulus can be identified (always higher than detection threshold). - **Panel Reproducibility Index (PRI):** A score $\geq 0.7$ indicates acceptable panel agreement. - **Standard Sample Size:** Minimum of **25–50 panelists** for consumer tests, **8–12** for trained panels, and **20–30** for laboratory panels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 2 explicitly demands knowledge of **(a) Types of sensory panels** and **(b) Step-by-step methodology** — both are covered exhaustively in this section. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Sensory Panels (Types of Panel) Sensory panels are broadly classified into **three** major categories based on training level and objective. Understanding the *why* behind each type is the most frequently tested KTU concept. ### A. Consumer Panel (Affective Panel) - **Composition:** 25 to 150 untrained, naïve panelists drawn from the target market. - **Objective:** Measure *preference, liking, and acceptance* (hedonic response). - **Use Case:** New product launch, market positioning, advertising claim validation. - **Key Trait:** Panelists are **NOT screened for sensory acuity**; they represent the end-user population. - **Sub-Types:** - **Central Location Test (CLT):** Conducted in malls, supermarkets, or community halls. - **Home-Use Test (HUT):** Product is sent to consumer's home for a defined trial period. - **Focus Group:** 8–12 participants in a moderated discussion (qualitative, not statistical). ### B. Trained (Expert) Panel - **Composition:** 8 to 12 highly trained assessors who undergo **40–200+ hours** of supervised training. - **Objective:** Generate *quantitative descriptive data* on specific attributes (e.g., sweetness intensity on a 15-cm line scale). - **Use Case:** Quality control, shelf-life studies, competitive benchmarking, flavor profile mapping. - **Key Trait:** Panelists are **screened, selected, trained, and continuously monitored** for performance. - **Sub-Types:** - **Descriptive Trained Panel:** Used in Flavor Profile Method, Texture Profile Method, QDA (Quantitative Descriptive Analysis), and Spectrum Method. - **Discrimination Trained Panel:** Used in Triangle, Duo-Trio, 2-AFC, 3-AFC tests. ### C. Semi-Trained (Laboratory) Panel - **Composition:** 10 to 25 assessors with **basic orientation training** (10–30 hours). - **Objective:** Conduct routine difference/quality control tests with moderate statistical power. - **Use Case:** Factory floor quality checks, supplier verification, batch-to-batch consistency. - **Key Trait:** Sits *between* the consumer and the trained panel in terms of cost, time, and data precision. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Affected Panel vs. Affective Panel — Do Not Confuse:** > - *Affective* = expressing liking/preference (Emotional response). > - *Affected* = influenced by external bias (e.g., information, packaging, branding). > KTU examiners often use this distinction as a 3-mark trick question. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Methodology for Sensory Evaluation The KTU 2024 syllabus requires a **linear, sequential methodology**. The complete workflow is as follows: ### Step 1: Define the Objective - What is the question? (e.g., "Is reformulated Product B sweeter than Product A?") - This dictates the **panel type**, **test method**, and **statistical analysis**. ### Step 2: Select the Panel - Recruit candidates matching the demographic target. - Screen using **ISO 8586** standard tests: triangle tests for basic tastes, odor recognition, color vision, texture discrimination. ### Step 3: Train the Panel (if required) - Calibrate panelists against **reference standards** (e.g., sucrose solutions for sweetness at 2%, 5%, 10%, 15%). - Conduct orientation sessions to align terminology. ### Step 4: Standardize the Environment - **Lighting:** Standardized, often red light to mask color differences. - **Temperature:** 20–22 °C for room, 4–10 °C for refrigerated samples. - **Booths:** Separate booths to prevent communication. - **Noise:** Controlled below **45 dB**. ### Step 5: Prepare Samples - **Coding:** Three-digit random codes (e.g., 487, 219, 653) — never sequential or semantic. - **Serving Order:** Randomized using a **Latin Square** design to counterbalance order and position bias. - **Volume:** Standardized (typically 15–30 mL for liquid, 5–10 g for solid). - **Temperature:** Held constant using water baths or insulated containers. - **Palate Cleansers:** Distilled water (room temperature), unsalted crackers, or plain biscuits between samples. ### Step 6: Conduct the Test - **Discrimination Tests** for "Is there a difference?" — Triangle, Duo-Trio, 2-AFC, 3-AFC, A-Not-A, Ranking. - **Descriptive Tests** for "What is the difference?" — Flavor Profile, Texture Profile, QDA, Spectrum. - **Affective Tests** for "How much is it liked?" — 9-point Hedonic, Just-About-Right (JAR), Preference Ranking. ### Step 7: Statistical Analysis & Interpretation - Apply the correct test: - **Discrimination** → Chi-square, binomial distribution, or critical table values. - **Descriptive** → ANOVA, MANOVA, principal component analysis (PCA). - **Affective** → Friedman test, two-way ANOVA, penalty analysis. ### Step 8: Report & Validate - Document findings, replicate to confirm, and validate against instrumental data where possible (e.g., GC-MS for aroma compounds). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Critical Formatting Note:** All absolute values and ORs use `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol `|` to preserve markdown table integrity. | Test Type | Decision Rule Formula | Number of Panelists (n) | Significance Level ($\alpha$) | Critical p-value | |-----------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------------------|-----------------| | Triangle Test | $p = \frac{2}{3}$ (chance) | 18–36 minimum | 0.05 or 0.01 | Refer ISO 4120 table | | Duo-Trio Test | $p = \frac{1}{2}$ (chance) | 20–40 minimum | 0.05 or 0.01 | Refer ISO 10399 table | | Paired Comparison | $p = \frac{1}{2}$ (chance) | 30+ | 0.05 | Binomial table | | 2-AFC (Two-Alternative Forced Choice) | $p = \frac{1}{2}$ | 25–50 | 0.05 | Binomial table | | 3-AFC | $p = \frac{1}{3}$ | 20+ | 0.05 | Refer ISO 13399 table | | Ranking Test | $\chi^{2} = \frac{12}{nk(k+1)} \sum R_{j}^{2} - 3n(k+1)$ | 8–30 | 0.05 | $\chi^{2}$ table, df $\vert k-1 \vert$ | | Hedonic (9-pt) | Mean score $\bar{x}$ | 50–100 | 0.05 | t-test, df $\vert n-1 \vert$ | | ANOVA (2-factor) | $F = \frac{MS_{treatment}}{MS_{error}}$ | Variable | 0.05 | F-table, df $\vert (k-1, N-k) \vert$ | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Food Industry:** Reformulation of reduced-sodium soups, low-fat dairy. - **Pharmaceuticals:** Masking bitter APIs (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients). - **Pet Food:** Palatability testing for dogs and cats (using pet panels — a special KTU hot topic). - **Cosmetics:** Skin feel, fragrance intensity, aftertaste of lip products. - **Beverage Industry:** Mouthfeel of plant-based milk alternatives (an emerging research area). > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Insight:** When the question says "briefly explain the methodology," always use a **numbered list** of 6–8 steps as shown above. This structured answer consistently scores full marks in KTU valuations. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Three Panel Types This matrix is the **single most important** study tool for answering "Compare and contrast the types of sensory panels" — a guaranteed KTU Part B question. | Parameter | Consumer Panel | Semi-Trained Panel | Trained/Expert Panel | |-----------|----------------|-------------------|----------------------| | **Sample Size (n)** | 25–150 (often 100+) | 10–25 | 8–12 | | **Training Hours** | 0–2 hours (brief orientation only) | 10–30 hours | 40–200+ hours | | **Screening** | Demographic matching | ISO 8586 basic screening | ISO 8586 + advanced screening | | **Data Type** | Hedonic (ordinal/liking) | Difference (categorical) | Descriptive (interval/ratio) | | **Statistical Power** | Low (high variance) | Moderate | High (low variance) | | **Cost** | Low–Moderate | Moderate | High | | **Time to Deploy** | 1–2 weeks | 4–8 weeks | 3–6 months | | **Best Use** | Preference, acceptance | Quality control, supplier audit | Product development, mapping | | **Test Types** | Hedonic, JAR, Preference | Triangle, Duo-Trio, Ranking | QDA, Spectrum, Profile | | **Data Output** | Liking score, JAR score | Correct/Incorrect decision | Intensity score (0–15 scale) | | **Reproducibility** | Low | Moderate | High | | **Reference Standard** | None needed | Internal reference samples | External standards (e.g., sucrose 2–15%) | ## 3.2 Derivation: Why "n = 30" is the Standard Minimum for Discrimination Tests This is a **classic KTU numerical problem**. Let us derive the minimum number of panelists for a Triangle Test to achieve a meaningful result. ### Given Conditions - Test: Triangle Test (3 samples, 1 odd, assessors must pick the odd one) - Probability of correct guess by chance: $p_{0} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$ - Confidence level: $95\%$, significance level: $\alpha = 0.05$ - Expected proportion correct: $p_{d} = 0.55$ (minimum practically meaningful difference) ### Step 1: Set Up the Binomial Hypothesis The number of correct responses $X$ out of $n$ panelists follows a Binomial distribution: $$X \sim B(n, p_{0})$$ $$H_{0}: p = p_{0} = \frac{1}{3} \quad \text{(no difference)}$$ $$H_{1}: p > p_{0} = \frac{1}{3} \quad \text{(perceivable difference exists)}$$ ### Step 2: Apply the Normal Approximation For large $n$, the binomial distribution is approximated by the normal distribution with mean $\mu = n \cdot p_{0}$ and standard deviation $\sigma = \sqrt{n \cdot p_{0} \cdot (1 - p_{0})}$. $$\mu = n \cdot \frac{1}{3}$$ $$\sigma = \sqrt{n \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{2n}{9}}$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Required Sample Size The minimum number of correct responses $X_{crit}$ to reject $H_0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed, $z_{0.05} = 1.645$) satisfies: $$\frac{X_{crit} - \mu}{\sigma} \geq 1.645$$ Substituting for a typical $n = 30$: $$\mu = 30 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 10 \text{ correct responses}$$ $$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 30}{9}} = \sqrt{6.667} \approx 2.582$$ $$X_{crit} = \mu + 1.645 \cdot \sigma = 10 + 1.645 \cdot 2.582 \approx 10 + 4.25 = 14.25$$ ### Step 4: Round Up to Integer Since $X$ must be an integer, the **minimum number of correct responses** is **15** out of 30. ### Step 5: Cross-Check with ISO 4120 Table Looking up the ISO 4120 standard table for Triangle Test with $n = 30$ and $\alpha = 0.05$: - The minimum number of correct responses is **15** out of 30. - If observed correct = 15 or more, reject $H_0$ → conclude a significant difference exists. $$\text{Decision Rule: } X_{obs} \geq 15 \implies \text{Reject } H_{0} \text{ at } \alpha = 0.05$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** This is exactly why **30 panelists** is the universally accepted minimum for a Triangle Test. The derivation above proves it mathematically. ## 3.3 Numerical Example: Hedonic Scale Interpretation A 9-point Hedonic Scale is scored from 1 (Dislike Extremely) to 9 (Like Extremely). Suppose a consumer panel of 50 people tests a new mango juice and the mean score is $\bar{x} = 7.2$ with standard deviation $s = 1.4$. The competitor's product has a known mean $\mu_{0} = 6.8$. Test whether the new product is significantly preferred at $\alpha = 0.05$. ### Step 1: State Hypotheses $$H_0: \mu = 6.8 \quad \text{(no difference in liking)}$$ $$H_1: \mu > 6.8 \quad \text{(new product preferred)}$$ ### Step 2: Compute Standard Error $$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{1.4}{\sqrt{50}} \approx \frac{1.4}{7.071} \approx 0.198$$ ### Step 3: Compute t-Statistic $$t_{calc} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_{0}}{SE} = \frac{7.2 - 6.8}{0.198} \approx 2.02$$ ### Step 4: Compare with Critical Value At $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed), with $df = n - 1 = 49$: $$t_{crit} = 1.677 \quad \text{(from t-table)}$$ ### Step 5: Decision Since $t_{calc} = 2.02 > t_{crit} = 1.677$, we **reject $H_0$** and conclude the new mango juice is significantly preferred over the competitor. $$\boxed{\text{Conclusion: New product has statistically higher liking at } 95\% \text{ confidence}}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flowchart: Complete Sensory Evaluation Methodology This diagram maps the linear workflow from problem definition to final report, as expected in any KTU 14-mark answer. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Define Test Objective] --> B[Select Panel Type] B --> B1[Consumer Panel
25 to 150 members] B --> B2[Semi Trained Panel
10 to 25 members] B --> B3[Expert Trained Panel
8 to 12 members] B1 --> C[Screening by ISO 8586] B2 --> C B3 --> C C --> D[Training and Calibration
10 to 200 hours] D --> E[Standardize Test Environment
Booths, Light, Temperature] E --> F[Sample Preparation
Coding, Temperature, Volume] F --> G[Choose Test Method] G --> G1[Discrimination
Triangle, Duo-Trio, AFC] G --> G2[Descriptive
QDA, Spectrum, Profile] G --> G3[Affective
Hedonic, JAR, Preference] G1 --> H[Statistical Analysis] G2 --> H G3 --> H H --> I[Interpretation and Report] I --> J[Validation and Replication] J --> K[End: Decision Ready Data] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Panel Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[New Sensory Test Request] --> B{What is the
Test Question?} B -- Is there a difference --> C[Discrimination Test] B -- What is the difference --> D[Descriptive Test] B -- How much is it liked --> E[Affective Test] C --> F{Product Type} D --> F E --> F F -- Food Product --> G{Resource
Available?} F -- Beverage --> G F -- Cosmetic --> G G -- High Budget, 3 to 6 months --> H[Trained Expert Panel
n equals 8 to 12] G -- Medium Budget, 1 to 2 months --> I[Semi Trained Panel
n equals 10 to 25] G -- Low Budget, Quick Answer --> J[Consumer Panel
n equals 25 to 150] H --> K[Outcome: Precise Descriptive Data] I --> L[Outcome: Reliable Difference Data] J --> M[Outcome: Preference and Liking Data] ``` ## 4.3 Architecture: Sensory Booth Layout (ASTM/ISO Standard) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Panelist Entry] --> P2[Isolated Booth
1.2m x 1.5m] P2 --> P3[Red Light Option
Masks Color] P2 --> P4[White Light Option
Reveals Color] P2 --> P5[Sample Pass-Through
Window] P2 --> P6[Computerized Data
Entry System] P6 --> P7[Response Recording] P5 --> P8[Kitchen and
Sample Prep Area] P8 --> P5 P7 --> P9[Statistical Analysis
Software] ``` ## 4.4 Concept Map: The Three Pillars of Sensory Methodology ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Sensory Evaluation] --> P[Panel Selection] S --> M[Methodology] S --> E[Environment] P --> P1[Types: Consumer, Semi, Trained] P --> P2[Screening: ISO 8586] P --> P3[Training: 0 to 200 hours] M --> M1[Discrimination Tests] M --> M2[Descriptive Tests] M --> M3[Affective Tests] E --> E1[Booth Standardization] E --> E2[Sample Preparation] E --> E3[Reference Standards] P1 --> X[Result: Valid Sensory Data] P2 --> X P3 --> X M1 --> X M2 --> X M3 --> X E1 --> X E2 --> X E3 --> X ``` > [!TIP] > **Visual Learning Strategy:** The four diagrams above collectively form a complete answer sketch. If the KTU exam asks for a diagram, drawing the **Decision Tree (4.2)** + **Flowchart (4.1)** together guarantees full marks for the illustrative component. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### Question 1 **Q: Differentiate between a Trained Sensory Panel and a Consumer Sensory Panel. List any three points of difference.** **Model Answer:** | S.No | Trained Panel | Consumer Panel | |------|---------------|----------------| | 1 | Size: 8–12 panelists | Size: 25–150 panelists | | 2 | Training: 40–200+ hours | Training: 0–2 hours orientation | | 3 | Objective: Generate descriptive/intensity data | Objective: Measure liking/preference | | 4 | Screening: ISO 8586 advanced tests | Screening: Demographic matching only | | 5 | Statistical analysis: ANOVA, MANOVA, PCA | Statistical analysis: t-test, Friedman, Penalty | **Difference in 3 points (any 3 above):** (3 marks awarded for clear comparative statements) --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### Question 2 **Q: What is a Triangle Test? When is it used in sensory evaluation?** **Model Answer:** - A Triangle Test is a **discrimination test** in which the assessor is presented with **three coded samples**: two are identical and one is different. The assessor's task is to identify the odd sample. - **Probability of correct guess by chance:** $p = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$. - **Minimum panelists required:** 18–36 (typically 30). - **Used when:** Detecting small sensory differences between two products (e.g., reformulation, ingredient substitution, storage effect). - **Governed by:** ISO 4120 standard. (3 marks for definition + ISO reference + use case) --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the different **types of sensory panels** used in food product evaluation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **step-by-step methodology** for conducting a sensory evaluation study. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks **Introduction (1 mark):** Sensory panels are classified into three major types based on training level, screening rigor, and test objective. **1. Consumer Panel (2 marks):** - Definition: 25–150 untrained panelists from the target market. - Used for: Hedonic tests, preference ranking, JAR scales, market acceptance. - Sub-types: Central Location Test, Home-Use Test, Focus Group. - Data: Ordinal/interval liking scores. **2. Semi-Trained Panel (2 marks):** - Definition: 10–25 panelists with 10–30 hours of basic training. - Used for: Routine difference tests (Triangle, Duo-Trio), factory QC, supplier audits. - Data: Categorical (correct/incorrect) responses. **3. Trained (Expert) Panel (2 marks):** - Definition: 8–12 panelists with 40–200+ hours of advanced training. - Used for: Descriptive analysis, QDA, Spectrum, Flavor Profile, Texture Profile. - Data: Continuous intensity scores (e.g., 0–15 line scales). **Conclusion (0 marks – integrated above):** Each panel type is matched to a specific objective; selection of the wrong type invalidates the data. #### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks **Methodology – 8 Sequential Steps (1 mark for stating the sequence + 6 marks for explaining each in 1-2 lines):** 1. **Define the objective** — Specify the sensory question (difference, description, preference). 2. **Select the panel type** — Match panel to objective (consumer, semi-trained, trained). 3. **Screen and recruit panelists** — Apply ISO 8586: triangle tests, odor recognition, color vision. 4. **Train the panel** — Calibrate using reference standards (sucrose solutions, reference flavors). 5. **Standardize environment** — Control booth lighting, temperature (20–22°C), noise (<45 dB). 6. **Prepare samples** — Random 3-digit codes, balanced serving order (Latin Square), constant temperature, palate cleansers (water, plain crackers). 7. **Conduct the test** — Apply the appropriate method (discrimination/descriptive/affective). 8. **Statistical analysis & report** — Use chi-square, ANOVA, t-test as appropriate; replicate for validity. **Bonus (0.5 marks):** Mention ISO 8589 (booth standards) and ASTM references. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q: (a)** Discuss the **selection criteria and screening procedures** for recruiting sensory panelists. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **discrimination, descriptive, and affective** sensory tests with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks **Selection Criteria (4 marks — explain any 4 in detail):** 1. **Age:** Typically 18–60 years; older assessors may have declining acuity. 2. **Health:** No chronic illness, allergies, or conditions affecting senses (e.g., anosmia). 3. **Availability & Commitment:** Willing to attend all sessions, no frequent absences. 4. **Smoking & Alcohol:** Non-smokers preferred; abstain 30 minutes before test. 5. **Cultural & Dietary Background:** Should match the target population. 6. **Communication Skills:** Ability to articulate sensory perceptions clearly. 7. **No Bias:** No commercial interest in the product category. **Screening Procedures (3 marks — explain any 3):** 1. **Triangle Tests for Basic Tastes:** Identify sweet, sour, salty, bitter at threshold. 2. **Odor Recognition Test:** Identify common aromas (vanilla, lemon, clove, etc.). 3. **Color Vision Test:** Ishihara test for color blindness. 4. **Texture Discrimination Test:** Rank textures of standard gels. 5. **Paired Comparison Test:** Identify samples differing in specific attribute. 6. **Health & Lifestyle Questionnaire:** Eliminate candidates with conflicts of interest. **Reference (0 marks):** Mention ISO 8586 standard for general guidelines. #### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks | Test Type | Question Answered | Example | Sample Size | Statistical Test | |-----------|-------------------|---------|-------------|------------------| | Discrimination | "Is there a difference?" | Triangle, Duo-Trio, 2-AFC | 18–50 | Chi-square, Binomial | | Descriptive | "What is the difference?" | QDA, Flavor Profile, Spectrum | 8–12 | ANOVA, MANOVA, PCA | | Affective | "How much is it liked?" | 9-point Hedonic, JAR, Preference | 50–100 | t-test, Friedman, Penalty | **Discrimination Tests (2 marks):** Used to determine if a perceivable difference exists. Example: Triangle test on reformulated low-sodium soup. **Descriptive Tests (2 marks):** Used to quantify the magnitude and nature of differences. Example: QDA on artisan vs. industrial bread for crust crispness. **Affective Tests (2 marks):** Used to measure consumer liking and acceptance. Example: 9-point Hedonic test for new mango juice. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The three test families are complementary — discrimination detects, descriptive characterizes, affective evaluates commercial viability. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **DO NOT confuse "Affective" with "Affected"** — Affective = liking/emotion; Affected = biased by external cues. > 2. **DO NOT state minimum panel size as a single number** — Always give a *range* (e.g., "8 to 12 for trained panels") to demonstrate mastery. > 3. **DO NOT forget ISO/ASTM references** — Examiners award bonus marks for citing ISO 8586, ISO 4120, ISO 10399, and ASTM Manual 26. > 4. **DO NOT mix up the order of methodology steps** — The expected order is: Objective → Panel → Screen → Train → Environment → Samples → Test → Analysis. > 5. **DO NOT omit the "Why"** — Simply listing steps without explaining *why* each step is necessary limits the answer to 60% of the marks. Always link each step to its purpose (e.g., "Random 3-digit coding prevents sequential bias"). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:** ### Core Concepts (Panel Types) - ☐ **Consumer Panel:** 25–150 untrained, hedonic/preference data. - ☐ **Semi-Trained Panel:** 10–25 with basic training, discrimination data. - ☐ **Trained/Expert Panel:** 8–12 with extensive training, descriptive/intensity data. - ☐ **Affected vs. Affective:** Affective = liking; Affected = biased. ### Core Concepts (Methodology) - ☐ **8-Step Workflow:** Objective → Panel → Screen → Train → Environment → Samples → Test → Analysis. - ☐ **ISO 8586:** Panel selection and training. - ☐ **ISO 8589:** Booth design and environment. - ☐ **ISO 4120:** Triangle Test. - ☐ **ISO 10399:** Duo-Trio Test. - ☐ **ASTM Manual 26:** General sensory standards. ### Critical Numbers to Memorize - ☐ Triangle Test: $p_{0} = 1/3$, minimum $n = 30$, minimum correct = 15 at $\alpha = 0.05$. - ☐ Duo-Trio Test: $p_{0} = 1/2$, minimum $n = 20$. - ☐ 2-AFC: $p_{0} = 1/2$. - ☐ 3-AFC: $p_{0} = 1/3$. - ☐ Booth dimensions: $1.2 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m}$ minimum. - ☐ Testing room temperature: $20–22°C$, humidity $50–55\%$. - ☐ Sample serving volume: $15–30 \text{ mL}$ (liquid), $5–10 \text{ g}$ (solid). ### Common KTU Exam Traps - ☐ Random 3-digit codes (NOT sequential, NOT semantic). - ☐ Latin Square design for order/position counterbalancing. - ☐ Palate cleansers: distilled water, unsalted crackers. - ☐ Red light to mask color differences. - ☐ $\alpha = 0.05$ (5% significance level is the default). ### High-Weightage Topics (Most Likely in 14-Mark Questions) - ☐ Comparison of the three panel types (table format). - ☐ Step-by-step methodology (numbered list). - ☐ ISO standards and their corresponding test methods. - ☐ Numerical problem on sample size determination for Triangle Test. - ☐ Hedonic scale interpretation using t-test. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** When answering any 14-mark question on this topic, always use the **"Definition + Table + Diagram + Conclusion"** formula. This four-part structure aligns perfectly with the KTU 2024 marking scheme (3 + 4 + 4 + 3 distribution typical for descriptive answers). **End of Module 2 Notes — Selection of Panel: Types of Panel, Methodology for Sensory Evaluation**

topics.title: Considerations for Testing Sensory Evaluation: Testing Area and Setup, Lighting, Testing Schedule, Preparation of Samples, Cooling and Order of Presentation, Choosing of Panels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT524 - SENSORY EVALUATION OF FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Considerations for Testing Sensory Evaluation: Testing Area and Setup, Lighting, Testing Schedule, Preparation of Samples, Cooling and Order of Presentation, Choosing of Panels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensory Evaluation** is a scientific discipline used to evoke, measure, analyse and interpret human responses to food products as perceived through the five senses: sight, smell, taste, touch and hearing. However, the validity, reliability and reproducibility of sensory data are critically dependent on a strict set of **environmental, procedural and human (panelist) considerations** that govern how the test is conducted. These considerations collectively eliminate *psychological, physiological and physical biases*, ensuring that the data captured reflects the true perceptual response of the panelist rather than a reaction to extraneous variables.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the **Considerations for Testing Sensory Evaluation** refer to the standardized protocols governing the *testing area and setup, lighting, testing schedule, preparation of samples, cooling and order of presentation, and choosing of panels* — all of which are designed to maximize the signal-to-noise ratio in human response data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The conditions under which a sensory test is performed are as important as the test method itself. A flawed testing environment can introduce a **bias of up to 30–40%** in panelist responses, rendering the entire experiment statistically invalid. Hence, ASTM, ISO and BIS standards mandate strict environmental controls.

### 1.2 The Six Pillars of Sensory Testing Considerations

| # | Pillar | Purpose |
|---|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Testing Area & Setup** | Control physical/environmental distractors |
| 2 | **Lighting** | Mask colour differences, control visual bias |
| 3 | **Testing Schedule** | Prevent fatigue, circadian variation |
| 4 | **Sample Preparation** | Standardize volume, temperature, form |
| 5 | **Cooling & Order of Presentation** | Counterbalance carry-over and order effects |
| 6 | **Panel Selection** | Ensure panelist sensitivity, availability, training |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Concert Hall Principle"

Imagine attending a symphony concert in a noisy marketplace. Even if the orchestra is world-class (the *product* is excellent), the audience (*the panelist*) will rate the performance poorly because the *environment* is distracting. Conversely, a well-designed acoustic hall (the *testing area*) allows every note to be heard clearly, so the audience can give a true verdict on the orchestra's skill.

**Sensory evaluation works on the same principle:** the *product* is the orchestra, the *panelist* is the audience, and the *testing environment* is the concert hall. A well-controlled "hall" lets the panelist's perception reflect the true quality of the product — nothing more, nothing less.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Primer (for KTU 2024):**
> - **Bias:** Any factor that systematically distorts sensory response away from the true attribute.
> - **Carry-over effect:** Residual perception of a previous sample influencing the next.
> - **Order effect:** Position of a sample in a sequence influencing its score.
> - **Panelist Fatigue:** Decline in discrimination ability after prolonged testing.
> - **Standard Reference:** A control sample used as an anchor for comparison.
> - **Blinding:** Concealing sample identity from the panelist.

### 1.4 Why These Considerations Matter — Engineering Perspective

From a **process-engineering viewpoint**, sensory evaluation is essentially a *measurement system* where the *human instrument* (panelist) replaces an electronic sensor. Just as a spectrometer requires a clean, dark, temperature-stable chamber, the sensory panel requires a quiet, odour-free, temperature-controlled booth. Without these controls, the *signal* (true product attribute) is overwhelmed by *noise* (environmental distractors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standards Governing Sensory Testing Environments:**
> - **ISO 8589:2007** — Design of test rooms for sensory analysis.
> - **ASTM E1871-17** — User requirements for sensory testing facilities.
> - **BIS IS 6273 (Part 1 & 2)** — Indian standard for sensory evaluation of food.
> - **IRRI / ICAR Sensory Lab Guidelines** — for food research institutes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Testing Area and Setup

#### 2.1.1 The Sensory Laboratory Architecture

A standard sensory laboratory comprises three physically separated zones:

1. **Preparation Area (Kitchen/Prep Room)** — Where samples are coded, portioned, and tempered to the required temperature. Equipped with hot-plates, ovens, refrigerators, water-baths, blenders and coded sample containers.
2. **Testing Area (Booths)** — Where panelists sit in isolated compartments and evaluate samples.
3. **Discussion/Conference Room** — Where trained panels deliberate and discuss results.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why separation matters:** If sample preparation occurs in the same room as testing, odours and visual cues from uncooked or differently-prepared foods will *contaminate* the panelist's perception.

#### 2.1.2 Testing Booth Specifications

Each booth must satisfy the following (per **ISO 8589**):

- **Booth dimensions:** Minimum 0.9 m (width) × 0.6 m (depth) per panelist. A standard bank of 6–10 booths is 3 m long.
- **Partition height:** At least **1.2 m** above the work surface to prevent visual interaction between panelists.
- **Work surface colour:** Neutral, matte **grey, white or beige** (no reflective gloss) to prevent colour bias.
- **Countertop:** Heat-resistant, easy-to-sanitize, non-absorbent.
- **Pass-through window:** A sliding/hatch window (approx. 35 cm × 35 cm) through which coded samples are passed by the server without panelist-server interaction.
- **Communication system:** An intercom/computer-linked system for panelists to record responses privately.
- **Sink and expectoration cup:** Each booth should have a small sink or disposable cup for expectoration of samples (especially for difference/discrimination tests).

#### 2.1.3 Environmental Controls of the Testing Area

| Parameter | Recommended Value | Tolerance |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------|
| Temperature | 20–22 °C | ± 2 °C |
| Relative Humidity | 50–55% | ± 5% |
| Air changes/hour | 10–15 | — |
| Background noise | < 45 dB | — |
| Vibration | Negligible | — |
| Odour concentration | Non-detectable | — |
| Air velocity | < 0.25 m/s | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Activated carbon + HEPA filters** are installed in the HVAC system of premium sensory labs (e.g., CSIRO, SGF International) to scrub volatile organic compounds (VOCs) that may otherwise be detected above the panelist's odour threshold (often as low as **10⁻⁹ M** for certain aroma molecules).

---

### 2.2 Lighting

#### 2.2.1 The Physics of Light in Sensory Booths

Lighting in sensory booths is not just about visibility — it is a critical tool for *controlling visual bias*. Different light sources have different **Colour Rendering Indices (CRI)** and **Correlated Colour Temperatures (CCT)**, which can dramatically alter how food appears.

**Key Metrics:**

- **Colour Rendering Index (CRI, Ra):** A 0–100 scale measuring how accurately a light source renders colours compared to a reference (sunlight = 100). For sensory work, **CRI ≥ 90** is mandatory for colour-evaluation tests.
- **Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT, K):** Warmth/coolness of light. Daylight ≈ 6500 K; tungsten ≈ 2800 K.
- **Lux (illuminance):** 1 lumen/m². Sensory booths typically operate at **750–1000 lux**.

#### 2.2.2 Types of Lighting Used in Sensory Labs

| Lighting Type | CCT (K) | CRI (Ra) | Use in Sensory Work |
|---------------|---------|----------|---------------------|
| **Daylight (D65)** | 6500 | 95+ | Reference standard for colour evaluation |
| **Cool white fluorescent (CWF)** | 4150 | 62 | Simulates supermarket lighting |
| **Warm white fluorescent (WWF)** | 3000 | 51 | Simulates home/kitchen lighting |
| **Incandescent (A)** | 2856 | 100 | Home lighting simulation |
| **Filtered/red light** | Variable | N/A | To mask colour differences |
| **Sodium vapour** | ~2000 | -40 | Nearly monochromatic yellow; extreme masking |

#### 2.2.3 The Logic Behind Lighting Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Sensory Lighting:**
> *Choose the light source that either (a) faithfully reveals colour, OR (b) deliberately masks colour — but never use a light source that does both inconsistently.*

**Decision logic:**

- **Difference tests (triangle, duo-trio, paired):** Use **red, green, or yellow filtered light** to mask colour, so the panelist *cannot* use visual cues and must rely on smell/taste/texture.
- **Descriptive/quantitative tests (QDA, profile):** Use **D65 daylight booths (CRI > 90)** to evaluate colour as a separate attribute.
- **Consumer tests (hedonic, preference):** Use **fluorescent lighting simulating retail conditions** (CWF, ~4150 K) to mimic the real consumer environment.

#### 2.2.4 The Macbeth Viewing Booth

A standard piece of equipment in any sensory lab is the **Macbeth Judge II Viewing Booth**, which contains five standardized light sources (A, CWF, WWF, D65, Horizon) switchable from a single panel. It allows rapid comparison of how a product appears under home, retail, and reference lighting.

---

### 2.3 Testing Schedule

#### 2.3.1 Why Schedule Matters

The human sensory apparatus exhibits strong **circadian variation**. Studies have shown that:

- **Taste sensitivity** peaks between **10:00–12:00 hrs** and **16:00–18:00 hrs**.
- **Olfactory sensitivity** is highest mid-morning.
- **Pancreatic enzyme / saliva flow** is reduced during early morning and post-prandial periods.

Hence, scheduling tests at the *wrong time* can produce systematically biased data.

#### 2.3.2 KTU 2024 Standard Scheduling Principles

1. **Time of day:** Conduct tests **mid-morning (10:00–11:00)** or **mid-afternoon (15:00–16:00)**, at least **1.5–2 hours after a meal**.
2. **Frequency:** Trained panels should not be tested **more than 2–3 times per week** on intensive tests (QDA). Consumer panels may run more frequently.
3. **Session length:** Each session should not exceed **45–60 minutes** to prevent fatigue and adaptation.
4. **Number of samples per session:** Maximum **6–8 samples** for trained panels, **4–6 samples** for consumer panels.
5. **Time between samples:** Allow **60–90 seconds** for palate cleansing and short rest, plus **5–10 minutes** after every 3 samples.

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptation Phenomenon:** When the tongue is exposed to a single taste (e.g., sweetness) for > 30 seconds, the receptors desensitize. This is why prolonged testing produces *diminishing responses*.

#### 2.3.3 Sample Sequence Planning

Within a session, samples are presented in a **balanced, randomized order** so that any bias (such as first-sample preference or last-sample fatigue) is distributed equally across all products.

**Williams Latin Square** designs are commonly used to balance for *first-order carry-over effects* across a set of 3–6 samples.

---

### 2.4 Preparation of Samples

#### 2.4.1 Critical Variables in Sample Preparation

| Variable | Standard Practice |
|----------|-------------------|
| **Sample volume / weight** | Identical for all samples (e.g., 30 mL liquid; 25 g solid) |
| **Sample temperature** | Controlled and identical (e.g., 22 ± 1 °C; 60 ± 1 °C) |
| **Sample container** | Identical, opaque, coded (e.g., 3-digit random codes) |
| **Sample coding** | 3-digit random numbers; no suggestive codes (no "123", "999") |
| **Sample state** | Uniform texture, cut size, viscosity |
| **Sample freshness** | Prepared within 2 hours of testing |
| **Water/rinse** | Room-temp, deionized water for palate cleansing |
| **Carrier medium** | Identical if applicable (e.g., sauce volume, bread slice) |

#### 2.4.2 Coding Systems

Codes are generated from a **random number table** or computer-generated random sequences. Examples of valid vs invalid codes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid these coding errors:**
> - ❌ "1", "2", "3" — implies order.
> - ❌ "100", "200", "300" — implies order/quantity.
> - ❌ "AAA", "BBB" — alphabetic codes are easier to remember.
> - ✅ "381", "745", "296" — random, no order bias.
> - ✅ Use **balanced 3-digit codes** from a published table (e.g., Amerine & Roessler).

#### 2.4.3 The Plate/Bowl Layout

Samples are placed on **white or neutral-coloured trays** with a **paper or water biscuit (cracker)** between samples as a palate cleanser. Numbered positions (1, 2, 3…) are used in trays only if all samples are presented simultaneously (e.g., triangle test); otherwise samples are passed sequentially.

---

### 2.5 Cooling and Order of Presentation

#### 2.5.1 Why Order Matters

In sequential presentation, the **position of a sample in a sequence** can affect its perceived intensity due to:

- **Contrast effect:** A mediocre sample following an excellent one appears worse.
- **Assimilation effect:** A mediocre sample following a poor one appears better.
- **Carry-over effect:** Residual taste of sample N affects perception of sample N+1.
- **First-sample and last-sample bias:** Novelty and fatigue effects.

#### 2.5.2 Counterbalancing Strategies

| Strategy | Description | Use Case |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Complete randomization** | Each panelist gets a fully random order | Consumer tests, large panels |
| **Latin Square** | Each product appears once in each position across panelists | Trained panels, 3–6 samples |
| **Williams Latin Square** | Two squares, balanced for first-order carry-over | Trained panels, ≤ 6 samples |
| **Block randomization** | Samples randomized within blocks of 3 | Difference tests |
| **Reference first** | Standard served first, then samples blind | Some descriptive tests |

#### 2.5.3 The Williams Square (n = 3, 4, 5, 6 samples)

For *n* = 3, the Williams design yields **6 unique orderings** (one for each combination of position rotation + direction reversal). Each sample appears equally often in each position, and the number of times it immediately follows each other sample is constant.

**Example for 3 samples (A, B, C):**

| Panelist | Order |
|----------|-------|
| 1 | A B C |
| 2 | B C A |
| 3 | C A B |
| 4 | C B A |
| 5 | B A C |
| 6 | A C B |

#### 2.5.4 Temperature Control During Presentation

- **Hot foods (e.g., soup, coffee):** Held at 60 ± 2 °C in a water bath; served in pre-warmed containers. Tested within **5 minutes** of removal from bath.
- **Cold foods (e.g., ice cream, beverages):** Held at 5 ± 1 °C; served in pre-chilled containers.
- **Ambient foods:** 22 ± 1 °C.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** A 5 °C drift in serving temperature can shift sweetness perception by **8–12%** and aroma intensity by **15–20%**. Students must mention explicit temperature control in their KTU answers.

---

### 2.6 Choosing of Panels

#### 2.6.1 Panel Classification (ISO/DIS 5492)

| Panel Type | Members | Screening | Training | Statistical Power |
|------------|---------|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Highly Trained Panel** | 8–12 (minimum 6) | Extensive (sensitivity, acuity, consistency) | 100+ hours | High (small differences) |
| **Trained Panel** | 10–15 | Moderate | 30–50 hours | Medium |
| **Semi-trained Panel** | 15–25 | Basic | 5–10 hours | Medium-low |
| **Consumer Panel** | 75–150+ (target consumer) | Demographic screening | None | Low (preference/acceptance) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Statistical Rule of Thumb:**
> - Trained panels can detect differences of **5–10%**.
> - Consumer panels typically require differences of **20–30%** to reach significance.

#### 2.6.2 Panelist Selection Criteria (Screening Tests)

A panelist must pass a battery of **screening tests** before being inducted:

1. **Visual acuity:** Snellen chart; ability to detect colour (Ishihara test for colour-blindness).
2. **Olfactory acuity:** Identification of common odours (e.g., clove, lemon, vanilla, coffee, mint, eucalyptus).
3. **Gustatory acuity:** Threshold tests for the four basic tastes — sweet (sucrose), sour (citric acid), salty (NaCl), bitter (caffeine or quinine).
4. **Texture/tactile sensitivity:** Identification of viscosity grades, particle sizes.
5. **Health & habits:** No allergies, no smoking (or abstaining on test day), no medication affecting taste, no pregnancy affecting smell/taste.
6. **Availability & commitment:** Willing to attend scheduled sessions.
7. **Personality & communication:** Ability to articulate perceptions; absence of strong biases.

#### 2.6.3 Panelist Demographics

- **Age:** 18–55 years typically (taste/smell declines after 60).
- **Gender:** Both, balanced as relevant to product.
- **Education:** Adequate to comprehend test protocols and rating scales.
- **Cultural background:** Matched to the consumer target.

#### 2.6.4 Training the Panel

Training involves:

- **Sensory vocabulary development** (e.g., lexicon of 30+ descriptors for coffee).
- **Reference standards** for each descriptor (e.g., 0.5% citric acid = "sour reference").
- **Calibration sessions** — repeated evaluation of the same product to establish inter-panelist consistency.
- **Statistical monitoring** of individual panelist performance (using ANOVA, PCA, or panel performance plots).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked about panel selection, always mention at least *3 of*: acuity screening, demographic matching, training hours, ethical considerations (informed consent, withdrawal rights), and motivation/incentive.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although sensory evaluation is more procedural than computational, a few statistical and physical relations frequently appear in KTU numerical/analytical questions.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Unit | Use |
|---------|-------------------|------|-----|
| **Loudness perception vs sound intensity** | $L = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | dB | Setting booth background noise level |
| **Odour Threshold (OT)** | $OT = \dfrac{\text{minimum detectable concentration}}{\text{concentration in air}}$ | dimensionless (often as ppm) | Cleaning air in booths |
| **Beidler's Law (taste response)** | $R = R_{max}\dfrac{C}{K_d + C}$ | — | Taste intensity vs concentration |
| **Sweetness scaling (Weber-Fechner)** | $P = k \cdot \log S$ | — | Perceived intensity vs stimulus |
| **Stevens' Power Law** | $P = k \cdot S^n$ | — | Perceived intensity for most senses |
| **Response consistency (Panel CV)** | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100$ | % | Panelist performance check |
| **Minimum panel size for triangle test** | $n \geq \dfrac{1.5 \cdot z_{\alpha}^{2}}{p(1-p)}$ | — | Power calculation |
| **Hedonic scale mean separation** | $LSD = t_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot MSE}{r}}$ | scale units | Post-hoc comparison |
| **Light intensity (lux)** | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | lx | Booth lighting check |
| **Colour Rendering Index (CRI)** | $R_a = 100 - \dfrac{4.6}{n}\sum \Delta E_i$ | 0–100 | Verifying sensory light source |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Tolerance Limits:**
> - **Booth temperature:** 20–22 °C ± 2 °C
> - **Serving temperature (hot):** 60 ± 2 °C
> - **Serving temperature (cold):** 5 ± 1 °C
> - **Air changes:** 10–15 ACH
> - **Lighting (colour work):** 750–1000 lx, CRI ≥ 90, D65
> - **Lighting (masking):** Red filter, transmission max at 700 nm
> - **Session length:** ≤ 60 min
> - **Inter-sample interval:** 60–90 s + 5 min every 3 samples
> - **Palate cleanser:** Deionized water (22 ± 1 °C) + unsalted cracker
> - **Panel size (trained):** 8–12 (min 6)
> - **Panel size (consumer):** ≥ 75 for two-sample test, ≥ 100 for hedonic
> - **CV threshold for panelist consistency:** < 25% (acceptable)

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Beidler's Law for Taste Response

The most fundamental equation in sensory science, **Beidler's Law**, relates the perceived taste response to the concentration of a tastant. Let us derive it rigorously.

#### Step 1: Define the assumption — Single-site reversible binding

Taste molecules (tastants) bind reversibly to receptor sites on the tongue's taste buds. The binding follows the **Law of Mass Action**.

$$T + R \;\underset{k_d}{\overset{k_a}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; TR$$

where:
- $T$ = tastant molecule concentration
- $R$ = free receptor concentration
- $TR$ = tastant-receptor complex (proportional to perceived response)
- $k_a$ = association rate constant
- $k_d$ = dissociation rate constant

#### Step 2: Apply the mass-action equilibrium

At equilibrium, the dissociation constant is:

$$K_d = \dfrac{[T][R]}{[TR]}$$

#### Step 3: Apply the conservation of receptors

The total receptor concentration $R_{tot}$ is the sum of free and bound:

$$R_{tot} = R + TR$$

Substituting $R = R_{tot} - TR$ into the equilibrium expression:

$$K_d = \dfrac{[T]\, (R_{tot} - TR)}{[TR]}$$

#### Step 4: Solve for the bound concentration $[TR]$

$$K_d \cdot [TR] = [T] \cdot R_{tot} - [T] \cdot [TR]$$

$$[TR] \cdot (K_d + T) = R_{tot} \cdot T$$

$$[TR] = \dfrac{R_{tot} \cdot T}{K_d + T}$$

#### Step 5: Identify the perceived response

The neural response $R$ is proportional to $[TR]$. Letting $R_{max} = k \cdot R_{tot}$:

$$\boxed{R = R_{max}\dfrac{C}{K_d + C}}$$

where $C$ is the tastant concentration.

#### Step 6: Verify limiting cases

- **Low concentration** ($C \ll K_d$): $R \approx \dfrac{R_{max}}{K_d} \cdot C$ (linear regime)
- **High concentration** ($C \gg K_d$): $R \to R_{max}$ (saturation)

**Engineering interpretation:** A trained panelist's response saturates at high stimulus — this is why extremely sweet/bitter foods must be diluted before discrimination testing, or the panelist's perceptual resolution collapses.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Weber-Fechner Law (for Sensory Intensity)

#### Step 1: Define Weber's ratio

The **just-noticeable difference (JND)** $\Delta I$ is proportional to the baseline stimulus $I$:

$$\Delta I = k \cdot I$$

#### Step 2: Form the differential equation

For an infinitesimal change $dI$ producing a JND $dP$ in perception:

$$dP = \dfrac{dI}{k \cdot I}$$

#### Step 3: Integrate from a reference stimulus $I_0$ to $I$

$$P = \int_{I_0}^{I} \dfrac{dI'}{k \cdot I'} = \dfrac{1}{k} \ln \left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

#### Step 4: Convert to base-10 logarithm (commonly used in sensory science)

$$P = 2.303 \cdot \dfrac{1}{k} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

$$\boxed{P = K \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)}$$

#### Step 5: Apply to booth noise control

For the sensory lab, if the **ambient noise** must be below the panelist's detection threshold, then the *minimum audible noise* $I_{min}$ relative to the test stimulus (e.g., the crunch of a chip) $I_{signal}$ must satisfy:

$$I_{noise} < I_{min} = I_{signal} \cdot 10^{-P_{thr}/K}$$

This is why **ISO 8589 mandates < 45 dB** background — a louder crunchy stimulus at ~65 dB has a Weber ratio of ~0.1, so a 45 dB ambient is just at the differential threshold.

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Panel Size for a Triangle Test (Power Analysis)

#### Step 1: Define hypotheses

- $H_0$: $p = 1/3$ (panelist guesses; correct by chance in triangle test)
- $H_1$: $p > 1/3$ (panelist truly detects a difference)

#### Step 2: Use the normal approximation to the binomial

For a triangle test with $n$ panelists, the number of correct responses $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$. Under $H_0$, the expected number correct is:

$$E[X \vert H_0] = n \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{n}{3}$$

with standard deviation:

$$\sigma_0 = \sqrt{n \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{2}{3}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2n}{9}}$$

#### Step 3: Set up the test statistic

For a one-sided test at significance $\alpha = 0.05$ (so $z_{0.05} = 1.645$):

$$z = \dfrac{X - n/3}{\sqrt{2n/9}}$$

We reject $H_0$ if $z \ge 1.645$.

#### Step 4: Solve for $n$ given a target effect size $(p_1 - 1/3)$

For power $1 - \beta = 0.80$ ($z_{0.20} = 0.842$), the required $n$ is:

$$n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha}\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)}}{p_1 - p_0}\right)^2$$

with $p_0 = 1/3$ and $p_1$ as the true probability under $H_1$.

**Worked Example:** If we want to detect a true proportion $p_1 = 0.55$ (a moderate effect), $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-sided), power = 0.80:

$$n = \left(\dfrac{1.645 \cdot \sqrt{(1/3)(2/3)} + 0.842 \cdot \sqrt{(0.55)(0.45)}}{0.55 - 0.333}\right)^2$$

Step 4a: Calculate $\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)}$:
$$\sqrt{(0.333)(0.667)} = \sqrt{0.222} = 0.4714$$

Step 4b: Calculate $\sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)}$:
$$\sqrt{(0.55)(0.45)} = \sqrt{0.2475} = 0.4975$$

Step 4c: Numerator:
$$1.645 \cdot 0.4714 + 0.842 \cdot 0.4975 = 0.7755 + 0.4189 = 1.1944$$

Step 4d: Denominator:
$$0.55 - 0.3333 = 0.2167$$

Step 4e: Final ratio and square:
$$n = \left(\dfrac{1.1944}{0.2167}\right)^2 = (5.51)^2 = 30.4 \approx 31$$

$$\boxed{n \ge 31 \text{ panelists for a triangle test with } p_1 = 0.55 \text{ at } \alpha = 0.05, \text{ power} = 0.80}$$

This is a **14-mark KTU-style analytical question** in essence — students may be asked to compute panel size for a given scenario.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Test Condition

| Scenario | Lighting | Sample Temp | Panel Type | Sample Volume | Schedule |
|----------|----------|-------------|------------|---------------|----------|
| Triangle test (colour of juice) | **Red filter** | 10 ± 1 °C | Trained (n ≥ 31) | 30 mL | 10:00 AM, single session |
| Triangle test (flavour of milk) | Daylight D65 | 15 ± 1 °C | Trained | 25 mL | 11:00 AM |
| QDA on coffee | D65 booth | 65 ± 2 °C (served hot) | Highly trained (n = 8–12) | 50 mL | 10:00 AM, 3 sessions/week |
| Hedonic test on biscuit | Retail fluorescent (CWF) | 22 ± 1 °C | Consumer (n = 100) | 1 biscuit (15 g) | 15:00–17:00, single session |
| Difference test on aroma | **Red light + nose clip removed** | 22 ± 1 °C | Trained | 10 mL in dark glass | 10:30 AM |
| Texture profile (TA) | Daylight | 22 ± 1 °C | Highly trained | 25 g cubes | 11:00 AM, single session |
