```mermaid
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Container and Coding]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Serving Temperature]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Lighting and Booth]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Time of Day and Schedule]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 Panelist State]
    L7 --> L8[Final Layer Perceived Response]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each layer introduces its own potential bias. A trained sensory analyst controls all eight layers. Failing to control even one (e.g., temperature drift) can corrupt the final response.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the recommended range of temperature and relative humidity in a sensory testing booth as per ISO 8589?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

As per **ISO 8589:2007**, a sensory testing booth must maintain:

- **Temperature:** 20–22 °C (tolerance ± 2 °C)
- **Relative Humidity:** 50–55% (tolerance ± 5%)

The temperature is controlled to prevent sweating or shivering (which can shift tactile and taste responses), and the humidity is controlled because very dry air (< 40% RH) desiccates the oral mucosa, reducing taste sensitivity, while very humid air (> 70% RH) promotes microbial growth and is uncomfortable for the panelist. The values are bold-highlighted above for the 1 mark of the temperature range, 1 mark for humidity, and 1 mark for the rationale.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a trained panel and a consumer panel in sensory evaluation. State the typical size of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Trained Panel | Consumer Panel |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Purpose | Detect and quantify specific differences | Measure preference/acceptance |
| Screening | Yes — acuity, sensitivity, consistency | Demographic screening only |
| Training | 30–100+ hours | None |
| Size | 8–12 (min 6) | 75–150+ |
| Statistical power | High (small differences) | Low (large differences) |
| Test types | Triangle, duo-trio, QDA, profile | Hedonic, preference, acceptance |

A trained panel answers *"What is the difference?"* whereas a consumer panel answers *"Do I like it?"* — fundamentally different research questions. (1 mark for each column + 1 mark for the conceptual difference.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the design and layout of a sensory testing laboratory as per **ISO 8589**. Include the booth specifications, environmental controls and the rationale for each design element.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the role of lighting in sensory evaluation. Explain the different types of light sources used, with specific reference to the **Macbeth Judge II Viewing Booth** and the **Colour Rendering Index (CRI)**.

---

#### Model Answer (a) — Booth Design as per ISO 8589 [7 marks]

A sensory testing laboratory, as standardized by **ISO 8589:2007**, is divided into three physically separated zones:

1. **Preparation Area (Prep Kitchen)** — Where samples are coded, portioned, tempered, and held until presentation. Equipped with hot plates, ovens, refrigerators, water baths, blenders, and opaque coded containers. (1 mark)

2. **Testing Area (Booths)** — The heart of the lab. Houses individual evaluation booths where panelists sit in isolation. (0.5 mark)

3. **Discussion Room** — Where trained panels deliberate and discuss descriptive data. (0.5 mark)

**Booth Specifications (3 marks):**

- **Dimensions:** Minimum 0.9 m wide × 0.6 m deep per panelist.
- **Partition height:** At least 1.2 m above the work surface to prevent visual communication between panelists.
- **Work surface:** Matte neutral colour (grey/white/beige) to prevent colour reflection.
- **Pass-through window:** 35 × 35 cm hatch for coded sample delivery without panelist-server interaction.
- **Sink or expectoration cup:** Per booth for rinse/spit protocols.
- **Data entry system:** Computer or paper ballot — must be private (no influence from neighbours).

**Environmental Controls (1 mark):**

- **Temperature:** 20–22 °C ± 2 °C (ISO 8589)
- **Relative humidity:** 50–55%
- **Air changes:** 10–15 ACH with HEPA + activated carbon filtration
- **Background noise:** < 45 dB
- **Vibration:** Negligible
- **Odor:** Non-detectable

**Rationale:** Each control eliminates a specific bias — temperature affects comfort and taste; humidity affects mucosa; airflow removes odours; lighting controls visual bias; partitions eliminate social influence. Failing any control compromises the validity of the entire data set. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Answer (b) — Lighting in Sensory Evaluation [7 marks]

**Why lighting matters (1 mark):**
The visual appearance of a food product is its first contact with the panelist, and a wrong light source can mask, exaggerate, or distort colour. Since colour often correlates with flavour expectation (e.g., a darker roast coffee is expected to taste more bitter), uncontrolled lighting produces a **visual bias** in the response.

**Types of Light Sources (3 marks):**

| Source | CCT (K) | CRI (Ra) | Sensory Use |
|--------|---------|----------|-------------|
| D65 Daylight | 6500 | 95+ | Reference; colour evaluation |
| Cool White Fluor. (CWF) | 4150 | 62 | Retail/supermarket simulation |
| Warm White Fluor. (WWF) | 3000 | 51 | Home/kitchen simulation |
| Incandescent (A) | 2856 | 100 | Home dining simulation |
| Sodium vapour | ~2000 | -40 | Monochromatic masking |
| Red filter | N/A | N/A | Colour masking for difference tests |

**CRI is a 0–100 scale** measuring how faithfully a source renders colours vs reference daylight. For colour work, **CRI ≥ 90** is mandatory. (1 mark)

**Macbeth Judge II Viewing Booth (2 marks):**
A standardized light cabinet containing 5 switchable sources — D65, A, CWF, WWF, and Horizon (H). Allows the panelist or QC analyst to view a product under retail, home, and reference lighting within seconds. Used for **QC colour checks, shelf-life studies, and packaging colour matching** in the food industry.

**Selection rule of thumb (1 mark):**
- **Difference tests** → Red or green filter to mask colour
- **Descriptive tests** → D65 with CRI ≥ 90
- **Consumer/preference tests** → CWF (4150 K) to simulate the retail environment

---

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the factors involved in **choosing a sensory panel**. Describe the screening tests used to select panelist candidates, and explain the difference between a **trained panel and a consumer panel** with examples of tests suited to each.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the importance of **sample preparation, cooling, and order of presentation** in sensory evaluation. Discuss the use of **randomization, Latin Square designs, and the Williams Square** in counterbalancing biases.

---

#### Model Answer (a) — Panel Selection [7 marks]

**Why panel selection matters (1 mark):**
The panelist is the *measurement instrument* in sensory evaluation. Just as a faulty thermometer gives wrong readings, an insensitive, biased, or untrained panelist produces invalid data. Hence, selection is the most critical human-side consideration.

**Screening Tests (3 marks):**

1. **Visual acuity:** Snellen chart (≥ 6/6); Ishihara test for colour blindness (deuteranopia, protanopia disqualify the candidate for colour tests).
2. **Olfactory acuity:** Identification of 6–10 common odours (e.g., clove, lemon, vanilla, coffee, mint, eucalyptus, rose, cinnamon, chocolate, anise).
3. **Gustatory acuity:** Threshold tests for the four basic tastes using standardized solutions:
   - Sweet — 0.5–2.0% sucrose
   - Sour — 0.05–0.2% citric acid
   - Salty — 0.1–0.5% NaCl
   - Bitter — 0.01–0.05% caffeine or quinine
4. **Texture sensitivity:** Identification of viscosity grades, particle sizes (e.g., ranking of 5 different grit sizes).
5. **Health, habits & demographics:** No smoking on test day, no recent illness, no medication affecting taste/smell, age 18–55, demographic match to target consumer, regular availability, ability to articulate perceptions.
6. **Personality & commitment:** No strong a-priori bias against the product category; willingness to attend scheduled sessions.

**Trained vs Consumer Panel (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Trained Panel | Consumer Panel |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Members** | 8–12 (min 6) | 75–150+ |
| **Screening** | Extensive (sensitivity, acuity) | Demographic only |
| **Training** | 30–100+ hours | None |
| **Statistical power** | High (small diffs of 5–10%) | Low (diffs of 20–30%) |
| **Tests** | Triangle, duo-trio, QDA, profile, ranking | Hedonic (9-point), preference, acceptance, JAR |
| **Answers** | "What is the difference?" | "Do I like it?" |

**Examples:**
- *Trained panel → Triangle test* on a new orange juice vs a standard (to detect a small process change).
- *Consumer panel → 9-point hedonic test* on the same juice to gauge market acceptance.

---

#### Model Answer (b) — Sample Preparation, Cooling, and Order [7 marks]

**Importance of Sample Preparation (2 marks):**

Sample preparation must be **identical for all samples** in a test, otherwise the observed differences reflect the prep method rather than the product. Key controls:

- **Volume/weight:** Identical (e.g., 30 mL liquid; 25 g solid).
- **Temperature:** Controlled (e.g., 22 ± 1 °C, 60 ± 1 °C for hot, 5 ± 1 °C for cold).
- **Container:** Identical, opaque, coded.
- **Coding:** 3-digit random numbers (e.g., 381, 745, 296) — never sequential or suggestive.
- **Palate cleanser:** Deionized water at 22 ± 1 °C + unsalted crackers.
- **Freshness:** Prepared within 2 hours of testing.

**Cooling and Temperature Control (1 mark):**

- **Hot foods** held at 60 ± 2 °C in a water bath; pre-warmed containers; tested within 5 min of removal.
- **Cold foods** held at 5 ± 1 °C; pre-chilled containers.
- **Ambient** at 22 ± 1 °C.
- A 5 °C drift can shift sweetness by 8–12% and aroma by 15–20%.

**Order of Presentation and Randomization (3 marks):**

| Strategy | Use Case |
|----------|----------|
| Complete randomization | Consumer panels, large n |
| Latin Square | Trained panels, 3–6 samples |
| **Williams Latin Square** | Trained panels; balances first-order carry-over |
| Block randomization | Difference tests |
| Reference-first | Some descriptive tests |

**Williams Latin Square** generates $2n$ unique orderings for $n$ samples (instead of $n!$ for full randomization). For $n = 3$, six orderings are used: A B C, B C A, C A B, C B A, B A C, A C B. Each sample appears equally often in each position, and the number of times it immediately follows each other sample is constant.

**Carry-over effect** is the residual perception of sample N affecting the evaluation of sample N+1. Controlled by (a) Williams design, (b) 60–90 s palate-cleansing interval, and (c) a 5 min rest every 3 samples.

**Order effect** is the bias due to the position of a sample in the sequence (first-sample novelty, last-sample fatigue). Controlled by randomized assignment of orderings to panelists.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention numerical values** — e.g., stating "controlled temperature" without specifying **20–22 °C**. Always quote the **ISO/ASTM value** in your answer.
> 2. **Confusing CRI with CCT** — these are two completely different metrics. CRI is colour fidelity (0–100), CCT is colour temperature (in Kelvin). Many students lose 1–2 marks for this.
> 3. **Writing "Williams Square" without showing the actual orderings** — for the 7-mark descriptive question, the examiner expects at least one worked example (e.g., the 6 orderings for n = 3).
> 4. **Omitting the rationale** — merely listing factors is not enough; the KTU 2024 scheme (NEP-2020 aligned) rewards *why* a control matters, not just *what* it is.
> 5. **Skipping the consumer-vs-trained distinction** — a common trap. Even when the question is about "panel selection", a 1–2 mark sub-credit is often awarded for the comparison.
> 6. **Wrong use of "Latin Square" vs "Williams Latin Square"** — Williams is the correct choice for *carry-over* control in sensory tests; classical Latin Square is a general balanced design.
> 7. **Ignoring ethical considerations** — NEP 2020 places strong emphasis on *informed consent, voluntary participation, and the right to withdraw*. Mention these in the panel-selection question.
> 8. **Failing to code samples properly** — never write "1, 2, 3" or "A, B, C" in a real protocol; always use 3-digit random numbers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam):**

- [x] **ISO 8589** is the international standard for sensory lab design.
- [x] **Booth temperature:** 20–22 °C ± 2 °C; **Humidity:** 50–55% ± 5%.
- [x] **Air changes:** 10–15 per hour with **HEPA + activated carbon** filters.
- [x] **Background noise:** < 45 dB.
- [x] **Booth dimensions:** 0.9 m × 0.6 m; **Partition height:** ≥ 1.2 m.
- [x] **Pass-through window:** 35 cm × 35 cm.
- [x] **Lighting for colour work:** D65 daylight, CRI ≥ 90, **750–1000 lux**.
- [x] **Lighting for masking colour:** Red, green, or yellow filter.
- [x] **Macbeth Judge II Booth** has 5 light sources: A, CWF, WWF, D65, Horizon.
- [x] **CRI** = colour fidelity (0–100); **CCT** = colour temperature (K).
- [x] **Test schedule:** 10–11 AM or 15–16 PM; 1.5–2 h after a meal.
- [x] **Session length:** ≤ 60 min; **Samples/session:** 6–8 (trained), 4–6 (consumer).
- [x] **Inter-sample interval:** 60–90 s + 5 min rest every 3 samples.
- [x] **Sample coding:** 3-digit random numbers (e.g., 381, 745, 296).
- [x] **Sample volume:** Identical for all (e.g., 30 mL); **Container:** Opaque, identical.
- [x] **Serving temperature (hot):** 60 ± 2 °C; **(cold):** 5 ± 1 °C; **(ambient):** 22 ± 1 °C.
- [x] **Palate cleanser:** Deionized water + unsalted cracker.
- [x] **Williams Latin Square** for 3–6 samples to control carry-over.
- [x] **Trained panel size:** 8–12 (min 6); **Consumer panel:** 75–150+.
- [x] **Screening tests:** Snellen, Ishihara, taste thresholds, odour identification, texture ranking.
- [x] **Beidler's Law:** $R = R_{max} \cdot \dfrac{C}{K_d + C}$.
- [x] **Weber-Fechner Law:** $P = K \cdot \log(I/I_0)$.
- [x] **Trained panel answers "What is the difference?"; Consumer panel answers "Do I like it?"**
- [x] **Statistically, trained panels detect 5–10% differences; consumer panels need 20–30%.**
- [x] **Ethical considerations:** Informed consent, voluntary participation, right to withdraw (NEP 2020).

> **End of Notes — Module 2, Topic: Considerations for Testing Sensory Evaluation**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensory Tests for Food - Analytical: Discriminative (Difference Test, Sensitivity Test)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT524 - SENSORY EVALUATION OF FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensory Tests for Food - Analytical: Discriminative (Difference Test, Sensitivity Test)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Analytical Sensory Tests** are highly controlled objective methods used to measure specific sensory characteristics of a food product and to determine whether a perceptible difference exists between samples. Unlike affective (preference) tests, analytical tests answer the question *"Is there a detectable difference?"* — not *"Which one do you prefer?"*.

These analytical tests are further subdivided into:

- **Discriminative Tests** → Detect whether a sensory difference exists between two or more products.
- **Descriptive Tests** → Quantify the magnitude and nature of sensory attributes.
- **Sensitivity Tests** → Measure the lowest concentration at which a stimulus can be perceived (thresholds).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discriminative Tests vs Sensitivity Tests:**
> - A **Difference Test** asks: *"Can you tell these two products apart?"* (Uses a panel of trained or semi-trained assessors).
> - A **Sensitivity Test** asks: *"At what concentration can you first detect a substance?"* (Uses highly trained assessors, often a single expert or a selected panel).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Difference Test** like a **"spot-the-difference" game** in a newspaper: two pictures are placed side by side, and you are asked to identify if they are identical. You don't have to say which one is better or more beautiful — only whether a difference exists.

Now think of a **Sensitivity Test** like a **doctor's hearing test**: the doctor plays sounds of gradually increasing volume and asks *"Can you hear this yet?"* The moment you say "yes", that level is recorded as your hearing threshold. Similarly, in food science, we lower the concentration of sugar, salt, or a flavor compound until the panelist can no longer taste it — that minimum concentration is the **detection threshold**.

### 1.3 Classification of Tests Under Discriminative & Sensitivity Categories

| Test Type | Sub-Category | Test Name | Primary Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discriminative | Difference | Triangle Test | Are any two of three samples different? |
| Discriminative | Difference | Duo-Trio Test | Which one of two samples matches the reference? |
| Discriminative | Difference | Paired Comparison (Directional) | Is sample A more intense than sample B? |
| Discriminative | Difference | 2-out-of-5 Test | Which two of five samples are different? |
| Discriminative | Difference | A-Not-A Test | Is this sample A or Not-A? |
| Discriminative | Difference | Same-Different Test | Are these two samples the same or different? |
| Sensitivity | Threshold | Detection Threshold (DT) | Lowest concentration perceived as different from blank |
| Sensitivity | Threshold | Recognition Threshold (RT) | Lowest concentration at which the stimulus can be identified |
| Sensitivity | Threshold | Terminal/Difference Threshold | Smallest perceptible difference between two stimuli (JND) |
| Sensitivity | Scaling | Dilution Tests | Series of dilutions to find threshold |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Threshold Definition (ISO 5492):** The minimum physical intensity of a stimulus that elicits a sensory response 50% of the time under specified test conditions.

### 1.4 Key Vocabulary for KTU Examinations

- **Panelist / Assessor** — A person who evaluates a food sample.
- **Panel** — A group of assessors (typically 25–50 for difference tests, 6–12 for threshold tests).
- **Blind Test** — The identity of the sample is hidden from the assessor.
- **Randomized Code** — Three-digit codes (e.g., 537, 892) used to label samples.
- **Balanced Design** — Order of presentation is randomized to prevent order bias.
- **Carry-Over Effect** — Residual perception from a previous sample.
- **JND (Just Noticeable Difference)** — The smallest detectable difference between two stimuli.
- **Weber's Fraction (k)** — The constant ratio of the JND to the original stimulus intensity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Triangle Test (Three-Sample Forced-Choice Difference Test)

**Procedure:** Three coded samples are presented simultaneously — two are identical (A) and one is different (B). The panelist is forced to identify the odd sample. Test is repeated with the position of the odd sample balanced across panelists.

**Logic:** Under the null hypothesis (no difference), the probability of correctly identifying the odd sample by chance is **1/3 = 0.333**. The number of correct responses is compared to a statistical table for significance.

**Significance Levels:** Typically tested at $\alpha = 0.05$ (95% confidence) or $\alpha = 0.01$ (99% confidence).

**Number of Correct Responses Required for Significance (Triangle Test):**

| Number of Panelists (n) | Min. Correct for $\alpha = 0.05$ | Min. Correct for $\alpha = 0.01$ | Min. Correct for $\alpha = 0.001$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| 20 | 10 | 11 | 13 |
| 30 | 13 | 15 | 17 |
| 40 | 16 | 18 | 21 |
| 50 | 19 | 22 | 25 |
| 60 | 22 | 25 | 29 |
| 100 | 36 | 41 | 47 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beta (β) risk (Type II error):** The Triangle Test has a relatively high probability of missing a real difference (~20% when $\alpha = 0.05$ and n = 30). It is therefore considered a **moderately powerful** test.

### 2.2 The Duo-Trio Test

**Procedure:** A reference sample (R) is presented first, followed by two coded samples. The panelist identifies which of the two samples matches the reference.

**Probability under null hypothesis:** $P = 1/2 = 0.5$

**Significance Table (Duo-Trio Test):**

| n | $\alpha = 0.05$ | $\alpha = 0.01$ | $\alpha = 0.001$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 8 | 9 | — |
| 20 | 13 | 15 | 16 |
| 30 | 18 | 20 | 22 |
| 40 | 22 | 25 | 28 |
| 50 | 27 | 30 | 34 |
| 60 | 31 | 35 | 39 |
| 100 | 50 | 56 | 63 |

> [!TIP]
> The Duo-Trio Test is **more powerful** than the Triangle Test (lower β-risk) because the chance probability is 0.5 instead of 0.333, leading to a higher expected proportion of correct answers under H₁.

### 2.3 The Paired Comparison (Directional Difference) Test

**Procedure:** Two samples (A and B) are presented as a pair. The panelist is asked: *"Which sample has more [attribute]?"* — a directional question.

**Probability under null hypothesis:** $P = 1/2 = 0.5$

**Use Case:** When the tester wants to know not just IF a difference exists, but the DIRECTION of the difference (e.g., Is the new formula sweeter than the old?).

### 2.4 The 2-Out-of-5 Test

**Procedure:** Five samples are presented: two are of one type (A) and three are of another type (B), or vice versa. The panelist separates them into two groups.

**Probability under null hypothesis:** 
$$P(\text{correct}) = \frac{1}{\binom{5}{2}} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.10$$

**Why it is powerful:** The 1/10 chance probability makes this the **most statistically powerful** of all common difference tests, requiring the fewest panelists.

### 2.5 The A-Not-A Test

**Procedure:** The panelist is first trained to recognize sample "A". Then a series of samples is presented (some A, some not-A) and the panelist must classify each.

**Special Feature:** Useful for products where repeated exposure is not feasible (e.g., tasting strong flavors, menthol, capsaicin) because there is no carry-over effect — each sample is judged independently.

### 2.6 The Same-Different Test

**Procedure:** Pairs of samples are presented (some identical, some different). The panelist states whether the pair is the "same" or "different".

**Limitation:** Susceptible to response bias (some people say "same" too often, others say "different" too often). A correction using the **"re-parameterized" version of the test** can be applied.

### 2.7 Sensitivity Tests — Threshold Determination

The **psychophysical function** describes the relationship between stimulus concentration and the probability of detection:

$$P(\text{response}) = f(\log C)$$

This is typically a sigmoidal (S-shaped) curve.

**Three Classical Thresholds:**

1. **Detection Threshold (DT):** Smallest concentration at which a stimulus is detected as different from a blank.
2. **Recognition Threshold (RT):** Smallest concentration at which the stimulus can be qualitatively described ("sweet", "bitter", "lemony").
3. **Terminal/Difference Threshold:** The JND — the smallest change in stimulus intensity that can be perceived.

> **General relationship:** $DT < RT$ because detection occurs before qualitative recognition.

### 2.8 Weber's Law (JND Formula)

The **Just Noticeable Difference (JND)** follows Weber's Law for many sensory modalities:

$$\Delta I = k \cdot I$$

Where:
- $\Delta I$ = Just Noticeable Difference (JND)
- $I$ = Original stimulus intensity
- $k$ = Weber's fraction (constant for a given modality)

**Weber's Fraction values for taste:**
- Salt (NaCl): $k \approx 0.083$
- Sucrose (sweet): $k \approx 0.079$
- Citric acid (sour): $k \approx 0.085$

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Statement | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Triangle Test chance prob. | $P_0 = 1/3$ | Hypothesis testing | dimensionless |
| Duo-Trio chance prob. | $P_0 = 1/2$ | Hypothesis testing | dimensionless |
| 2-out-of-5 chance prob. | $P_0 = 1/10$ | Hypothesis testing | dimensionless |
| Same-Different chance prob. | $P_0 = 1/2$ | Hypothesis testing | dimensionless |
| A-Not-A chance prob. | $P_0 = 1/2$ | Hypothesis testing | dimensionless |
| Beta (β) for Triangle | $\beta \approx 0.20$ at n=30, α=0.05 | Power analysis | dimensionless |
| Weber's Law | $\Delta I = k \cdot I$ | JND calculation | varied |
| Weber's Fraction (salt) | $k \approx 0.083$ | Threshold | dimensionless |
| Psychometric Function | $P = f(\log C)$ | Threshold curve | probability |
| Forced-choice rule | Panelist MUST guess | Avoids zero response | — |
| Counterbalancing | Latin square design | Removes order bias | — |
| Min. panel (Triangle) | n ≥ 25–30 | Reliability | panelists |
| Min. panel (Threshold) | 6–12 trained | Reliability | panelists |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why We Use Forced-Choice Procedures:** Allowing panelists to answer "I don't know" inflates Type II error (β). Forced-choice forces a guess, which mathematically controls the chance probability to a known value (e.g., 1/3 for triangle).

### 2.10 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Quality Control:** Triangle tests in production lines to detect batch-to-batch variation.
- **Shelf-Life Studies:** Paired comparison to detect flavor loss over storage time.
- **New Product Development:** Duo-trio to compare prototypes against the market leader.
- **Ingredient Substitution:** A-Not-A test for reformulated products (e.g., sugar → artificial sweetener).
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Detection thresholds for off-flavors in drinking water (e.g., 2-Methylisoborneol at 10 ng/L).
- **Sensory Fatigue Studies:** Threshold tests to measure panelist desensitization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Chance Probability for the Triangle Test

Under the null hypothesis ($H_0$: "There is NO difference between samples"), the panelist has **no sensory information** to identify the odd sample. The decision is therefore random.

**Step 1 — Define the sample set.**

The panelist is presented with 3 samples: 2 of type A and 1 of type B.

**Step 2 — Count the total possible choices.**

The number of ways to choose 1 sample (the "odd one") out of 3 is:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \binom{3}{1} = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Count the favorable (correct) outcomes under $H_0$.**

Only **1** of these 3 choices is the true odd sample.

$$
N_{\text{correct}} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the classical definition of probability.**

$$
P(\text{correct} \mid H_0) = \frac{N_{\text{correct}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

**Conclusion:** Under $H_0$, a panelist will be correct 1/3 of the time purely by chance.

### 3.2 Derivation of Chance Probability for the 2-Out-of-5 Test

**Step 1 — Define the sample set.**

5 samples are presented: 2 of type A and 3 of type B (or vice versa).

**Step 2 — Count the total ways to separate into two groups.**

The panelist must group the 5 samples into a set of 2 and a set of 3. The number of ways to choose which 2 samples form one group is:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \binom{5}{2} = \frac{5!}{2! \cdot 3!} = 10
$$

**Step 3 — Count the correct grouping.**

Only **1** of these 10 groupings correctly separates the 2 A's from the 3 B's.

$$
N_{\text{correct}} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Compute probability.**

$$
P(\text{correct} \mid H_0) = \frac{1}{10} = 0.10
$$

**Conclusion:** With chance probability of just 10%, the 2-out-of-5 test is the most powerful of the standard difference tests.

### 3.3 Derivation of Sample Size Using the Normal Approximation (Triangle Test)

For large $n$, the binomial distribution is approximated by the normal distribution.

**Step 1 — State the null and alternative hypotheses.**

$$
H_0 : p = \tfrac{1}{3} \quad \text{(no difference)} \qquad H_1 : p > \tfrac{1}{3} \quad \text{(difference exists)}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the test statistic.**

For a binomial proportion $p$ with $n$ trials, the standardized statistic is:

$$
Z = \frac{X/n - p_0}{\sqrt{\frac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}}}
$$

Where $X$ = number of correct responses, $p_0 = 1/3$.

**Step 3 — Set the significance level.**

For a one-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical $Z$-value is:

$$
Z_{\text{critical}} = 1.645
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$ given a target proportion of correct answers.**

Assume the panel achieves $p_1 = 0.6$ (60% correct) when a real difference exists. We want the test to detect this with probability $1 - \beta = 0.80$ (i.e., $\beta = 0.20$, $Z_\beta = 0.842$).

**Step 5 — Use the sample size formula for two proportions.**

$$
n = \left[ \frac{Z_{\alpha} \sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)} + Z_{\beta} \sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)}}{p_1 - p_0} \right]^2
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the values.**

$$
n = \left[ \frac{1.645 \cdot \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3}} + 0.842 \cdot \sqrt{0.6 \cdot 0.4}}{0.6 - 0.333} \right]^2
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate each component.**

$$
\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3}} = \sqrt{0.2222} = 0.4714
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.6 \cdot 0.4} = \sqrt{0.24} = 0.4899
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
1.645 \cdot 0.4714 + 0.842 \cdot 0.4899 = 0.7755 + 0.4125 = 1.188
$$

**Step 9 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
0.6 - 0.333 = 0.267
$$

**Step 10 — Final calculation.**

$$
n = \left( \frac{1.188}{0.267} \right)^2 = (4.449)^2 = 19.79
$$

**Step 11 — Round up.**

$$
\boxed{n \approx 20 \text{ panelists}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **minimum** sample size. In practice, sensory scientists use $n \geq 30$ to improve robustness against dropout and individual variation.

### 3.4 Derivation of Detection Threshold via Psychometric Function

**Step 1 — Define a series of dilutions.**

Let concentration $C_i = C_0 / d^i$ for $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, k$ where $d$ is the dilution factor (typically 2 or 3).

**Step 2 — Present to the panel in ascending concentration.**

Each panelist evaluates each concentration. The response is binary: detected (1) or not detected (0).

**Step 3 — Compute the proportion detecting at each level.**

$$
\hat{p}_i = \frac{\text{Number of "detected" responses at level } i}{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Fit a logistic or probit model.**

A common choice is the logistic function:

$$
P(C) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \log C)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the concentration at $P = 0.5$ (50% detection).**

Setting $P = 0.5$:

$$
0.5 = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \log C_{50})}}
$$

The denominator must equal 2:

$$
1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \log C_{50})} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \log C_{50})} = 1
$$

Taking natural log of both sides:

$$
-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \log C_{50}) = 0
$$

Solving for $\log C_{50}$:

$$
\log C_{50} = -\frac{\beta_0}{\beta_1}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert back to linear concentration.**

$$
C_{\text{DT}} = 10^{-\beta_0 / \beta_1}
$$

**Conclusion:** The detection threshold is defined as the concentration at which 50% of the panel detects the stimulus.

### 3.5 Derivation of Weber's Fraction (JND Calculation)

**Step 1 — Weber's empirical observation.**

For many stimuli, the JND is proportional to the original stimulus intensity:

$$
\Delta I = k \cdot I
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Weber fraction $k$.**

$$
k = \frac{\Delta I}{I}
$$

**Step 3 — Example calculation for salt.**

Suppose a 5% NaCl solution is tasted. The minimum detectable increase in concentration is 0.4%.

$$
k = \frac{0.4}{5.0} = 0.08
$$

**Step 4 — Predict the JND at a different concentration.**

At 8% NaCl, the predicted JND would be:

$$
\Delta I = 0.08 \cdot 8.0 = 0.64\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A higher baseline concentration requires a larger absolute change to be noticed.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Test

| Scenario | Recommended Test | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Detect any difference (no direction known) | Triangle Test | Simple, widely used, balanced design |
| Compare new vs. reference (no direction) | Duo-Trio Test | Higher power than triangle |
| Compare sweetness / saltiness direction | Paired Comparison | Provides directional answer |
| Limited number of expert panelists | 2-out-of-5 Test | Most powerful (1/10 chance) |
| Strong/lingering stimuli (e.g., capsaicin) | A-Not-A Test | No carry-over effect |
| Repeated tasting not feasible | Same-Different Test | Quicker but needs bias correction |
| Find min. detectable concentration | Detection Threshold Test | Uses ascending dilution series |
| Find min. identifiable concentration | Recognition Threshold Test | Panelist must name the sensation |
| Find min. perceivable change | Difference Threshold (JND) | Weber's Law applies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power ranking (most → least powerful):** 2-out-of-5 > Duo-Trio ≈ Paired Comparison > Triangle > Same-Different > A-Not-A.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flow for Selecting a Discriminative Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need a Difference Test] --> B{Is the direction of difference known?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Paired Comparison Directional Test]
    B -- No --> D{How many expert panelists are available?}
    D -- Fewer than 15 --> E[Use 2-out-of-5 Test]
    D -- 15 to 50 --> F{Is the stimulus strong with carry-over risk?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use A-Not-A Test]
    F -- No --> H{Is a reference sample feasible?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use Duo-Trio Test]
    H -- No --> J[Use Triangle Test]
    E --> K[End: Test Selected]
    C --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: How a Triangle Test is Conducted

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Prepare three coded samples] --> S2[Step 2: Two identical, one different]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Randomize position of odd sample]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Present all three simultaneously]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Panelist identifies the odd sample]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Record number of correct responses]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Compare to critical value table]
    S7 --> S8{Is number of correct responses above threshold?}
    S8 -- Yes --> R1[Conclusion: Significant difference exists]
    S8 -- No --> R2[Conclusion: No significant difference]
```

### 4.3 Threshold Determination Procedure (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Start: Define stimulus and solvent] --> T2[Step 1: Prepare dilution series]
    T2 --> T3[Step 2: Begin with lowest concentration]
    T3 --> T4[Step 3: Present to panelist]
    T4 --> T5{Does panelist detect stimulus?}
    T5 -- No --> T6[Step 4: Increase concentration by dilution factor]
    T6 --> T4
    T5 -- Yes --> T7[Step 5: Record as detection point]
    T7 --> T8[Step 6: Continue for all panel members]
    T8 --> T9[Step 7: Compute group detection threshold at 50 percent]
    T9 --> T10[End: Threshold value obtained]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Power Analysis of Difference Tests

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[2 out of 5: chance 1 over 10] --> P1[Highest power]
    B[Duo Trio: chance 1 over 2] --> P2[High power]
    C[Paired Comparison: chance 1 over 2] --> P2
    D[Triangle: chance 1 over 3] --> P3[Moderate power]
    E[Same Different: chance 1 over 2 with bias risk] --> P4[Lower effective power]
    F[A Not A: chance 1 over 2] --> P4
    P1 --> Z[Power ranking from highest to lowest]
    P2 --> Z
    P3 --> Z
    P4 --> Z
```

### 4.5 Forced-Choice vs Free-Choice Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[How should panelists respond?] --> Q2{Is the stimulus subtle?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Forced Choice: must pick an answer]
    Q2 -- No --> R3[Free Choice allowed: less sensitive test]
    R1 --> R2[Result: Chance probability is known and controlled]
    R3 --> R4[Result: Higher beta risk and undefined chance probability]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a discriminative sensory test. List any four commonly used difference tests.*

**Model Answer:**

A **discriminative sensory test** is an objective analytical sensory method used to determine whether a perceptible difference exists between two or more food samples, without measuring preference or liking.

Four commonly used difference tests are:
1. **Triangle Test** — three samples (two identical, one different); panelist identifies the odd sample.
2. **Duo-Trio Test** — one reference plus two coded samples; panelist picks the one matching the reference.
3. **Paired Comparison (Directional)** — two samples; panelist judges which has more of a specified attribute.
4. **2-out-of-5 Test** — five samples; panelist separates the two-of-a-kind from the three-of-a-kind.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the list with brief description)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is a detection threshold? How does it differ from a recognition threshold?*

**Model Answer:**

- **Detection Threshold (DT):** The lowest concentration of a stimulus at which a sensory difference from a blank (e.g., water) can be perceived, without necessarily identifying the nature of the stimulus. By ISO 5492, this is the concentration detected 50% of the time.
- **Recognition Threshold (RT):** The lowest concentration at which the stimulus can be qualitatively identified and described (e.g., "this tastes sweet" or "this is bitter").

**Key Difference:** $DT < RT$ — Detection occurs at a lower concentration than recognition because the assessor must first perceive a *change* before they can *name* it.

*(3 marks: 1 for DT + 1 for RT + 1 for the comparison)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure, application, and statistical analysis of the **Triangle Test** in sensory evaluation of foods. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **Weber's Law** as applied to sensory evaluation. The JND for sucrose at a 5% solution is 0.4%. Calculate Weber's fraction and predict the JND at 12% sucrose concentration. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Principle:** Three coded samples are presented simultaneously — two are identical (A) and one is different (B). The panelist is forced to identify the odd sample.

2. **Procedure:**
   - Prepare three samples: 2 × A and 1 × B (or vice versa).
   - Label with random three-digit codes.
   - Counterbalance the position of the odd sample across panelists using a Latin square design to remove position bias.
   - Serve in identical containers under uniform conditions (temperature, lighting).
   - Panelist sniffs/tastes and writes down the code of the odd sample.

3. **Applications:**
   - Quality control in production lines.
   - Reformulation trials (e.g., checking if replacing an ingredient changes flavor).
   - Shelf-life studies to detect flavor deterioration.
   - Comparison of two processing methods.

4. **Statistical Analysis:**
   - Under $H_0$ (no difference), the chance probability of being correct is $P_0 = 1/3$.
   - Count the number of correct responses $X$ out of $n$ panelists.
   - Compare $X$ to the critical value from the Triangle Test significance table at $\alpha = 0.05$.
   - If $X \geq X_{\text{critical}}$ → reject $H_0$ and conclude a significant difference exists.
   - If $X < X_{\text{critical}}$ → fail to reject $H_0$; no significant difference detected.

5. **Limitations:** The Triangle Test has a relatively high β-risk (~20% at n = 30, α = 0.05). It also does not tell the *direction* or *magnitude* of the difference.

*(7 marks: 1 principle + 2 procedure + 2 application + 2 statistical analysis)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

1. **Weber's Law Statement:** The Just Noticeable Difference (JND) is proportional to the magnitude of the original stimulus. Mathematically:

$$
\Delta I = k \cdot I
$$

where $k$ is Weber's fraction (a constant for a given sensory modality).

2. **Given Data:**
   - Original concentration $I = 5\%$ sucrose.
   - JND $\Delta I = 0.4\%$.

3. **Calculate Weber's fraction $k$:**

$$
k = \frac{\Delta I}{I} = \frac{0.4}{5.0} = 0.08
$$

4. **Predict JND at 12% sucrose:**

$$
\Delta I_{\text{new}} = k \cdot I_{\text{new}} = 0.08 \cdot 12 = 0.96\%
$$

5. **Interpretation:** As the baseline concentration increases from 5% to 12%, the absolute JND also increases from 0.4% to 0.96%, confirming Weber's prediction that a higher baseline requires a larger absolute change to be perceptible.

*(7 marks: 1 law statement + 1 data + 2 calculation of k + 2 calculation of new JND + 1 interpretation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare the **Triangle Test, Duo-Trio Test, and 2-out-of-5 Test** with respect to procedure, chance probability, and statistical power. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **sensitivity testing** in sensory evaluation. Describe a procedure for determining the **detection threshold** of sucrose in water. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | Triangle Test | Duo-Trio Test | 2-out-of-5 Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of samples** | 3 (2A + 1B) | 3 (1 reference + 2 coded) | 5 (2 of one type + 3 of other) |
| **Panelist task** | Identify the odd one | Pick the one matching the reference | Group the 2 of one kind vs 3 of another |
| **Chance probability** | 1/3 ≈ 0.333 | 1/2 = 0.500 | 1/10 = 0.100 |
| **Direction of difference** | Not determined | Not determined | Not determined |
| **Statistical power** | Moderate | High | Highest |
| **Min. panelists (typical)** | 25–30 | 20–25 | 10–15 |
| **Best used when** | Generic difference check | Reference available | Few expert panelists |
| **Counterbalancing** | Position of odd sample | Order of coded samples | Position of pairs |

**Ranking (most powerful → least):** 2-out-of-5 > Duo-Trio > Triangle.

The 2-out-of-5 test requires the fewest panelists because the chance probability (1/10) is so low that even a small number of correct answers yields statistical significance.

*(7 marks: 2 procedure comparison + 2 chance probability + 2 power + 1 conclusion)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

1. **Concept of Sensitivity Testing:** Sensitivity testing measures the lowest concentration of a stimulus that can be perceived by a panelist. It evaluates the *acuity* of the human sensory system and is essential for establishing detection limits of flavors, off-flavors, and contaminants.

2. **Panel Selection:** 6–12 panelists are selected and trained using standard reference solutions (e.g., sucrose in water at known concentrations). Only those whose individual thresholds fall within an acceptable range are retained.

3. **Procedure for Detection Threshold of Sucrose:**
   - **Step 1:** Prepare a dilution series of sucrose in distilled water, e.g., 0.1%, 0.2%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 2.0%, 3.0%, 5.0%, 7.0%, 10.0%.
   - **Step 2:** Label all samples with three-digit random codes. Include blank (water) samples for control.
   - **Step 3:** Present samples to panelists in **ascending order of concentration** (to avoid adaptation/fatigue effects).
   - **Step 4:** For each concentration, ask the panelist: *"Is there any taste here, or is it just water?"* — a **forced-choice 2-AFC** (2-alternative forced choice) is often used: present sucrose vs. water, and ask which one tastes different.
   - **Step 5:** Record the lowest concentration at which the panelist correctly identifies the sweetened sample.
   - **Step 6:** Repeat for all panel members and calculate the group detection threshold as the concentration detected by **50% of the panel** (group best-estimate threshold) or by each panelist individually (individual threshold).
   - **Step 7:** Plot a psychometric function: % detection vs. log(concentration) to verify the sigmoidal shape.

4. **Expected value:** The detection threshold for sucrose in water is typically around 0.2–0.5% w/v.

*(7 marks: 1 concept + 1 panel selection + 4 procedure steps + 1 expected value)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Loss Areas):**
> 1. **Mixing up "Discriminative" and "Affective" tests** — Affective tests measure preference (Hedonic scale); discriminative tests measure only the presence/absence of a difference. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting "Forced-Choice" terminology** — Always mention that the test is *forced-choice* and identify the chance probability.
> 3. **Wrong chance probability for 2-out-of-5** — Students often write 1/5 or 1/2. The correct value is **1/10** because you must choose *which 2* are the same.
> 4. **Weber's Law sign error** — Using $k = I/\Delta I$ instead of $k = \Delta I/I$.
> 5. **Confusing DT and RT** — Detection ≠ Recognition. The examiner expects you to clearly distinguish.
> 6. **Skipping counterbalancing** — Always mention random/balanced presentation order; this is a 1-mark item often missed.
> 7. **Omitting the null hypothesis** — Every difference test must explicitly state $H_0$ and the chance probability.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Discriminative tests** answer *"Is there a difference?"* — they are **not** preference tests.
- **Five major difference tests:** Triangle, Duo-Trio, Paired Comparison, 2-out-of-5, A-Not-A (Same-Different).
- **Chance probabilities to memorize:**
  - Triangle = 1/3
  - Duo-Trio = 1/2
  - Paired Comparison = 1/2
  - 2-out-of-5 = **1/10** (most powerful)
  - A-Not-A = 1/2
  - Same-Different = 1/2
- **Significance is tested using pre-computed tables** at $\alpha = 0.05$ (5% risk) or $\alpha = 0.01$ (1% risk).
- **Sensitivity tests** measure the *minimum perceptible intensity* — detection threshold (DT), recognition threshold (RT), and difference threshold (JND).
- **DT < RT** (you detect before you can name).
- **Weber's Law:** $\Delta I = k \cdot I$. Weber's fraction $k$ is constant for a given modality within its working range.
- **Weber's fractions to remember:** Salt ≈ 0.08, Sugar ≈ 0.08, Citric acid ≈ 0.085.
- **Forced-choice procedures** are mandatory to control chance probability.
- **Counterbalancing** (Latin square) removes position and order bias.
- **Power ranking (high → low):** 2-out-of-5 > Duo-Trio > Paired Comparison > Triangle > A-Not-A.
- **Triangle test sample size:** typically $n \geq 30$ for 80% power.
- **Threshold test panel size:** 6–12 trained panelists.
- **Psychometric function** is sigmoidal: $P(\text{detection})$ vs. $\log(\text{concentration})$.
- **The 50% point on the psychometric function defines the threshold.**
- **Applications** include QC, shelf-life studies, reformulation, and regulatory compliance for off-flavor limits.
- **Limitations of difference tests:** high β-risk, no magnitude info, no direction (except Paired Comparison).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensory Tests for Food - Descriptive: Category Scaling, Ratio Scaling, Flavour Profile Analysis, Texture Profile Analysis, Quantitative Descriptive Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT524 - SENSORY EVALUATION OF FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensory Tests for Food - Descriptive: Category Scaling, Ratio Scaling, Flavour Profile Analysis, Texture Profile Analysis, Quantitative Descriptive Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Descriptive Sensory Tests — The Formal Definition

In the framework of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT524)**, *Descriptive Sensory Tests* are defined as a class of advanced sensory methods in which **trained panelists** identify (qualitative) AND quantify (intensity) the perceived sensory attributes of a food product. They sit at the top of the **sensory test hierarchy** — above discrimination tests (which only answer *“is there a difference?”*) and above affective/consumer tests (which only answer *“do you like it?”*).

A descriptive test answers the most powerful question in sensory science:

> **“What is the product, how intense is each attribute, and how does it differ from another product?”**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The five methods explicitly listed are:
> 1. **Category Scaling** (also called *Line/Interval Scaling*)
> 2. **Ratio Scaling** (Magnitude Estimation)
> 3. **Flavour Profile Analysis (FPA)**
> 4. **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)**
> 5. **Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA)**
>
> All five are *trained-panel* tests, not consumer tests.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The “Movie Reviewer” Analogy

Imagine a panel of food testers as a team of professional **movie critics** who have been trained for years to articulate exactly *why* they like or dislike a film.

| Analogy Element | Sensory Science Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Critic’s vocabulary (cinematography, pacing, dialogue) | Descriptive lexicon (astringent, springy, buttery) |
| Star rating (★/5) | Category scale (9-point hedonic) |
| “This tomato is 3× more sour than the last one” | Ratio scaling (magnitude estimation) |
| Flavour-only review (taste + aroma) | Flavour Profile Analysis |
| Texture-only review (mouthfeel, chew) | Texture Profile Analysis |
| Full critical review (look, sound, story, feel) | Quantitative Descriptive Analysis |

In short, a descriptive test is the **verbal + numerical fingerprint** of a food product. Just as a fingerprint uniquely identifies a person, a QDA profile uniquely identifies a product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (exam favourite):**
> - **Category Scaling** → assigns numbers from a *finite, ordered* set (e.g., 1–9).
> - **Ratio Scaling** → assigns numbers that *preserve true ratios* of sensation.
> - **FPA / TPA** → are *legacy, company-developed* descriptive methods.
> - **QDA** → is the *modern, statistically robust* descriptive method (industry gold standard).

---

## 1.3 The Five Methods at a Glance

| # | Method | Panel Size | Scale Type | Key Originator / Year |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Category Scaling | 25–50 semi-trained | Categorical (e.g., 5, 7, 9-point) | Generic / Multiple |
| 2 | Ratio Scaling (Magnitude Estimation) | 10–25 trained | Open-ended ratio | S. S. Stevens (1960s) |
| 3 | Flavour Profile Analysis | 4–6 trained | 15-point category | Cairncross & Sjöström (Arthur D. Little, 1949–1970) |
| 4 | Texture Profile Analysis | 5–10 trained | 14-point category | Szczesniak, Brandt, Friedman (General Foods, 1963) |
| 5 | Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA®) | 10–12 trained | 15-cm unstructured line | Stone, Sidel, Oliver (Tragon Corp., 1974) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marks Tip:** Notice that FPA uses *flavour* alone, TPA uses *texture* alone, while QDA combines **all** attributes (appearance, aroma, flavour, texture, aftertaste) in a single statistically validated method. This is the most-asked comparison in KTU exams.

---

## 1.4 Why Are These Tests Important in Food Engineering?

- **Product Development (R&D):** “Lead” vs. “lag” sensory attributes in new formulations.
- **Quality Control (QC):** Detect drift from a target sensory fingerprint.
- **Shelf-Life Studies:** Track oxidation (rancidity), staling, or taints over time.
- **Competitive Benchmarking:** Quantify how a brand differs from a competitor on *specific* descriptors (e.g., “crunchy,” “juicy,” “metallic”).
- **Cost Optimization:** Replace expensive instruments (e.g., texturometers) for some attributes with trained human panels.

---

## 1.5 Foundational Terminology (bold the constants)

- **Lexicon** — the agreed-upon set of descriptive words used by the panel.
- **Intensity** — the perceived magnitude of a sensory attribute; reported on an ordinal or ratio scale.
- **Panel Leader** — the scientist who trains, monitors, and statistically validates the panel.
- **Reference Standard (Anchor)** — a physical or chemical reference used to anchor the ends of an intensity scale (e.g., refined sugar for “sweet,” citric acid for “sour”).
- **Rep** — a single sensory evaluation; usually **8–15 reps** are conducted per product for reproducibility.
- **Replicates** — independent sessions on different days, to remove day-effect bias.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Category Scaling — Theory

**Category Scaling** is the most widely used scaling method in sensory science. The panelist chooses one category from an ordered, discrete set, and the chosen number is the response.

### 2.1.1 Principles
1. Categories are **ordered** (weak → strong).
2. The categories are assumed to be **equidistant** on the psychological continuum (this is an assumption, not a proven fact).
3. Data are **ordinal**; therefore, only **non-parametric statistics** (medians, Mann-Whitney U, Friedman test) or rank transformations are strictly correct.
4. End anchors are defined by **reference standards**.

### 2.1.2 Common Category Scales

| Scale Name | Categories | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| 3-Point | weak / moderate / strong | Quick screening |
| 5-Point | very weak → very strong | Affective / trained panels |
| **9-Point Hedonic** | 1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely | **Consumer acceptance** |
| 15-Point (Generic Descriptive) | 0 = none, 15 = extreme | **FPA, TPA, QDA** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 9-point hedonic scale is *affective*, not descriptive.** However, the same 9-point *intensity* structure (1 = extremely weak, 9 = extremely strong) is *descriptive* and is widely used in KTU syllabus contexts. Do not confuse the two.

### 2.1.3 Mathematical Treatment

Although category data are ordinal, in practice they are treated numerically (interval-like) for parametric statistics. The mean and standard deviation are computed as:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}{N}
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N-1}}
$$

**Why it works (intuition):** With *trained* panelists and well-anchored categories, the interval assumption holds *approximately*; ANOVA and t-tests can be used cautiously.

---

## 2.2 Ratio Scaling — Theory (Magnitude Estimation)

**Ratio Scaling**, also called **Magnitude Estimation (ME)**, was introduced by **S. S. Stevens** in the 1950s–60s. The panelist assigns a number proportional to the perceived intensity — there is **no upper or lower bound** and **no fixed category**.

### 2.2.1 Procedure
1. The panelist is given a *reference* sample and told it has an arbitrary value (commonly **10** or **100**).
2. Subsequent samples are rated as ratios: “This is 2× as intense as the reference,” so the response is **20** (or **200**).
3. The data are **log-transformed** before statistical analysis (because variance is proportional to the mean — a hallmark of ratio data).

### 2.2.2 Key Property — The Power Law

Stevens demonstrated that the perceived intensity $I$ is related to the physical stimulus $S$ by:

$$
I = k \cdot S^n
$$

Taking logarithms:

$$
\log I = \log k + n \cdot \log S
$$

The exponent **$n$** is the **characteristic of the sensory continuum**:

| Modality | Exponent $n$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Saltiness (NaCl) | ≈ 1.3 | Slightly compressed |
| Sweetness (sucrose) | ≈ 1.0 | Linear |
| **Sourness (citric acid)** | ≈ 0.8 | **Compressed** |
| **Bitterness (quinine)** | ≈ 0.6 | **Highly compressed** |
| Loudness | ≈ 0.6 | Highly compressed |
| Heaviness (weight) | ≈ 1.45 | Expanded |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Knowing $n$ allows food technologists to predict how much sucrose or salt to add to double the perceived sweetness or saltiness — directly applicable to product reformulation for **sodium or sugar reduction**.

---

## 2.3 Flavour Profile Analysis (FPA)

### 2.3.1 Origin & Philosophy
- Developed at **Arthur D. Little, Inc.**, in the late 1940s by **Cairncross and Sjöström**, formalised by **Keane (1992)**.
- **Goal:** Capture the *complete flavour experience* — aroma + taste + aftertaste + mouthfeel — of a product using a small, highly trained panel.
- **Panel size:** **4–6 panelists** (smaller than QDA).
- **Sessions per product:** Multiple reps, each in **separate booths** to avoid panelist interaction.

### 2.3.2 Procedure
1. **Orientation:** Panelists are trained using reference standards to agree on the *lexicon*.
2. **Evaluation:** Each panelist independently describes the sequence and intensity of flavour notes.
3. **Scoring:** A **15-point category scale** (0 = none, 15 = extremely strong) is used for each descriptor.
4. **Discussion:** Panelists convene and *reconcile* their individual scores into a **consensus profile** under the panel leader.
5. **Output:** A *flavour wheel* (radar / spider plot) and a written narrative.

### 2.3.3 Characteristic Features
- **Consensus-based** (subjective) — not statistically replicated.
- Highly **holistic**; captures *temporal* aspects (first impression, mid-palate, finish).
- Best for **complex, layered** products (wine, coffee, cheese, chocolate, whiskey).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** FPA is *flavour only* — it does **not** include texture or appearance. TPA covers texture separately. QDA covers *everything*.

---

## 2.4 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)

### 2.4.1 Origin
- **General Foods Corporation, USA, 1963.**
- Pioneers: **Alina Szczesniak, M. A. Brandt, L. L. Friedman** (and later the Skinner / Bourne / Cadden group).
- Designed to translate *human mouthfeel* into the language of **food rheology** and later **instrumental texture** (TPA on a texturometer, e.g., Instron, TA-XT2, Stable Micro Systems).

### 2.4.2 Conceptual Framework
Szczesniak classified texture into three layers:
1. **Mechanical Characteristics** — how the food reacts to stress (hard, firm, soft).
2. **Geometrical Characteristics** — shape, particle size, orientation (gritty, fibrous, cellular).
3. **Other (Mouthfeel) Characteristics** — moisture, fat content, oiliness.

### 2.4.3 Standard TPA Parameters (Sensory + Instrumental)

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Instrumental Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** | $H$ | Force required to bite through | Peak force of first compression, $F_1$ |
| **Cohesiveness** | $C$ | Degree to which food holds together | Ratio of positive force areas, $A_2 / A_1$ |
| **Adhesiveness** | $A_d$ | Work needed to pull food from mouth | Negative area, $A_3$ |
| **Springiness** | $S_p$ | Rate at which deformed food returns to shape | $t_2 / t_1$ |
| **Chewiness** | $C_h$ | Work to chew solid food to swallow | $H \times C \times S_p$ |
| **Gumminess** | $G$ | Work to chew semi-solid to swallow | $H \times C$ |
| **Resilience** | $R$ | How well a food “pops back” | $A_4 / A_1$ |
| **Fracturability** | $F$ | Force at which food fractures | Force at first significant drop |

### 2.4.4 Panel Scale
- The sensory panel rates each parameter on a **14-point category scale** (or 15-point, depending on the cited version):
  - **1** = very weak / none
  - **7** = moderate
  - **14** (or 15) = very strong / extreme
- Ratings are taken at **first bite** and **mastication**.

---

## 2.5 Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA®)

### 2.5.1 Origin
- Developed by **Howard Stone, Joel Sidel, and Shirley Oliver** at **Tragon Corporation, USA, 1974**.
- Built to **overcome the subjectivity of FPA** and the **narrow scope of TPA**.
- Now a **registered trademark** of Tragon; the generic term is **Descriptive Analysis (DA)**.

### 2.5.2 Why QDA Reigns Supreme
- **Statistical rigour:** Every attribute is rated by 10–12 trained panelists, **8–15 replicates**, allowing ANOVA, MANOVA, PCA, and external preference mapping.
- **Reproducibility:** Individual booths, no panel consensus, blind-coded samples, randomised design.
- **Comprehensiveness:** Appearance, aroma, flavour, texture, aftertaste — all in one test.
- **Scale:** 15-cm **unstructured line scale**; panelists mark a point; the distance from the left anchor is measured (converted to a numerical score, typically 0–15 or 0–100).

### 2.5.3 Steps of QDA
1. **Lexicon Development** — open discussion, attribute generation, reference standards.
2. **Panel Training** — 50–100 hours over 4–12 weeks; use of references.
3. **Sample Evaluation** — randomised, replicated, blind-coded, in individual booths.
4. **Data Analysis** — ANOVA, PCA, correlation with consumer data.
5. **Reporting** — spider plots, mean intensity bars, statistical groupings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard:** ISO 13299 (2016) and ASTM MNL 13 recognise QDA-type methods as the benchmark for sensory descriptive work worldwide.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic mean | $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{N}$ | Average intensity score | points |
| Standard deviation | $s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N-1}}$ | Panel variance | points |
| Stevens’ Power Law | $I = k \cdot S^n$ | Perceived intensity vs. stimulus | dimensionless |
| Log-form Power Law | $\log I = \log k + n \log S$ | Linearise for regression | log units |
| TPA — Hardness | $H = F_1$ | Peak force of 1st bite | N (or g) |
| TPA — Cohesiveness | $C = A_2 \div A_1$ | Work ratio 2nd / 1st compression | dimensionless |
| TPA — Springiness | $S_p = t_2 \div t_1$ | Time ratio recovery | dimensionless |
| TPA — Adhesiveness | $A_d = \vert A_3 \vert$ | Area of negative force | N·s |
| TPA — Chewiness | $C_h = H \times C \times S_p$ | Energy to chew solid | N or J |
| TPA — Gumminess | $G = H \times C$ | Energy to chew semi-solid | N |
| TPA — Resilience | $R = A_4 \div A_1$ | Instantaneous recovery | dimensionless |
| Line-scale conversion | $L_{score} = \dfrac{x_{mark} - x_{left}}{x_{right} - x_{left}} \times 15$ | Convert line-scale to 0–15 | points |
| QDA — Anchor length | $L = 15 \text{ cm}$ | Standard unstructured line | cm |
| Reproducibility (CV) | $CV = \dfrac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$ | Panel agreement | % |

> [!NOTE]
> All TPA mechanical parameters are **dimensionless except hardness** (force) and adhesiveness (work). Chewiness has the unit of energy/work because it is the product of force × distance × dimensionless ratios. In strict SI, chewiness is in **joules**, but texturometers often report it in **N** when using peak force × dimensionless ratios.

---

## 2.7 Comparative Master Table (Most Important for KTU)

| Aspect | Category Scaling | Ratio Scaling (ME) | FPA | TPA | QDA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Descriptive | Descriptive | Descriptive | Descriptive | Descriptive |
| Trained panel? | Semi-trained | Trained | Highly trained | Highly trained | Highly trained |
| Panel size | 25–50 | 10–25 | 4–6 | 5–10 | 10–12 |
| Scale | 5, 7, 9, 15-pt category | Open-ended (ratio) | 15-pt category | 14–15-pt category | 15-cm unstructured line |
| Attributes | All | All | Flavour only | Texture only | All (appearance → aftertaste) |
| Statistical analysis | Non-parametric / careful parametric | Log-transformation | Consensus (subjective) | ANOVA / PCA | Full ANOVA / MANOVA / PCA |
| Origin | Generic | Stevens 1950s | A.D. Little 1949 | General Foods 1963 | Tragon 1974 |
| Trademark / Patent? | No | No | No | No | **Yes (QDA®)** |
| Industry use | High | Research | Niche / traditional | Common | **Gold standard** |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Stevens’ Power Law (Ratio Scaling)

The foundation of ratio scaling is **Stevens’ Psychophysical Power Law**. Below is a clean step-by-step derivation for a KTU answer.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let:
- $I$ = perceived sensory intensity
- $S$ = physical stimulus magnitude (e.g., concentration of sucrose, % w/v)
- $k$ = constant depending on the modality and units
- $n$ = modality-specific exponent (psychophysical constant)

**Step 1.** Stevens proposed from experimental data that equal **ratios of stimulus** produce equal **ratios of sensation**:

$$
\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \left( \frac{S_2}{S_1} \right)^n
$$

**Step 2.** Choose $S_1$ as a reference stimulus. Rearranging for $I$:

$$
I_2 = I_1 \cdot \left( \frac{S_2}{S_1} \right)^n
$$

**Step 3.** Let $I_1 = k \cdot S_1^{\,n}$ and substitute:

$$
I_2 = k \cdot S_1^{\,n} \cdot \left( \frac{S_2}{S_1} \right)^n
$$

$$
I_2 = k \cdot S_1^{\,n} \cdot \frac{S_2^{\,n}}{S_1^{\,n}}
$$

The $S_1^{\,n}$ terms cancel:

$$
I_2 = k \cdot S_2^{\,n}
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\,I = k \cdot S^{n}\,}
$$

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A trained panel performed magnitude estimation on sucrose solutions. The reference (5 % w/v) was assigned an intensity of 10. At 20 % w/v the mean rating was 28.0. Estimate the exponent $n$ for sweetness.

**Step 1.** Identify the stimuli:
- $S_1 = 5$ % w/v → $I_1 = 10$
- $S_2 = 20$ % w/v → $I_2 = 28.0$

**Step 2.** Use the ratio form of Stevens’ law:

$$
\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \left( \frac{S_2}{S_1} \right)^n
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute:

$$
\frac{28.0}{10} = \left( \frac{20}{5} \right)^n
$$

$$
2.8 = 4^n
$$

**Step 4.** Take natural log of both sides:

$$
\ln(2.8) = n \cdot \ln(4)
$$

**Step 5.** Evaluate numerically:

$$
\ln(2.8) = 1.0296
$$

$$
\ln(4) = 1.3863
$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $n$:

$$
n = \frac{1.0296}{1.3863} = 0.743
$$

**Interpretation:** Because $n < 1$, sweetness shows **compression** — a 4-fold increase in sucrose gives only a 2.8-fold increase in perceived sweetness. This explains why artificial sweeteners can replace sucrose at vastly lower concentrations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of TPA Composite Parameters (Texture Profile Analysis)

In a standard **two-bite compression test** on a texturometer, the force–time curve looks like the schematic below:

- **First compression** (first bite): peak force $F_1$, area under the curve $A_1$.
- **Second compression** (second bite): area under the curve $A_2$.
- **Negative area** between bites (when probe lifts and food sticks to it): $A_3$.
- **First “upstroke” recovery area:** $A_4$.
- **Time of first compression:** $t_1$.
- **Time between bites** (contact end of bite 1 to start of bite 2): effectively $t_2 - t_1$ for the second bite contact; standard definition is $t_2 / t_1$ where $t_2$ is the time at peak 2.

### 3.2.1 Hardness

By definition:

$$
H = F_1
$$

**Units:** Newtons (N). For a typical biscuit, $H \approx 30$–80 N.

### 3.2.2 Cohesiveness

Defined as the ratio of the *work* of the second bite to the *work* of the first bite:

$$
C = \frac{A_2}{A_1}
$$

**Units:** dimensionless, $0 < C \leq 1$. A value of 1 = perfectly cohesive (does not crumble); a value of 0.2 = very crumbly (e.g., wafer).

### 3.2.3 Springiness (Elasticity)

Defined as the ratio of the time the sample spends in the second bite to the first:

$$
S_p = \frac{t_2}{t_1}
$$

**Units:** dimensionless, $0 < S_p \leq 1$. Marshmallow ≈ 0.9; hard candy ≈ 0.3.

### 3.2.4 Adhesiveness

The work done *against* the food when the probe withdraws — i.e., the magnitude of the negative area:

$$
A_d = \vert A_3 \vert
$$

**Units:** N·s (or g·s). Peanut butter ≈ 1.5 N·s.

### 3.2.5 Chewiness

The total *work* required to chew a solid food until it is ready for swallowing. By physical reasoning, work = force × distance. Force is captured by $H$, distance by $S_p$, and the “internal binding” by $C$:

$$
C_h = H \times C \times S_p
$$

**Units:** N (or J if integrated properly). For a chewy caramel, $C_h \approx 20$–60 N.

### 3.2.6 Gummininess

The work required to disintegrate a *semi-solid* (e.g., cheese spread, gummy). No springiness is involved because semi-solids do not spring back; only hardness × cohesiveness is needed:

$$
G = H \times C
$$

**Units:** N. Gummy candy ≈ 8–20 N.

### 3.2.7 Resilience

The ratio of the area under the upstroke of the first bite (instantaneous recovery, $A_4$) to the area under the first bite itself ($A_1$):

$$
R = \frac{A_4}{A_1}
$$

**Units:** dimensionless, $0 < R \leq 1$. Bread crust ≈ 0.3; gelatin ≈ 0.7.

### 3.2.8 Worked TPA Example (KTU Examination Style)

> **Problem:** A panel measured a cheese spread on a texturometer. The following data were recorded:
> - Peak force first bite $F_1 = 12$ N
> - Area first bite $A_1 = 60$ N·s
> - Area second bite $A_2 = 48$ N·s
> - Negative area $A_3 = 5$ N·s
> - Time of first bite $t_1 = 2.0$ s
> - Time of second bite $t_2 = 1.6$ s
> - Upstroke recovery $A_4 = 18$ N·s
>
> Calculate Hardness, Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, Chewiness, and Resilience.

**Step 1 — Hardness:**

$$
H = F_1 = 12 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Cohesiveness:**

$$
C = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{48}{60} = 0.80
$$

**Step 3 — Adhesiveness:**

$$
A_d = \vert A_3 \vert = 5 \text{ N·s}
$$

**Step 4 — Springiness:**

$$
S_p = \frac{t_2}{t_1} = \frac{1.6}{2.0} = 0.80
$$

**Step 5 — Gumminess:**

$$
G = H \times C = 12 \times 0.80 = 9.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Chewiness:**

$$
C_h = H \times C \times S_p = 12 \times 0.80 \times 0.80 = 7.68 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 7 — Resilience:**

$$
R = \frac{A_4}{A_1} = \frac{18}{60} = 0.30
$$

**Result table:**

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Hardness | 12.00 | N |
| Cohesiveness | 0.80 | — |
| Adhesiveness | 5.00 | N·s |
| Springiness | 0.80 | — |
| Gumminess | 9.60 | N |
| Chewiness | 7.68 | N |
| Resilience | 0.30 | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Chewiness < Gumminess here?** Chewiness includes springiness ($S_p = 0.8$); a semi-solid (cheese spread) does not spring back, so the chewy value is lower. For a *solid* like beef jerky, chewiness ≈ gumminess (because $S_p \approx 1$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Test

| Situation in Industry | Recommended Test | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Quick screening of 30 samples by QC staff | Category scaling | Fast, low training |
| Academic study of how sucrose intensity scales with concentration | Ratio scaling (ME) | Yields Stevens’ exponent $n$ |
| Coffee, wine, whiskey quality profile | FPA | Captures temporal/holistic notes |
| Crispness, crunch of biscuits, extrudates | TPA | Bridges sensory & instrument |
| Full product development, benchmarking, shelf-life | QDA | Statistical rigour + completeness |
| Salt-/sugar-reduction program | Ratio scaling | Predicts reformulation levels |
| Perceptual mapping with consumers | QDA + external preference | Industry standard |
