# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q1.** Differentiate between **Category Scaling** and **Ratio Scaling** as descriptive sensory methods.

**Model Answer (key points — write any three for full marks):**

1. **Scale Type:** Category scaling uses a *finite, ordered* set of discrete categories (e.g., 1–9). Ratio scaling uses an *open-ended* continuum in which panelists assign numbers proportional to the perceived intensity.
2. **Data Treatment:** Category data are *ordinal* (often treated as interval). Ratio data are *log-transformed* before statistical analysis because variance is proportional to mean.
3. **Theoretical Basis:** Ratio scaling is grounded in **Stevens’ Power Law**, $I = k S^{n}$, which yields modality-specific exponents.
4. **Use Case:** Category scaling is used for routine product evaluation; ratio scaling is used for psychophysical research (e.g., salt-/sugar-reduction programmes).

---

### **Question 2 (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q2.** List the **mechanical characteristics** of texture in **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)**. Mention the originator of TPA.

**Model Answer:**

- **Originator:** Alina Szczesniak (General Foods Corporation, USA, 1963).
- **Mechanical Characteristics of Texture (any three, 1 mark each):**
  1. **Hardness** — force required to bite through.
  2. **Cohesiveness** — degree to which sample holds together.
  3. **Adhesiveness** — work to pull food from a surface (e.g., palate).
  4. **Springiness** — rate of recovery after deformation.
  5. **Chewiness** — work to chew a solid to swallow.
  6. **Gumminess** — work to chew a semi-solid to swallow.
  7. **Fracturability** — force at which the sample fractures/cracks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> **Q(a) (7 marks):** Explain in detail the **Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA®)** method. Mention its origin, panel size, scale used, attributes covered, and statistical treatment.
>
> **Q(b) (7 marks):** A texturometer test on a bread sample gave the following data. Calculate Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Adhesiveness, Chewiness, Gumminess, and Resilience.
> - $F_1 = 18$ N, $A_1 = 90$ N·s, $A_2 = 63$ N·s, $A_3 = 6$ N·s
> - $t_1 = 2.5$ s, $t_2 = 1.75$ s, $A_4 = 27$ N·s

#### **Model Answer — Q(a)**

**QDA — Origin & Philosophy (2 marks):**
QDA was developed in **1974** at **Tragon Corporation, USA** by **Stone, Sidel, and Oliver**. It is a trademarked descriptive method built to overcome the subjectivity of FPA and the narrow focus of TPA. It provides a *complete sensory fingerprint* of a product.

**Panel Composition (2 marks):**
- **10–12 panelists** (recommended for statistical power).
- Panelists are recruited, screened, and trained for **50–100 hours** over several weeks.
- Each panelist evaluates samples in *individual booths* (ISO 8589 standard) to prevent bias.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. **Lexicon development** — open discussion to generate descriptive terms.
2. **Training with reference standards** — anchors for each end of the scale.
3. **Sample evaluation** — blind-coded, randomised, replicated 8–15 times.
4. **Scale used** — **15-cm unstructured line scale**; the distance from the left anchor is measured and converted to a numerical score (typically 0–15).
5. **Statistical analysis** — ANOVA, MANOVA, Principal Component Analysis (PCA), correlation with consumer data.

**Attributes Covered (1 mark):**
- **Appearance, aroma, flavour, texture, aftertaste** — and even **sound** (e.g., crunch of chips).

#### **Model Answer — Q(b) — Step-by-Step**

**Given:**
$F_1 = 18$ N; $A_1 = 90$ N·s; $A_2 = 63$ N·s; $A_3 = 6$ N·s; $t_1 = 2.5$ s; $t_2 = 1.75$ s; $A_4 = 27$ N·s.

**Step 1 — Hardness:**

$$
H = F_1 = 18 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Cohesiveness:**

$$
C = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{63}{90} = 0.70
$$

**Step 3 — Springiness:**

$$
S_p = \frac{t_2}{t_1} = \frac{1.75}{2.50} = 0.70
$$

**Step 4 — Adhesiveness:**

$$
A_d = \vert A_3 \vert = 6 \text{ N·s}
$$

**Step 5 — Gumminess:**

$$
G = H \times C = 18 \times 0.70 = 12.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Chewiness:**

$$
C_h = H \times C \times S_p = 18 \times 0.70 \times 0.70 = 8.82 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 7 — Resilience:**

$$
R = \frac{A_4}{A_1} = \frac{27}{90} = 0.30
$$

**Result table (final 1 mark):**

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Hardness | 18.00 | N |
| Cohesiveness | 0.70 | — |
| Springiness | 0.70 | — |
| Adhesiveness | 6.00 | N·s |
| Gumminess | 12.60 | N |
| Chewiness | 8.82 | N |
| Resilience | 0.30 | — |

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> **Q(a) (7 marks):** Describe the **Flavour Profile Analysis (FPA)** method. Mention its origin, panel size, attributes covered, scale used, and limitations.
>
> **Q(b) (7 marks):** A magnitude-estimation study on **citric acid solutions** used a 0.05 % reference (assigned an intensity of 10). At 0.40 % concentration the mean rating was 22.0. Using **Stevens’ Power Law**, calculate the exponent $n$ and state whether the modality is *expanding, linear, or compressing*.

#### **Model Answer — Q(a)**

**Origin (1.5 marks):**
- Developed at **Arthur D. Little, Inc. (USA)** in the late **1940s**, formalised by **Cairncross and Sjöström** and updated by **Keane (1992)**.

**Panel Size (1 mark):**
- **4–6 highly trained panelists** — small but highly expert.

**Attributes Covered (1.5 marks):**
- **Aroma, taste, aftertaste, basic mouthfeel** — *flavour only* (no texture or appearance).
- Captures **temporal evolution**: first impression, mid-palate, finish.

**Scale Used (1 mark):**
- **15-point category scale** (0 = none, 15 = extremely strong).

**Procedure (1.5 marks):**
1. Panelists independently evaluate samples in separate booths.
2. They describe **sequence of flavour notes** and assign intensity scores.
3. Panelists convene and *reconcile* scores into a **consensus profile** under a panel leader.
4. Output: a *flavour wheel / spider plot* and a written narrative.

**Limitations (0.5 mark):**
- *Subjective consensus* — not statistically replicated.
- Niche method; superseded in many applications by **QDA**.

#### **Model Answer — Q(b) — Step-by-Step**

**Step 1.** State Stevens’ Power Law in ratio form:

$$
\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \left( \frac{S_2}{S_1} \right)^n
$$

**Step 2.** Identify the stimuli and intensities:
- $S_1 = 0.05$ %, $I_1 = 10$
- $S_2 = 0.40$ %, $I_2 = 22.0$

**Step 3.** Substitute the values:

$$
\frac{22.0}{10} = \left( \frac{0.40}{0.05} \right)^n
$$

$$
2.20 = (8)^n
$$

**Step 4.** Take natural logarithms of both sides:

$$
\ln(2.20) = n \cdot \ln(8)
$$

**Step 5.** Evaluate:

$$
\ln(2.20) = 0.7885
$$

$$
\ln(8) = 2.0794
$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $n$:

$$
n = \frac{0.7885}{2.0794} = 0.379
$$

**Step 7. Interpretation (1 mark):**
- Since $0 < n < 1$ → the modality is **compressing**.
- This matches the known trend: **sourness is highly compressed** ($n \approx 0.4$–0.8); a large 8-fold increase in acid concentration produces only a modest 2.2-fold increase in perceived sourness. In reformulation, this means only a small fraction of acid is needed to maintain perceived sourness.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Do not mix FPA with QDA.** FPA is *consensus-based* (no replication statistics); QDA uses *individual booths, replicated, statistically analysed*. Marks are routinely lost for confusing the two.
> 2. **Do not call TPA only a sensory method.** TPA is the *bridge* between sensory and instrumental texture (texturometer). Always mention both sides.
> 3. **Chewiness is NOT = Gumminess.** Chewiness includes springiness; gumminess does not. Many candidates write $C_h = H \times C$ and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **In Ratio Scaling, take logarithms** before any parametric statistic. Using raw mean ratings is wrong because variance is proportional to the mean.
> 5. **For the 9-point hedonic scale** — recall it is *affective* (liking), not descriptive. Question papers often trap students by saying “9-point scale used in descriptive tests.”
> 6. **Unit of Stevens’ $n$ is dimensionless.** Do not write N or kg.
> 7. **Stevens’ Power Law is $I = k S^{n}$, NOT $I = k + nS$.** A common linear-vs-power confusion costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Descriptive tests** use **trained** panelists and answer “*What?*” and “*How intense?*”.
- ☐ **Category Scaling** = discrete ordered categories (e.g., 1–9, 1–15). Data are *ordinal*; non-parametric or careful parametric statistics.
- ☐ **Ratio Scaling (Magnitude Estimation)** = open-ended, *ratio* data. **Log-transform** before analysis. Grounded in **Stevens’ Power Law** $I = k S^{n}$.
- ☐ **$n$ values** for common modalities: sweetness ≈ 1.0, saltiness ≈ 1.3, sourness ≈ 0.8, bitterness ≈ 0.6, loudness ≈ 0.6.
- ☐ **Flavour Profile Analysis (FPA)** — Arthur D. Little, 1949. Panel = 4–6. Scale = 15-point category. Scope = **flavour only**. Method = **consensus**.
- ☐ **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** — Szczesniak, General Foods, 1963. Panel = 5–10. Scale = 14–15-point category. Scope = **texture only**. Method = **sensory + instrumental** (texturometer).
- ☐ **QDA®** — Stone, Sidel, Oliver, Tragon Corp., 1974. Panel = 10–12. Scale = **15-cm unstructured line** (0–15). Scope = **all** attributes. Method = **statistical (ANOVA, PCA)**.
- ☐ **TPA Formulas:** $H = F_1$; $C = A_2 / A_1$; $S_p = t_2 / t_1$; $A_d = \vert A_3 \vert$; $C_h = H \times C \times S_p$; $G = H \times C$; $R = A_4 / A_1$.
- ☐ **Chewiness applies to solids**; **Gumminess applies to semi-solids**.
- ☐ **For sugar / salt reduction** projects → use **Ratio Scaling (ME)** because it predicts reformulation levels from exponent $n$.
- ☐ **For full product fingerprinting & benchmarking** → use **QDA**.
- ☐ **For texture alone (crunch, chew)** → use **TPA** (and pair with texturometer).
- ☐ **For wine, coffee, whiskey temporal notes** → use **FPA**.
- ☐ **Always use individual booths, randomised design, blind coding, and replicated sessions** — these are the hallmarks of *all* descriptive tests.
- ☐ **Statistical requirement:** QDA demands $\geq 8$ reps for valid ANOVA; FPA does not need replication because it is consensus-based.
- ☐ **ISO standards:** ISO 8589 (booths), ISO 13299 (general guidelines for descriptive analysis).
- ☐ **Remember the trademark:** Only **QDA®** is a registered trademark of Tragon; the others (FPA, TPA, ME) are generic descriptors.
- ☐ **Trigonometric / log pitfalls in ME:** Always work in $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$ — never raw numbers.
- ☐ **Anchor the scales** with reference standards (e.g., citric acid, sucrose, NaCl) at *both* ends.
- ☐ **CV of the panel** should be **< 25 %** per attribute for a *qualified* panel.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 2, Descriptive Sensory Tests**
> *Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme • PEFTT524 • Outcome-Based Education (OBE) • NEP 2020*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensory Tests for Food - Affective: Paired Performance, Ranking, Rating (Hedonic and Food Action Scale)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT524 - SENSORY EVALUATION OF FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensory Tests for Food - Affective: Paired Performance, Ranking, Rating (Hedonic and Food Action Scale)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Affective Sensory Tests** are a category of sensory evaluation methods designed to measure the **degree of liking, preference, or acceptance** of a food product by a consumer/panelist. Unlike discriminative or analytical tests, affective tests answer the question **"How much do you like it?"** rather than **"Is there a difference?"** or **"What is the intensity?"**

The three principal affective tests covered in this module are:

1. **Paired Preference Test (Affective Version)** — A two-sample test where the panelist indicates which of two coded samples is preferred.
2. **Ranking Test (Affective Version)** — A multi-sample test where samples are arranged in order of preference (most to least liked).
3. **Rating Tests** — Tests where the panelist expresses the degree of liking/acceptance on a structured numerical scale. The two most important rating scales are:
   - **Hedonic Rating Scale** (Peryam & Pilgrim, 9-point)
   - **Food Action (Rating) Scale — FACT** (Schutz, 1965)

> [!NOTE]
> **Affective vs. Discriminative Tests — The Cardinal Distinction**
> Affective tests use **untrained or semi-trained consumers** (50–150 panelists) and the data is **subjective and ordinal/preferential**. Discriminative tests use trained panels and produce objective yes/no data. **Do not confuse them in the exam.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into an ice-cream parlor with three new flavors — Mango Chili, Salted Caramel, and Lavender Honey. Before you try them, you might:

- **Taste two and say "I prefer this one"** → that is the **Paired Preference Test**.
- **Taste all three and say "Best → Okay → Worst"** → that is the **Ranking Test**.
- **Taste one and rate it on a scale from "Dislike Extremely" to "Like Extremely"** → that is the **Hedonic Rating**.
- **Taste one and decide how often you would actually eat it in real life** → that is the **Food Action Scale**.

In every case, the measurement is **personal preference (subjective acceptance)**, not chemical analysis or trained-panel discrimination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Affective Tests Matter in Industry (KTU 2024 Industry Connect)**
> Affective tests are the **only sensory tests that directly predict consumer purchase behavior**. New product launches, reformulations, shelf-life decisions, and packaging changes are all validated using these tests before commercial release.

### 1.3 Standard Test Population Metrics

- **Panel size for affective tests:** Typically **50 to 150 consumers** (larger than discriminative tests which use 25–50 trained panelists).
- **Environment:** Central location test (CLT) or home-use test (HUT), under normal lighting and neutral booths.
- **Sample coding:** Random 3-digit codes (e.g., 472, 815) to prevent bias.
- **Test temperature:** **20 ± 2 °C** for most liquid/semi-solid foods; chilled products at **4–7 °C**.
- **Water for palate cleansing:** Served at **room temperature (~25 °C)**.
- **Hedonic scale midpoint:** **5 (Neither like nor dislike)** is treated as a neutral reference point.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Affective Test 1 — Paired Preference Test

**Principle:** Two coded samples (A and B) are presented simultaneously to each panelist. The panelist is asked: *"Which sample do you prefer?"* (forced choice). It is a one-question, one-answer test.

**Procedure (Operational Steps):**
- Select a panel of at least **50 consumers** (minimum 30 for screening).
- Prepare two samples, identically coded with random 3-digit numbers.
- Present in **balanced/randomized order** (half the panel gets A then B; the other half gets B then A) to remove order bias.
- Provide water and plain crackers for palate cleansing between samples.
- Ask: *"Which sample do you prefer?"*
- Tally the number of panelists preferring A (call it `nA`) and B (call it `nB`); the remainder (if any) are "no preference".

**Statistical Analysis — Binomial Test:**
- **Null hypothesis H₀:** There is no preference (P(A) = P(B) = 0.5).
- **Alternative hypothesis H₁:** One sample is preferred (two-tailed) or specifically preferred (one-tailed).
- Compare the observed count to the **binomial probability table** or the minimum number of agreeing responses for significance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum Number of Preferences for Significance (Two-Tailed, α = 0.05)**
> For 30 panelists, at least **20** must prefer the same sample. For 50 panelists, at least **32** must agree. For 100 panelists, at least **61** must agree. These are read from a standard binomial/paired-preference table.

### 2.2 Affective Test 2 — Ranking Test

**Principle:** Three or more (typically 3–6) coded samples are presented, and the panelist is asked to **rank them in order of preference** from "most preferred" to "least preferred". The test is **forced** — ties are not allowed.

**Procedure (Operational Steps):**
- Panel of **30 to 50 consumers** (more for finer discrimination).
- Samples presented in **randomized order** to each panelist (Latin square design).
- Question: *"Please rank the samples from most preferred (1) to least preferred."*
- Record the rank (1, 2, 3 …) assigned to each sample by each panelist.
- Compute the **sum of ranks** (Rank Sum, R) for each sample.

**Statistical Analysis — Friedman's Test (for ranking by panels of equal size):**

$$
\chi^2_r = \frac{12}{n \cdot k \cdot (k+1)} \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{k} R_j^2 \; - \; 3n(k+1)
$$

Where:
- `n` = number of panelists (judges)
- `k` = number of samples ranked
- `R_j` = sum of ranks for sample j
- Degrees of freedom = `k − 1`

The calculated χ²_r is compared with the critical χ² value at α = 0.05. If significant, use **Kramer's multiple comparison table** to identify which specific pairs differ.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rank 1 is the MOST preferred.** The sample with the **LOWEST rank sum** is the most preferred. This is a common confusion point — **lower rank sum = more preferred**.

### 2.3 Affective Test 3 — Hedonic Rating Scale (Peryam & Pilgrim, 1957)

**Principle:** Each panelist rates a single sample on a structured 9-point verbal-numerical scale ranging from extreme dislike to extreme like. The scale was developed at the **U.S. Army Quartermaster Food and Container Institute** and is the most widely used affective scale in the world.

**The 9-Point Hedonic Scale (Verbal Anchors):**

| Numerical Score | Verbal Anchor |
|:---:|:---|
| 9 | Like extremely |
| 8 | Like very much |
| 7 | Like moderately |
| 6 | Like slightly |
| 5 | Neither like nor dislike |
| 4 | Dislike slightly |
| 3 | Dislike moderately |
| 2 | Dislike very much |
| 1 | Dislike extremely |

**Procedure:**
- Panel of **50 to 100 consumers** (untrained, representative of the target market).
- One sample per session (or multiple samples in balanced order).
- Question: *"Using the 9-point scale, please rate how much you like or dislike the sample."*
- Compute the **mean hedonic score**, **standard deviation**, and frequency distribution.

**Interpretation:**
- Mean score **≥ 7** → Strong acceptance (commercially viable).
- Mean score **5–6** → Neutral / mild acceptance (reformulate).
- Mean score **< 4** → Rejection (do not launch).

> [!NOTE]
> **Variants of the Hedonic Scale**
> The 9-point scale can be reduced to a 7-point scale (combining "extremely" and "very much") or expanded to an 11-point scale. The 9-point version has the highest test–retest reliability and is the **KTU-recommended** version.

### 2.4 Affective Test 4 — Food Action (Rating) Scale — FACT (Schutz, 1965)

**Principle:** Developed by **Howard G. Schutz** at the University of California, Davis, the Food Action Scale measures the **likelihood of actual consumption** rather than just the degree of liking. It is more behaviorally predictive than the hedonic scale because it answers: *"Would you really eat this, and how often?"*

**The Food Action Scale (FACT) Anchors:**

| Score | Verbal Anchor |
|:---:|:---|
| 9 | I would eat this every opportunity I had |
| 8 | I would eat this very often |
| 7 | I would frequently eat this |
| 6 | I would like to eat this |
| 5 | I would eat this if available but would not go out of my way |
| 4 | I don't think I would eat this |
| 3 | I would eat this only if there were no other foods available |
| 2 | I would eat this only if forced |
| 1 | (reserved / unused) |

**Procedure:**
- Same panel requirements as the hedonic test.
- Question: *"Indicate the statement that best describes your eating behavior toward this food."*
- Compute mean FACT score; **higher mean = greater likelihood of consumption**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hedonic vs. FACT — The Key Difference**
> The **Hedonic scale** measures *emotional* liking. The **FACT scale** measures *behavioral* intent-to-eat. A product can be liked (high hedonic) but rarely consumed (low FACT) — e.g., a luxurious truffle. Conversely, a product can be rated neutral hedonically but eaten daily (e.g., bread, rice) — high FACT, mid hedonic.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Test | Core Formula | Variables | Threshold / Use |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Paired Preference | Compare observed count to binomial table at α = 0.05 | n, nA, nB | nA (or nB) ≥ table value for significance |
| Ranking (Friedman) | $\chi^2_r = \dfrac{12}{n k (k+1)} \sum R_j^2 - 3n(k+1)$ | n, k, R_j | Compare with χ²_(k−1, 0.05) |
| Hedonic | $\bar{H} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}{n}$ | H_i = individual score | $\bar{H} \geq 7$ = strong acceptance |
| FACT | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{\sum F_i}{n}$ | F_i = individual score | Higher = greater intent to consume |
| Standard Deviation | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | x_i, $\bar{x}$, n | For dispersion in hedonic/FACT scores |
| Standard Error of Mean | $SE = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | s, n | For confidence interval around mean |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in the Food Industry

- **New Product Development (NPD):** Concept screening and final product optimization.
- **Reformulation:** Reducing sugar/sodium/fat while preserving consumer acceptance.
- **Shelf-life studies:** Tracking how hedonic/FACT scores decline over storage days.
- **Competitive benchmarking:** Rating your product vs. a competitor on the same hedonic scale.
- **Menu engineering (HoReCa):** Identifying "stars" (high liking, high profit) vs. "dogs".
- **Quality assurance:** Detecting batch-to-batch consumer-acceptance drift.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Paired Preference Test (Binomial Analysis)

**Problem:** A food company tests two formulations of a mango drink, **Formulation X** and **Formulation Y**, using a paired preference test with **n = 50 untrained consumers**. At the end of the test, **34 panelists preferred Y**, **14 preferred X**, and **2 had no preference**. Test whether there is a statistically significant preference at α = 0.05 (two-tailed).

**Step 1 — State the Hypotheses**

$$
H_0: P_X = P_Y = 0.5 \quad \text{(no preference)}
$$

$$
H_1: P_X \neq P_Y \quad \text{(there is a preference — two-tailed)}
$$

**Step 2 — Choose the Test**
Since each panelist makes a forced binary choice, the data follows a **binomial distribution** with parameters n and p = 0.5 under H₀.

**Step 3 — Determine the Critical Number of Preferences**
From the standard paired-preference / binomial table at α = 0.05 (two-tailed) for n = 50:

$$
n_{critical} = 32
$$

**Step 4 — Compare Observed with Critical**

$$
n_Y^{observed} = 34 \quad ; \quad n_{critical} = 32
$$

Since **34 > 32**, we reject H₀.

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

$$
\boxed{\text{Formulation Y is significantly preferred over Formulation X at } p < 0.05.}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Ranking Test (Friedman's Test)

**Problem:** Four variants of a biscuit (A, B, C, D) are ranked by **n = 6 panelists** for overall preference. The rank sums obtained are:

| Sample | A | B | C | D |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Rank Sum (R_j) | 18 | 14 | 11 | 17 |

Test if there is a significant difference in preference among the four variants at α = 0.05.

**Step 1 — Hypotheses**

$$
H_0: \text{All samples are equally preferred.}
$$

$$
H_1: \text{At least one sample differs in preference.}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Friedman's Formula**

$$
\chi^2_r = \frac{12}{n \cdot k \cdot (k+1)} \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{k} R_j^2 - 3n(k+1)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values**

$$
n = 6, \quad k = 4
$$

$$
\sum R_j^2 = (18)^2 + (14)^2 + (11)^2 + (17)^2
$$

$$
\sum R_j^2 = 324 + 196 + 121 + 289 = 930
$$

$$
\chi^2_r = \frac{12}{6 \times 4 \times 5} \times 930 - 3 \times 6 \times 5
$$

$$
\chi^2_r = \frac{12}{120} \times 930 - 90
$$

$$
\chi^2_r = 0.1 \times 930 - 90
$$

$$
\chi^2_r = 93 - 90
$$

$$
\boxed{\chi^2_r = 3.00}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Critical Value**
Degrees of freedom = k − 1 = 3. From the χ² table, χ²_(3, 0.05) = **7.815**.

Since 3.00 < 7.815, we **fail to reject H₀**.

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

$$
\boxed{\text{There is no statistically significant difference in preference among A, B, C, and D at } p < 0.05.}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Hedonic Rating Test (Mean & Interpretation)

**Problem:** A new flavored yogurt is rated on the 9-point hedonic scale by **n = 60 consumers**. The sum of all hedonic scores is **432**. Compute the mean hedonic score and interpret the result.

**Step 1 — Apply the Mean Formula**

$$
\bar{H} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}{n} = \frac{432}{60} = 7.20
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation**
Mean score 7.20 falls between "Like moderately" (7) and "Like very much" (8). The product is in the **strong acceptance zone**.

$$
\boxed{\bar{H} = 7.20 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Strong consumer acceptance (commercially viable)}.}
$$

**Step 3 — (Extension) Standard Deviation**
Suppose the sum of squared deviations is Σ(H_i − 7.20)² = 168. Then:

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (H_i - \bar{H})^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{168}{59}} = \sqrt{2.847} \approx 1.687
$$

**Step 4 — (Extension) 95% Confidence Interval**

$$
SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{1.687}{\sqrt{60}} = \frac{1.687}{7.746} \approx 0.218
$$

$$
CI_{95\%} = \bar{H} \pm 1.96 \cdot SE = 7.20 \pm (1.96 \times 0.218) = 7.20 \pm 0.43
$$

$$
\boxed{CI_{95\%} = [6.77, \; 7.63]}
$$

The entire confidence interval lies in the acceptance zone, confirming robustness of the result.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — FACT Scale Interpretation

**Problem:** A newly launched breakfast cereal is rated by **n = 80 consumers** on the Food Action Scale. The sum of FACT scores is **512**. Calculate the mean FACT score and interpret the consumer's intent to eat.

**Step 1 — Mean FACT Score**

$$
\bar{F} = \frac{512}{80} = 6.40
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation**
A mean of 6.40 corresponds to the verbal anchor *"I would like to eat this"*. This indicates **positive but moderate** consumption intent.

$$
\boxed{\bar{F} = 6.40 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Moderate positive intent to consume.}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the mean hedonic score for the same cereal were 7.5 (high liking) but the mean FACT were only 5.2 (mild intent), the product team would conclude the cereal is liked emotionally but not integrated into daily eating habits — a typical finding for exotic or premium SKUs.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix of the Four Affective Tests

| Feature | Paired Preference | Ranking | Hedonic Rating | FACT Rating |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Number of samples per session** | 2 | 3–6 (typically 4) | 1 (or multiple coded) | 1 (or multiple coded) |
| **Type of data** | Nominal (binary) | Ordinal | Ordinal (interval-ish) | Ordinal (behavioral) |
| **Statistical test** | Binomial | Friedman + Kramer | Mean ± SD, t-test, ANOVA | Mean ± SD, t-test, ANOVA |
| **Panel size** | 50–100 | 30–50 | 50–150 | 50–150 |
| **What it measures** | Direction of preference | Order of preference | Degree of liking | Behavioral intent to eat |
| **Discrimination power** | Low (only 2 samples) | Medium (multiple) | High (continuous) | High (continuous) |
| **Ease of administration** | Very easy | Easy | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Industry use case** | Quick A vs. B screening | Pre-screen multiple formulations | Final product acceptance | Real-world consumption prediction |
| **Originator / Year** | Classical | Classical | Peryam & Pilgrim, 1957 | Schutz, 1965 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — Affective Test Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Research Objective: Measure consumer liking] --> q1A{Number of samples to compare?}
    q1A -- Two samples --> q2A{Need a yes/no preference decision?}
    q2A -- Yes --> pptA[Paired Preference Test]
    q2A -- No, need degree --> hedA[Hedonic Rating on both]
    q1A -- Three to six samples --> q3A{Need ranked order?}
    q3A -- Yes --> rtA[Ranking Test]
    q3A -- No, need individual scores --> q4A{Measure liking or intent?}
    q4A -- Degree of liking --> hedB[Hedonic 9-point Scale]
    q4A -- Behavioral intent to eat --> factA[FACT Scale by Schutz]
    pptA --> analyA[Binomial test at alpha 0.05]
    rtA --> analyB[Friedman test plus Kramer post hoc]
    hedA --> analyC[Mean, SD, ANOVA]
    hedB --> analyC
    factA --> analyD[Mean FACT score, comparison]
    analyA --> reportA[Interpretation and decision]
    analyB --> reportA
    analyC --> reportA
    analyD --> reportA
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — Paired Preference Test Operational Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sP[Step 1: Define objective and select consumer panel of 50 to 100] --> sP2[Step 2: Prepare two coded samples A and B with random 3-digit numbers]
    sP2 --> sP3[Step 3: Counterbalance presentation order across panelists]
    sP3 --> sP4[Step 4: Serve samples simultaneously with water and palate cleanser]
    sP4 --> sP5[Step 5: Ask forced choice question: Which sample do you prefer]
    sP5 --> sP6[Step 6: Tally responses nA and nB and no preference]
    sP6 --> sP7[Step 7: Compare observed count to binomial critical value at alpha 0.05]
    sP7 --> sP8{Is nA or nB greater than critical value?}
    sP8 -- Yes --> sP9[Reject H0: significant preference exists]
    sP8 -- No --> sP10[Fail to reject H0: no significant preference]
    sP9 --> sP11[Report preferred sample and level of significance]
    sP10 --> sP11
```

### 4.3 Flowchart — Hedonic Rating Test Procedure and Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hS1[Step 1: Recruit 50 to 150 untrained consumer panelists] --> hS2[Step 2: Prepare coded samples with random 3-digit numbers]
    hS2 --> hS3[Step 3: Serve samples one at a time in randomized order]
    hS3 --> hS4[Step 4: Present 9-point Hedonic Scale card to each panelist]
    hS4 --> hS5[Step 5: Panelist rates sample by circling or marking the chosen score]
    hS5 --> hS6[Step 6: Collect score sheets and tabulate data]
    hS6 --> hS7[Step 7: Compute mean Hedonic score and standard deviation]
    hS7 --> hS8[Step 8: Compute 95 percent confidence interval around the mean]
    hS8 --> hS9{Is mean score greater than or equal to 7?}
    hS9 -- Yes --> hS10[Strong acceptance: launch approved]
    hS9 -- No --> hS11{Is mean score between 5 and 6.9?}
    hS11 -- Yes --> hS12[Neutral: reformulate and retest]
    hS11 -- No --> hS13[Rejection: do not launch]
    hS10 --> hS14[Generate sensory report with statistical tables]
    hS12 --> hS14
    hS13 --> hS14
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Relationship Between the Four Affective Tests

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    affective[Affective Sensory Tests] --> binary[Paired Preference]
    affective --> ordinal[Ordering Tests]
    affective --> continuous[Rating Tests]
    ordinal --> rank[Ranking Test]
    continuous --> hedonic[Hedonic 9-point Scale]
    continuous --> fact[FACT Scale by Schutz]
    binary --> statA[Binomial distribution analysis]
    rank --> statB[Friedman test plus Kramer]
    hedonic --> statC[Mean SD and ANOVA]
    fact --> statD[Mean SD and behavioral correlation]
    statA --> decision[Consumer Acceptance Decision]
    statB --> decision
    statC --> decision
    statD --> decision
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define affective sensory tests. List the four major types of affective tests used in food evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Affective sensory tests are a class of sensory evaluation methods that measure the **degree of liking, preference, or acceptance** of a food product by a consumer panel. They are subjective in nature and use untrained or semi-trained panelists (typically 50–150 in number). They differ from discriminative and descriptive tests in that they focus on the consumer's personal response rather than objective sensory differences.

**The four major types of affective tests (1.5 marks):**
1. **Paired Preference Test** — a two-sample forced-choice preference test.
2. **Ranking Test** — multi-sample ordered preference test.
3. **Hedonic Rating Test (Peryam & Pilgrim, 9-point)** — measures degree of liking.
4. **Food Action (Rating) Scale (FACT) by Schutz, 1965** — measures behavioral intent to consume.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between the Hedonic Rating Scale and the Food Action (FACT) Scale. Mention the originator and year of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hedonic Rating Scale | Food Action (FACT) Scale |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **What it measures** | Degree of emotional liking | Behavioral intent to consume |
| **Number of points** | 9 (Dislike extremely to Like extremely) | 9 (frequency/intent-based anchors) |
| **Originator** | Peryam & Pilgrim | Howard G. Schutz |
| **Year** | 1957 | 1965 |
| **Sample anchors** | "Like moderately" / "Dislike slightly" | "I would eat this every opportunity I had" / "I would eat this only if forced" |
| **Industry relevance** | Final product acceptance | Real-world consumption prediction |

The **key difference** is that the Hedonic scale captures *emotional liking* (a feeling), whereas the FACT scale captures *behavioral intent* (an action). A product can be emotionally liked but rarely consumed (high hedonic, low FACT) — e.g., luxury chocolates.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** KTU End-Semester Examinations allow an **internal choice** between two full-length questions. Both options are provided below.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Paired Preference Test** and the **Ranking Test** as affective sensory methods, with their procedures, statistical analyses, and industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food company formulates **three variants of a low-sugar chocolate** (P, Q, R). The variants are ranked by **n = 8 trained consumer panelists** for overall preference. The rank sums obtained are: **R_P = 16, R_Q = 11, R_R = 19**. Apply the **Friedman test** at α = 0.05 and interpret the result. Use χ²_(3, 0.05) = 7.815. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) — Paired Preference and Ranking Tests (7 marks)**

**Paired Preference Test (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A two-sample forced-choice affective test in which each panelist receives two coded samples (A and B) and indicates which is preferred.
- **Panel size:** 50 to 100 consumers.
- **Procedure:** Random 3-digit coding → counterbalanced order → simultaneous presentation → water and crackers provided → forced-choice question → tally responses (nA, nB, no preference).
- **Statistical analysis:** Binomial test under H₀: P(A) = P(B) = 0.5. The observed count of the preferred sample is compared with the critical value from a binomial/paired-preference table (e.g., n = 50 → critical = 32 at α = 0.05 two-tailed).
- **Industrial applications:** Quick A vs. B screening of new formulations, packaging variants, brand comparisons, price-vs-acceptance trade-offs.

**Ranking Test (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A multi-sample affective test in which 3 to 6 coded samples are ordered from "most preferred" (rank 1) to "least preferred" by each panelist. Ties are not permitted.
- **Panel size:** 30 to 50 panelists.
- **Procedure:** Random sample coding → Latin-square randomized presentation → panelist ranks in order → sum of ranks (R_j) computed for each sample.
- **Statistical analysis:** Friedman's test (χ²_r) to detect overall differences; if significant, Kramer's multiple-comparison table is used to identify which pairs differ.
- **Industrial applications:** Pre-screening multiple prototypes before final hedonic testing, cost-effective preliminary comparison of 3–6 formulations.

**(b) — Friedman's Test Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses**

$$
H_0: \text{Variants P, Q, R are equally preferred.}
$$

$$
H_1: \text{At least one variant differs in preference.}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Friedman's Formula**

$$
\chi^2_r = \frac{12}{n \cdot k \cdot (k+1)} \cdot \sum R_j^2 - 3n(k+1)
$$

Here, n = 8 panelists, k = 3 samples, and R_P = 16, R_Q = 11, R_R = 19.

**Step 3 — Compute ΣR_j²**

$$
\sum R_j^2 = (16)^2 + (11)^2 + (19)^2
$$

$$
\sum R_j^2 = 256 + 121 + 361 = 738
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Formula**

$$
\chi^2_r = \frac{12}{8 \times 3 \times 4} \times 738 - 3 \times 8 \times 4
$$

$$
\chi^2_r = \frac{12}{96} \times 738 - 96
$$

$$
\chi^2_r = 0.125 \times 738 - 96
$$

$$
\chi^2_r = 92.25 - 96
$$

$$
\boxed{\chi^2_r = -3.75}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result**

A negative χ²_r is **mathematically impossible** in classical Friedman's analysis and indicates a **computational or data-entry error**. The expected minimum value of χ²_r is 0. The student should re-verify the rank-sum values and recheck the calculation.

*(In an exam setting, if the student obtains a non-negative χ²_r that is less than 7.815, the conclusion would be: fail to reject H₀ — no significant difference among P, Q, R at α = 0.05.)*

**Examiner's Note (1 mark):** Always verify that the sum of all rank sums equals $\frac{n \cdot k \cdot (k+1)}{2}$ — a quick sanity check. Here, 16 + 11 + 19 = 46, and $\frac{8 \times 3 \times 4}{2}$ = 48. There is a 2-unit discrepancy, indicating a **data inconsistency in the problem**. A genuine rank-sum total must satisfy this identity; any deviation implies an erroneous dataset.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Describe the **9-point Hedonic Rating Scale** of Peryam & Pilgrim in detail. Discuss its procedure, statistical analysis, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A new brand of **ready-to-drink (RTD) green tea** is evaluated by **n = 75 consumers** on the 9-point Hedonic scale. The sum of all scores is **517.5**. Compute the **mean hedonic score, standard deviation** (assume Σ(x − $\bar{x}$)² = 192), and the **95% confidence interval**. Comment on the commercial viability of the product. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) — 9-Point Hedonic Rating Scale (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The 9-point Hedonic Rating Scale was developed by **David P. Peryam** and **F. J. Pilgrim** in **1957** at the **U.S. Army Quartermaster Food and Container Institute** to measure consumer food acceptance during World War II ration development. It is the most widely used affective scale globally.

**The Scale (1.5 marks):** The scale uses both **verbal anchors** and a **numerical score (1 to 9)**:

| Score | Anchor |
|:---:|:---|
| 9 | Like extremely |
| 8 | Like very much |
| 7 | Like moderately |
| 6 | Like slightly |
| 5 | Neither like nor dislike |
| 4 | Dislike slightly |
| 3 | Dislike moderately |
| 2 | Dislike very much |
| 1 | Dislike extremely |

**Procedure (1.5 marks):**
- Recruit **50 to 150 untrained consumer panelists** representative of the target market.
- Code samples with random 3-digit numbers to eliminate bias.
- Serve samples one at a time (or in balanced order if multiple).
- Present the hedonic scale card; panelist marks the appropriate score.
- Collect, tabulate, and compute mean, SD, and confidence interval.

**Statistical Analysis (1 mark):** Compute mean ($\bar{H}$), standard deviation (s), and standard error. For comparing two products, use **t-test**; for three or more, use **ANOVA** followed by post-hoc tests (Tukey HSD).

**Advantages (1 mark):** Quick, intuitive, high test–retest reliability, easy statistical analysis, large industry database for benchmarking.

**Limitations (1 mark):** Subject to central-tendency bias (panelists avoid extremes), may not predict actual purchase behavior, requires literate panelists, cultural variations in scale interpretation.

**(b) — Hedonic Score Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Mean Hedonic Score**

$$
\bar{H} = \frac{\sum H_i}{n} = \frac{517.5}{75} = 6.90
$$

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation**

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{192}{74}}
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{2.5946} \approx 1.611
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Error of the Mean**

$$
SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{1.611}{\sqrt{75}} = \frac{1.611}{8.660} \approx 0.186
$$

**Step 4 — 95% Confidence Interval**

$$
CI_{95\%} = \bar{H} \pm 1.96 \times SE = 6.90 \pm (1.96 \times 0.186)
$$

$$
CI_{95\%} = 6.90 \pm 0.365
$$

$$
\boxed{CI_{95\%} = [6.54, \; 7.26]}
$$

**Step 5 — Commercial Viability Interpretation (1 mark):**
- The mean hedonic score of **6.90** lies between "Like slightly" (6) and "Like moderately" (7).
- The lower bound of the 95% CI (6.54) is comfortably above the neutral midpoint of 5, confirming statistically significant positive acceptance.
- However, the upper bound (7.26) does not reach the "strong acceptance" threshold of **≥ 7** on average.
- **Conclusion:** The RTD green tea is **moderately accepted** by consumers. It is **commercially viable but not strongly preferred**. A reformulation (e.g., adjusting sugar level, adding lemon) is recommended to push the mean above 7 before a full market launch.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Hedonic and FACT scales** (loss: 1–2 marks per question). Remember: Hedonic = emotional liking; FACT = behavioral intent.
> 2. **Forgetting to state H₀ and H₁** in ranking/preference test answers. KTU examiners allocate at least 1 mark explicitly for hypothesis framing.
> 3. **Wrong sign of χ²_r**: A negative value means a calculation error — never write it off as "approximately zero".
> 4. **Misinterpreting rank sums**: Rank 1 is *most* preferred → *lowest* rank sum is best. Many students write the opposite.
> 5. **Skipping the interpretation step** in the worked numerical problem. Always end with a **boxed conclusion** in plain English.
> 6. **Using `±` symbol inside markdown tables** — the markdown table parser breaks. Use `\pm` in LaTeX, not `| ± |`.
> 7. **Omitting panel size** when describing a test. Always mention the typical number of consumers (50, 100, etc.).
> 8. **Not citing the originator/year** of the Hedonic (Peryam & Pilgrim, 1957) and FACT (Schutz, 1965) scales — these are frequently asked for 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Affective tests** measure liking/preference/acceptance — *not* differences.
- [x] **Three core affective tests:** Paired Preference, Ranking, Rating (Hedonic + FACT).
- [x] **Panel size for affective tests:** typically **50 to 150 untrained consumers**.
- [x] **Sample coding:** always random 3-digit numbers to remove bias.
- [x] **Paired Preference** uses a **binomial test**; critical value at α = 0.05 (e.g., n = 50 → 32).
- [x] **Ranking Test** uses **Friedman's χ²_r statistic** + **Kramer's post-hoc table**.
- [x] **Hedonic Scale** = 9 points, originator: **Peryam & Pilgrim, 1957**.
- [x] **FACT Scale** = 9 points, originator: **Howard G. Schutz, 1965**.
- [x] **Mean hedonic ≥ 7** = strong acceptance; **< 4** = rejection.
- [x] **Hedonic vs FACT:** Hedonic = emotional liking; FACT = real-life eating intent.
- [x] **Rank 1 = most preferred**; **lowest rank sum = best sample**.
- [x] **Confidence interval** of the mean: $\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \times SE$ at 95%.
- [x] **KTU examiner expects:** H₀, H₁, formula, substitution, calculation, boxed conclusion, and plain-English interpretation — in that exact order.
- [x] **Industrial relevance:** These tests predict **consumer purchase behavior** — they are the **bridge between sensory science and marketing**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subjective Measurements: Physiological Aspects, Psychological Aspects, Mechanical Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT524 - SENSORY EVALUATION OF FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Subjective Measurements: Physiological Aspects, Psychological Aspects, Mechanical Aspects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Subjective Measurements — Formal Definition

In sensory evaluation of food products, **Subjective Measurements** refer to the analytical techniques that depend entirely on the **human panelist as the measuring instrument** to record, interpret, and quantify sensory responses to food stimuli (taste, smell, texture, appearance, and sound). Unlike objective/instrumental measurements, subjective measurements carry inherent variability originating from the human body's biology (physiology), the mind's interpretation (psychology), and the physical conditions under which the test is conducted (mechanics).

The three pillars of subjective measurements are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Triad of Subjective Measurement in Sensory Science**
> 1. **Physiological Aspects** — *Can the panelist detect the stimulus?* (Body & Nervous System)
> 2. **Psychological Aspects** — *How does the panelist perceive and respond?* (Mind & Cognition)
> 3. **Mechanical Aspects** — *Under what controlled conditions is the test conducted?* (Environment & Method)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Lens Camera" Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **three-lens camera** to photograph a dish of biryani:

- **Lens 1 (Physiology)** is the *sensor chip* — it determines whether light (the stimulus) is bright enough to register at all, and at what intensity it saturates. This is your body's receptors and nervous system.
- **Lens 2 (Psychology)** is the *image processor* — it adjusts brightness, contrast, applies filters, and labels the photo as "good" or "bad" based on memory and mood. This is your brain's interpretation.
- **Lens 3 (Mechanics)** is the *tripod, lighting setup, and ISO setting* — the standardized conditions ensuring every photo is taken fairly. This is the test environment.

Just as a bad lens 3 (dim room) ruins even the best sensor and processor, a poorly designed test environment can corrupt even perfect panelists. **All three lenses must be calibrated together for a clear "sensory photograph" of the food.**

---

## 1.3 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 – Subjective Measurements (Syllabus Excerpt, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> - Physiological aspects — sensory thresholds (detection, recognition, difference, terminal), adaptation, fatigue, sensory receptor physiology.
> - Psychological aspects — psychological errors (expectation, stimulus, halo, order, contrast), scaling methods (category, magnitude, ratio, hedonic, JAR), Signal Detection Theory.
> - Mechanical aspects — testing environment (booth design, lighting colour code: **red, green, yellow, white**, temperature, ventilation), sample preparation, presentation order, randomization, use of sensory software.

### 1.3.1 Key Physiological Constants to Memorize

| Constant / Threshold | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Threshold (DT) | $C_{DT}$ | ~1–10 ppm (varies by compound) | Lowest concentration at which stimulus is detected |
| Recognition Threshold (RT) | $C_{RT}$ | ~2–5× DT | Concentration at which stimulus is **identified** |
| Difference Threshold / JND | $\Delta I$ | ~5–20% of stimulus intensity | Just Noticeable Difference |
| Weber's Fraction | $k$ | 0.05–0.20 (for taste/odor) | $\Delta I / I$ = constant |
| Stevens' Exponent (taste) | $n$ | 0.3–1.7 (sweet=0.8, sour=1.0, salty=1.3, bitter=0.6) | Perceived intensity vs concentration |
| Reaction Time (taste) | $RT$ | 0.2–2.0 s | Time from stimulus contact to neural response |

---

## 1.4 Why Subjective Measurements Matter in Food Science

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Axiom of Sensory Science**
> *No instrument can ever fully replace the human sensory system, because food acceptability is ultimately a HUMAN EXPERIENCE.* Subjective measurements are the **gold standard** for:
> - Product development (optimizing flavor balance)
> - Quality control (consumer acceptance)
> - Shelf-life studies (sensory changes over time)
> - Market research (preference mapping)
> - Regulatory and labeling claims ("tastes like real chocolate")
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Physiological Aspects of Subjective Measurement

Physiology deals with the **biological machinery** that converts a chemical/physical stimulus into a neural signal. The human body contains specialized receptors for each modality.

### 2.1.1 Sensory Receptors & Transduction Pathways

| Modality | Receptor Type | Location | Transduction Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Taste (Gustation) | Taste buds (Taste Receptor Cells — TRCs) | Tongue, soft palate, epiglottis | G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) detect sweet, bitter, umami; ion channels detect salt, sour |
| Smell (Olfaction) | Olfactory Receptor Neurons (ORNs) | Olfactory epithelium (~10 cm²) | Odorant binding to ~400 types of ORNs → G-protein cascade |
| Sight (Vision) | Rods & Cones | Retina | Photon absorption → photopigment bleaching → neural impulse |
| Touch/Texture (Somesthesis) | Meissner, Pacinian, Ruffini, Merkel discs | Skin, oral mucosa | Mechanical deformation → ion channel opening |
| Sound (Audition) | Hair cells (inner/outer) | Cochlea (Organ of Corti) | Basilar membrane vibration → hair cell deflection |

### 2.1.2 The Four Classical Thresholds (KTU High-Priority)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Hierarchy (Lowest → Highest Concentration)**
> 1. **Detection Threshold (DT)** — *"I sense SOMETHING is there, but I don't know what it is."* Lowest concentration at which a stimulus produces a sensation.
> 2. **Recognition Threshold (RT)** — *"I can identify what this is (sweet, sour, etc.)."*
> 3. **Difference Threshold (DL / JND — Just Noticeable Difference)** — *"This sample is different from that one."* Smallest detectable change in stimulus intensity.
> 4. **Terminal Threshold (TT)** — *"This is unbearably strong."* Highest concentration the panelist can tolerate.

### 2.1.3 Sensory Adaptation & Fatigue

- **Adaptation** — Continuous exposure to a stimulus → *decreased* sensitivity. Example: walking into a bakery — the smell feels strong at first, then fades.
- **Cross-Adaptation** — Exposure to one odorant reduces sensitivity to a *related* odorant.
- **Sensory Fatigue** — Prolonged stimulation that *temporarily* elevates the threshold (e.g., tasting bitter compounds for hours dulls bitter detection).
- **Potentiation** — One stimulus *increases* sensitivity to another (sucrose enhances vanilla perception).

### 2.1.4 Factors Affecting Physiological Response

- **Age** — Taste/smell acuity peaks in 20s–40s, declines after 60.
- **Hunger state** — Hypoglycemia elevates sweet/salt sensitivity.
- **Health** — Colds, allergies, medications, pregnancy alter taste/smell.
- **Genetics** — ~25% of people are "taste blind" to PTC/PROP bitterness (non-tasters, medium tasters, super-tasters).
- **Gender** — Women generally outperform men in olfactory identification.

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Psychological Aspects of Subjective Measurement

Psychology governs **how the mind interprets and reports** the neural signal. Even with identical physiology, two people can perceive the same stimulus very differently.

### 2.2.1 Sensation vs. Perception

- **Sensation** — Raw neural response to stimulus (physiological).
- **Perception** — Brain's *interpretation* of sensation, influenced by memory, expectation, culture, mood (psychological).

> **Example:** Eating *thayyir sadham* (curd rice). Sensation is "sour + creamy + cool." Perception is "comfort food, reminds me of home, summer meal."

### 2.2.2 Psychological Errors in Sensory Testing (KTU Hot Topic)

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Error Zoo" — Every KTU examiner tests these.**

| Error | Definition | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Expectation Error** | Panelist's preconception alters their response (e.g., knowing a sample is "organic" → rates it better) | Blind coding, balanced information |
| **Stimulus Error** | Panelist's response is influenced by non-test attributes (e.g., commenting on color when asked about taste) | Proper orientation & controlled environment |
| **Halo Effect** | One strong attribute biases rating of all other attributes (lovely aroma → rates texture as excellent too) | Train panelists, use structured scales |
| **Order (Position) Effect** | Samples evaluated differently based on presentation sequence (first/last bias) | Randomization, balanced design (Williams' Latin Square) |
| **Contrast Effect** | A weak sample makes the next one seem stronger, or vice versa | Use reference controls, blind presentation |
| **Central Tendency Error** | Avoidance of extreme scale ends | Use scales with adequate gradation |
| **Acquiescence Bias** | Tendency to agree regardless of true perception | Reverse-word some attributes, use forced-choice |
| **Mundane Realism Error** | Lab setting reduces panelist engagement | Realistic serving conditions |

### 2.2.3 Scaling Methods (Most Asked in KTU)

1. **Category Scaling** — Discrete boxes (e.g., 1–9 point). Quick, easy, but interval assumption is weak.
2. **Visual Analogue Scale (VAS)** — 100 mm line; mark anywhere. Continuous data.
3. **Magnitude Estimation** — Panelist assigns a number proportional to perceived intensity. Ratio data.
4. **Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale** — 5- or 7-point bipolar ("much too little" → "much too much"). Used for optimization.
5. **Hedonic Scale** — 9-point (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely). Used for **preference/acceptance**.
6. **Forced-Choice / Paired-Preference** — Pick the one you prefer. Avoids scaling biases.

### 2.2.4 Signal Detection Theory (SDT)

SDT separates the panelist's **sensitivity** (d′) from their **response bias** (β).

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Mechanical Aspects of Subjective Measurement

The "hardware" of sensory testing — the **controlled environment and standardized procedures** that ensure every panelist experiences the stimulus under identical conditions.

### 2.3.1 Testing Environment Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight — Booth & Facility Design**
> - **Separate testing booths** (≥ 1 m apart), neutral colour (grey/beige).
> - **Lighting** colour-coded by sensory test type:

| Light Colour | Wavelength | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Red** | ~700 nm | Masks colour differences; used when only taste/smell/matter is being tested |
| **Green** | ~550 nm | Highlights colour differences in vegetables (chlorophyll-based) |
| **Yellow** | ~580 nm | Hides brown/yellow tints; useful for certain batters |
| **White** | Full spectrum | Standard reference; mimics daylight (D65) |

- **Temperature** — Controlled at **20–22 °C** (room) and serving temperature per product.
- **Ventilation** — Negative pressure, charcoal-filtered air, ≥ 10 air changes/hour.
- **Noise** — < 40 dB; no distracting odours.

### 2.3.2 Sample Preparation & Presentation

- **Sample size** — Identical volume/weight per panelist (typically 30–50 mL liquid, 5–10 g solid).
- **Serving temperature** — Standardized (cold drinks at **10 °C**, hot beverages at **60 °C**, etc.).
- **Container** — Odour-neutral, coded with **3-digit random numbers** (e.g., 487, 219).
- **Order of presentation** — Randomized using **Williams' Latin Square** (for n ≤ 12) or **complete block design**.
- **Rinse between samples** — Distilled water at room temperature + unsalted crackers/bread to cleanse palate.
- **Inter-stimulus interval** — 30 s to 5 min depending on product.

### 2.3.3 Sensory Software (Modern Tool)

- **Compusense® Five** (Canada)
- **Sensory Information Management Systems (SIMS™)**
- **Fizz (Biosystèmes)**
- **RedJade**
- **EyeQuestion**

These automate sample coding, data capture (digital tablets), randomization, and statistical analysis.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THESE — They appear in nearly every KTU Module 3 question.**

| # | Law / Equation | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weber's Law** | $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k$ | JND is a constant fraction of original stimulus |
| 2 | **Fechner's Law** | $S = k \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Sensation grows logarithmically with stimulus |
| 3 | **Stevens' Power Law** | $S = k \cdot I^{n}$ | Sensation grows as a power function of stimulus |
| 4 | **JND (count)** | $N_{JND} = \dfrac{\log(I/I_0)}{\log(1+k)}$ | Number of JNDs between two intensities |
| 5 | **SDT Sensitivity (d′)** | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | $z$ = z-score of Hit rate and False-alarm rate |
| 6 | **SDT Bias (β)** | $\beta = \dfrac{y(H)}{y(F)}$ | Ratio of likelihoods at criterion |
| 7 | **Hit Rate (H)** | $H = \dfrac{\text{Hits}}{\text{Hits + Misses}}$ | Correct detection of signal |
| 8 | **False Alarm (F)** | $F = \dfrac{\text{FA}}{\text{FA + CR}}$ | Incorrect "yes" when no signal |
| 9 | **% Discrimination (Triangle Test)** | $P_c = \dfrac{1}{3} + \dfrac{2}{3}d'$ | Probability of correct identification in triangle test |
| 10 | **Williams' Latin Square Order** | $n \times n$ matrix, each sample appears once per row & column | Counter-balances 1st-order carry-over effects |
| 11 | **Hedonic Mean** | $\bar{H} = \dfrac{\sum H_i}{n}$ | Average liking score across panel |
| 12 | **Penalty Analysis (JAR)** | Drop in liking outside JAR zone = **penalty** | Identifies which attributes are "off-target" |

**Units Convention:** All threshold concentrations in **ppm, ppb, or % w/v**; intensities in **SI units or arbitrary sensory units (a.u.)**; reaction time in **seconds (s)**.

### 2.4.1 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Weber's/Fechner's/Stevens' Laws** are used in **product reformulation** to determine how much sucrose reduction is needed to remain "indistinguishable" to consumers.
- **SDT (d′)** is used in **discrimination testing** to separate true sensory ability from a panelist's guessing tendency.
- **JAR Penalty Analysis** is used in **commercial product optimization** to identify which ingredient needs adjustment.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Weber's Law from First Principles

**Statement:** The just noticeable difference ($\Delta I$) in stimulus intensity is a constant fraction ($k$) of the original stimulus intensity ($I$).

### Step 1 — Define the JND

Let $I$ be the reference stimulus intensity and $\Delta I$ the smallest change the panelist can reliably detect. Define the ratio:

$$
r = \frac{\Delta I}{I}
$$

**Logic:** A candle flame in a dark room is easily detectable ($\Delta I$ small), but the same candle in sunlight is not. Hence $\Delta I$ must be proportional to $I$.

### Step 2 — Empirically Observe the Ratio

Experimental data show that for a given modality (e.g., taste), $r$ is approximately constant across a wide range of $I$:

$$
\frac{\Delta I}{I} = k \quad \text{(Weber's constant)}
$$

**Logic:** The fractional increment required for detection does not depend strongly on the absolute level of stimulation.

### Step 3 — Solve for JND

$$
\Delta I = k \cdot I
$$

**Logic:** Doubling the concentration means doubling the increment required for a noticeable change.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (Sugar in Tea)

Suppose sugar concentration $I = 10\% \text{ w/v}$ and Weber's constant for sweetness $k = 0.08$.

$$
\Delta I = 0.08 \times 10 = 0.8\% \text{ w/v}
$$

Therefore, the panelist will not notice a change unless sugar is increased from 10% to **10.8%** (or decreased to **9.2%**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Stevens' Power Law

**Statement:** Perceived magnitude $S$ is a power function of stimulus intensity $I$.

### Step 1 — Propose a Power Function

$$
S = k \cdot I^{n}
$$

**Logic:** Magnitude estimation experiments (assigning numbers proportional to sensation) yield straight lines on log-log plots, the signature of a power law.

### Step 2 — Linearize via Logarithm

Take the natural log of both sides:

$$
\ln S = \ln k + n \cdot \ln I
$$

**Logic:** This is of the form $Y = a + bX$, a straight line. Plotting $\ln S$ vs. $\ln I$ gives slope $n$ and intercept $\ln k$.

### Step 3 — Determine n for Each Modality (Empirical)

| Modality | Stevens' Exponent $n$ | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Sweetness | 0.8 | Compressed — increases slower than concentration |
| Saltiness | 1.3 | Expanded — increases faster than concentration |
| Sourness | 1.0 | Linear |
| Bitterness | 0.6 | Highly compressed |
| Heaviness (weight) | 1.45 | Highly expanded |
| Brightness (light) | 0.33 | Highly compressed |

### Step 4 — Worked Example: Saltiness

If $I = 0.5\% \text{ NaCl}$, $k = 1.2$, $n = 1.3$:

$$
S = 1.2 \times (0.5)^{1.3}
$$

Compute $(0.5)^{1.3}$:

$$
(0.5)^{1.3} = e^{1.3 \cdot \ln(0.5)} = e^{1.3 \times (-0.6931)} = e^{-0.9010} = 0.4066
$$

Therefore:

$$
S = 1.2 \times 0.4066 = 0.488 \text{ a.u. (sensory units)}
$$

**Interpretation:** A salt concentration of 0.5% yields a perceived saltiness of 0.49 arbitrary units. To double perceived saltiness (S = 0.98), the required concentration would be:

$$
I = \left(\frac{S}{k}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{0.98}{1.2}\right)^{1/1.3} = (0.8167)^{0.7692} = 0.856\% \text{ NaCl}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Signal Detection Theory Parameters

**Setup:** Panelist is presented with a stimulus (signal) or blank (noise). They respond "Yes" (signal present) or "No" (signal absent). The outcomes are:

| | Signal Present | Signal Absent |
|---|---|---|
| Panelist says "Yes" | **Hit (H)** | **False Alarm (FA)** |
| Panelist says "No" | **Miss (M)** | **Correct Rejection (CR)** |

### Step 1 — Compute Hit and False-Alarm Rates

$$
H = \frac{\text{Hits}}{\text{Hits + Misses}}
$$

$$
F = \frac{\text{False Alarms}}{\text{False Alarms + Correct Rejections}}
$$

**Logic:** Both are probabilities (range 0 to 1).

### Step 2 — Convert to Z-scores

Let $z(p)$ denote the inverse standard-normal CDF evaluated at $p$. Then:

$$
d' = z(H) - z(F)
$$

**Logic:** $d'$ is the distance (in standard-deviation units) between the signal and noise distributions on the internal decision axis. Higher $d'$ = better discrimination.

### Step 3 — Compute Response Bias (β)

The likelihood functions of signal and noise at the criterion $c$ are:

$$
\beta = \frac{y(c \mid \text{noise})}{y(c \mid \text{signal})}
$$

**Logic:** $\beta = 1$ = neutral; $\beta < 1$ = "loose" criterion (says "yes" too often); $\beta > 1$ = "strict" criterion.

### Step 4 — Worked Example (Triangle Test)

A panelist participates in **30 triangle tests**. They correctly identify the odd sample in **21** trials. Of the 9 missed, follow-up questioning shows 4 were "guesses."

Compute Hit rate and FA rate (estimated):

$$
H = \frac{21}{30} = 0.700
$$

Assume FA rate $F = 0.05$ (low).

Compute $z$-scores:

$$
z(0.700) = 0.524
$$

$$
z(0.05) = -1.645
$$

Compute $d'$:

$$
d' = 0.524 - (-1.645) = 2.169
$$

**Interpretation:** $d' = 2.17$ is considered **excellent discrimination ability** (any $d' > 1.5$ is good).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Pillars

| Aspect | Physiological | Psychological | Mechanical |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Body / Nervous system | Mind / Cognition | Environment / Procedure |
| **Unit of analysis** | Receptor, neuron, threshold | Scale, bias, error | Booth, light, temperature |
| **Source of variability** | Age, health, genetics | Mood, expectation, memory | Inconsistent procedure |
| **Key tool** | Threshold tests, reaction time | Hedonic scales, SDT | Booths, randomization |
| **Controllable?** | Partially (panelist screening) | Partially (training, blinding) | Fully (engineering control) |
| **KTU exam focus** | Threshold definitions, Stevens' Law | Error types, scaling methods | Light colours, Latin Square |
| **Real-world control** | Health screening, training | Blind labeling, motivation | ISO 8589 standards |

### 3.4.1 Interaction Equation — Subjective Response is a Function of All Three

$$
R_{subjective} = f\left(P_{physio}, P_{psych}, P_{mech}\right) + \varepsilon
$$

Where:
- $P_{physio}$ = physiological state (sensitivity)
- $P_{psych}$ = psychological state (bias, perception)
- $P_{mech}$ = mechanical setup (environment)
- $\varepsilon$ = random error

**Implication:** KTU problems often ask to **diagnose which pillar is the source of error** in a given scenario. Always check physiological factors first (hunger, fatigue), then psychological (expectation, halo), then mechanical (lighting, temperature).

---

## 3.5 Penalty Analysis (JAR Scale) — Step-by-Step

JAR (Just-About-Right) is a 5- or 7-point bipolar scale used to determine whether an attribute is at the optimal level. A **penalty analysis** quantifies how much consumer liking drops when a sample is judged "too little" or "too much" of an attribute.

### Step 1 — Collect JAR and Liking Data

For each panelist, record JAR rating (1 = much too little, 3 = JAR, 5 = much too much) and overall liking (9-pt hedonic).

### Step 2 — Bin Samples

Group samples into three categories: "Too Little," "JAR," "Too Much."

### Step 3 — Compute Mean Liking in Each Group

$$
\bar{L}_{TooLow} = \text{mean liking in "Too Little" group}
$$

$$
\bar{L}_{JAR} = \text{mean liking in "Just-About-Right" group}
$$

$$
\bar{L}_{TooMuch} = \text{mean liking in "Too Much" group}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Penalty

$$
Penalty_{Low} = \bar{L}_{JAR} - \bar{L}_{TooLow}
$$

$$
Penalty_{High} = \bar{L}_{JAR} - \bar{L}_{TooMuch}
$$

### Step 5 — Decide Action

If $Penalty > 0.5$ (typically), reformulate in that direction.

### Worked Example

| Group | n | Mean Liking (9-pt) |
|---|---|---|
| Too little sweetness | 25 | 5.2 |
| JAR sweetness | 60 | 7.4 |
| Too much sweetness | 15 | 6.5 |

$$
Penalty_{Low} = 7.4 - 5.2 = 2.2
$$

$$
Penalty_{High} = 7.4 - 6.5 = 0.9
$$

**Decision:** The reformulation should **increase sweetness** (penalty 2.2 > 0.9). Add ~5–8% more sugar.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – April 2024]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)**
Differentiate between **Detection Threshold** and **Recognition Threshold** with a suitable food-based example.

**Model Answer:**

The **Detection Threshold (DT)** is the lowest concentration of a stimulus at which a sensory response is elicited *without* identification of the stimulus quality. The **Recognition Threshold (RT)** is the concentration at which the panelist can both detect the stimulus **and** correctly identify its nature (sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami).

*Food Example:* In a sucrose solution, DT may be at **0.2% w/v** (panelist says "I sense something"), while RT is at **0.4% w/v** (panelist says "This is sweet"). RT is always ≥ DT (typically 2–5× higher).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "detection" with "recognition." Always clarify that DT ≠ identification, while RT = detection + identification.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)**
Explain the **Halo Effect** in sensory evaluation. How can it be controlled?

**Model Answer:**

The **Halo Effect** is a psychological error where a panelist's overall positive (or negative) impression of one attribute *spills over* and biases their rating of other, unrelated attributes. For example, if a sample has a beautiful appearance and pleasant aroma, the panelist may also rate its taste and texture more favourably than they truly are.

**Control Measures:**
- Use **trained panelists** who can independently rate each attribute.
- Use **structured, attribute-specific scales** (not global liking).
- Conduct tests **blindly** (no extraneous information).
- Use **monadic sequential presentation** rather than comparative side-by-side.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Don't confuse **Halo Effect** with **Order Effect**. Halo = cross-attribute bias; Order = cross-sample position bias.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**Q. (a) (7 Marks)** Define **Weber's Law** and **Stevens' Power Law**. Explain with an example how each is used in food product reformulation.

**Model Answer:**

**Weber's Law (1834, E. H. Weber):** The just noticeable difference (JND, $\Delta I$) in a stimulus is a constant fraction ($k$) of the original stimulus intensity ($I$):

$$
\frac{\Delta I}{I} = k
$$

**Food Reformulation Example:** For sweetness, $k \approx 0.08$. If a chocolate bar has 40% sugar, then:

$$
\Delta I = 0.08 \times 40 = 3.2\%
$$

The panelist cannot tell the difference between a 40% and a 43.2% sugar bar. This gives the reformulator a **reformulation window** of 3.2% (i.e., sugar can be reduced by up to ~3.2% without consumers noticing).

---

**Stevens' Power Law (1957, S. S. Stevens):** The perceived magnitude ($S$) is a power function of stimulus intensity ($I$):

$$
S = k \cdot I^{n}
$$

**Food Reformulation Example:** For saltiness, $n = 1.3$ (expanded). To halve perceived saltiness (from 1.0 to 0.5 sensory units, with $k = 1.2$):

$$
I = \left(\frac{S}{k}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{0.5}{1.2}\right)^{1/1.3} = (0.4167)^{0.7692} = 0.512\% \text{ NaCl}
$$

So instead of halving 1.0% → 0.5%, the reformulator only needs to reduce to 0.51% to halve perceived saltiness. This is the "magic" of salt reduction strategies in industry (FSSAI, WHO initiatives).

---

**Q. (b) (7 Marks)** Describe the **mechanical aspects** of sensory evaluation. What is the significance of the standard colour coding of lighting in sensory booths?

**Model Answer:**

The **mechanical aspects** refer to the standardized physical environment and procedural controls that ensure every panelist perceives the stimulus under identical conditions. They include:

1. **Booth Design** — Individual booths with neutral grey/beige walls, separated by ≥ 1 m, to prevent panelist interaction and visual bias.
2. **Climate Control** — Temperature 20–22 °C, relative humidity 45–55%, and negative-pressure ventilation with ≥ 10 air changes/hour and activated-carbon filtration to remove odours.
3. **Lighting** — Controlled, colour-coded fluorescent/LED illumination (see below).
4. **Sample Preparation** — Standardized recipe, temperature, volume (e.g., 30 mL liquid at 20 °C), and serving vessels (odour-neutral).
5. **Sample Coding** — Three-digit random numbers (e.g., 587, 219) to maintain blinding.
6. **Order of Presentation** — Randomized using **Williams' Latin Square** to counterbalance first-order carry-over effects.
7. **Palate Cleansers** — Distilled water and unsalted crackers between samples; inter-stimulus interval of 30 s – 5 min.
8. **Sensory Software** — Compusense, SIMS, or Fizz for digital data capture and analysis.

**Significance of Standard Lighting Colours:**

| Light Colour | Wavelength | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Red** | ~700 nm | **Masks all colour differences** — used in tests where colour is *not* an attribute of interest (taste, aroma, texture-focused tests). |
| **Green** | ~550 nm | **Enhances chlorophyll-based green differences** — used for vegetables, herbs. |
| **Yellow** | ~580 nm | **Masks brown/yellow tones** — hides Maillard browning in baked goods. |
| **White (D65)** | Full spectrum | **Reference lighting** simulating midday daylight — used when visual appearance *is* an attribute. |

**Significance:** Standard lighting eliminates visual bias, ensures reproducibility across labs (ISO 8589, ASTM E1871), and allows the same product to be evaluated consistently for the *intended* attribute. For example, testing the sweetness of a beverage should not be confounded by a panelist's visual reaction to its colour, hence red light is used.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**Q. (a) (7 Marks)** Explain the **psychological aspects** of sensory evaluation. Discuss in detail the different **scaling methods** used in sensory testing.

**Model Answer:**

**Psychological Aspects** deal with the mental processes — perception, interpretation, expectation, memory, and bias — that occur *after* the physiological transduction of a stimulus. The mind does not passively record sensations; it actively interprets them based on context, prior experience, and emotion.

**Key Psychological Phenomena:**

1. **Sensation vs. Perception:** Sensation is the raw neural response; perception is the *meaning* the brain assigns (e.g., "This tastes like my grandmother's sambar").
2. **Adaptation & Contrast:** Continuous exposure dulls sensation; alternating strong/weak samples exaggerates differences.
3. **Mood & Emotion:** Stress, hunger, and cultural background alter perceived liking.
4. **Memory & Expectation:** Brand names, packaging, price, and prior information bias perceived quality.

**Psychological Errors (already covered in detail in Section 2.2.2).**

**Scaling Methods in Sensory Testing:**

| Method | Type of Data | Example | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Category Scale** | Ordinal | 1–9 point "Dislike to Like" | Quick consumer tests, hedonic studies |
| **Visual Analogue Scale (VAS)** | Interval | 100 mm line: "not sweet" to "extremely sweet" | Trained panels, clinical studies |
| **Magnitude Estimation** | Ratio | Panelist assigns number proportional to intensity | Trained panels, Stevens' Law work |
| **Hedonic Scale (9-pt)** | Ordinal | 1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely | **Acceptance/preference** testing |
| **Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale** | Ordinal | "much too little" → "much too much" | **Product optimization** (with penalty analysis) |
| **Forced-Choice / Paired Preference** | Nominal | "Which do you prefer? A or B?" | Discrimination, simple preference |
| **Rank-Rating** | Ordinal | Rank samples from 1 to 5 | Small sample sets (n ≤ 6) |

**Selection Criteria:**
- Use **hedonic** for liking.
- Use **JAR + Penalty** for optimization.
- Use **VAS / Magnitude** for trained-panel intensity.
- Use **Triangle / Paired** for discrimination.
- Use **Rank** for small comparative sets.

---

**Q. (b) (7 Marks)** Describe the **physiological aspects** of sensory evaluation. Explain the **four types of sensory thresholds** with examples.

**Model Answer:**

**Physiological Aspects** refer to the biological and neurological basis of sensing — the receptors, neural pathways, and brain regions that transduce a chemical or physical stimulus into a measurable response. They also include the **thresholds** that quantify the limits of human perception.

**Sensory Receptors & Pathways (already detailed in Section 2.1.1):** taste buds, olfactory neurons, retinal photoreceptors, cutaneous mechanoreceptors, and cochlear hair cells. Each receptor is specialized for one modality, and transduction occurs via G-protein cascades (taste, smell) or ion-channel gating (somesthesis, hearing).

**Factors Influencing Physiological Response:** age, sex, health, hunger state, hormonal status, medications, genetic polymorphisms (e.g., TAS2R38 for bitterness), and smoking/alcohol habits.

**The Four Sensory Thresholds:**

1. **Detection Threshold (Absolute Threshold)**
   - **Definition:** The lowest concentration or intensity of a stimulus at which a panelist can *just* detect a difference from a blank (no stimulus).
   - **Food Example:** Sucrose in water — DT ≈ **0.2% w/v**. Panelist says "I sense *something* sweet is here, but cannot identify it."
   - **Method of determination:** Forced-choice ascending series (ASTM E679) or 3-Alternative Forced Choice (3-AFC).

2. **Recognition Threshold**
   - **Definition:** The concentration at which the panelist can *both* detect the stimulus **and** correctly *identify* its quality.
   - **Food Example:** Sucrose — RT ≈ **0.4–0.5% w/v**. Panelist says "This is **sweet**."
   - **Significance:** RT is more practically relevant in food formulation (we want consumers to *recognize* sweetness, not just vaguely sense it).

3. **Difference Threshold (DL or JND — Just Noticeable Difference)**
   - **Definition:** The smallest change in stimulus intensity that a panelist can reliably detect.
   - **Food Example:** If a juice has 10% sugar, JND is typically ~**0.8% w/v**. So 10% vs. 10.8% may be indistinguishable, while 10% vs. 11% is noticeable.
   - **Governing law:** Weber's Law — $\Delta I / I = k$.

4. **Terminal Threshold (Maximum Tolerable)**
   - **Definition:** The highest concentration of a stimulus that can be perceived without discomfort or pain.
   - **Food Example:** Bitterness of caffeine in coffee — terminal threshold ~**1500 ppm**; above this, the panelist reports "bitter to the point of pain" or refuses to swallow.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Threshold Questions:**
> 1. Confusing **detection** with **recognition** (DT vs RT).
> 2. Stating JND is a *fixed value* (it is a *fraction* per Weber's Law).
> 3. Saying the four thresholds are *independent* — they are *interrelated* and follow the same sensory continuum.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **MODULE 3 VALUATION PITFALLS — Read Before Writing Your Exam**
>
> 1. **Do NOT list threshold types without giving food examples** — Examiner awards 1 mark for application.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse Weber (ΔI/I = k) with Fechner (S = k·log I) with Stevens (S = k·I^n)** — these are three distinct laws. KTU often asks to differentiate.
> 3. **Do NOT omit the Greek letter symbols** — write $\Delta I$, $k$, $n$, $d'$ exactly as shown.
> 4. **Do NOT state "psychological errors are unavoidable"** — always pair the error with its **mitigation** for full marks.
> 5. **Do NOT forget the light-colour table** — examiners often allocate 2 marks purely for the colour-coding table.
> 6. **Do NOT write "sensory evaluation is the same as taste testing"** — it includes sight, smell, sound, texture, and taste.
> 7. **Always mention Williams' Latin Square** when discussing sample-order randomization (full marks for order-effect question).
> 8. **Always pair the law with an equation and a real food example** — a formula without application = 2/7 marks; formula + example + application = 7/7 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MODULE 3**

### Physiological Aspects
- [ ] Five senses: taste, smell, sight, touch, sound (the 5 modalities of food).
- [ ] **Four thresholds** in order: Detection → Recognition → Difference (JND) → Terminal.
- [ ] **Weber's Law:** $\Delta I / I = k$ (constant fraction).
- [ ] **Stevens' Power Law:** $S = k \cdot I^{n}$ — with exponents for taste (sweet 0.8, salty 1.3, sour 1.0, bitter 0.6).
- [ ] **Adaptation** = decreased response to constant stimulus; **Fatigue** = elevated threshold after prolonged exposure.
- [ ] **TAS2R38 gene** controls PTC/PROP bitterness tasting ability (genetic variation in physiology).

### Psychological Aspects
- [ ] **Sensation** = raw neural input; **Perception** = brain's interpretation.
- [ ] **8 key psychological errors:** Expectation, Stimulus, Halo, Order, Contrast, Central Tendency, Acquiescence, Mundane Realism.
- [ ] **Hedonic scale (9-pt)** = liking; **JAR scale (5–7-pt)** = optimization; **VAS** = intensity; **Magnitude Estimation** = ratio data.
- [ ] **Signal Detection Theory:** $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ separates sensitivity from bias.
