- [ ] **Penalty analysis** quantifies how much liking drops when samples are "off-JAR."

### Mechanical Aspects
- [ ] **Booth design:** ≥ 1 m separation, neutral colour, climate-controlled (20–22 °C).
- [ ] **Lighting colour code (CRITICAL):**
  - **Red** = mask colour differences (taste-only tests).
  - **Green** = highlight chlorophyll differences (vegetables).
  - **Yellow** = hide brown/yellow tints (baked goods).
  - **White (D65)** = standard reference for appearance.
- [ ] **Ventilation:** Negative pressure, ≥ 10 air-changes/hour, carbon-filtered air.
- [ ] **Sample coding:** 3-digit random numbers; **order:** Williams' Latin Square.
- [ ] **Serving temperature standards:** Cold beverages ~10 °C, hot beverages ~60 °C, room temperature 20 °C.
- [ ] **Inter-stimulus interval:** 30 s – 5 min; **palate cleansers:** distilled water + unsalted crackers.
- [ ] **Software tools:** Compusense, SIMS, Fizz, RedJade, EyeQuestion.

### KTU-Specific Golden Rules
- [ ] **Always** pair a law/equation with a **food-based numerical example**.
- [ ] **Always** provide a **table** for comparing methods (scales, thresholds, light colours).
- [ ] **Always** state **units** (ppm, %, °C, a.u.) — the examiner checks for unit awareness.
- [ ] **Always** mention **mitigation** for every psychological error.
- [ ] **Always** close with a **concluding sentence** (e.g., "Hence, all three aspects must be controlled for valid sensory data.")
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Imitative Measurements: Equipments Used, Texture Profile Analysis, Dynamic Test for Evaluation of Food Texture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT524 - SENSORY EVALUATION OF FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Imitative Measurements: Equipments Used, Texture Profile Analysis, Dynamic Test for Evaluation of Food Texture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Imitative Measurements — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imitative Measurements** (also called **Mimetic Tests** or **Mastication Tests**) are **objective instrumental methods** designed to **mechanically simulate the actions of the human mouth/jaw** (biting, chewing, cutting, shearing, compressing, and penetrating) on a food product, and to record the **force–time** or **force–deformation** response of the food as a numerical surrogate of its sensory texture.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, these are classified under the broader domain of **Objective Texture Measurement** (as opposed to Subjective Sensory Evaluation covered in earlier modules). They convert qualitative descriptors like "crunchy", "tough", "gummy", or "sticky" into **reproducible, quantitative engineering parameters** such as hardness, adhesiveness, chewiness, etc.

### 1.2 Categories of Imitative Measurements (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

The three primary KTU-classified imitative test families are:

1. **Fundamental Tests** — e.g., compression, tension, shear on a defined geometry.
2. **Imitative Tests** — devices specifically engineered to mimic oral/jaw action.
3. **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** — the most popular **two-bite imitative test** using a Texture Analyzer.

The present topic focuses on **(b) Imitative Test equipment** and the canonical **TPA**, plus the **Dynamic Tests** for food texture.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Robotic Mouth"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an Imitative Tester as a "Robotic Mouth with a Clipboard."**
>
> - The **probe/cell** is the **teeth and tongue**.
> - The **load cell** is the **taste-and-touch nerve** that *writes down* (records) the force felt.
> - The **crosshead movement** is the **jaw opening and closing**.
> - The **force–deformation curve** is the *complete written transcript* of what the food "said" while being chewed.
>
> Just as a human panelist fills out a sensory scorecard, the instrument fills out a **force–time scorecard**, but with much higher precision (typically to **±0.001 N**) and reproducibility.

### 1.4 Why Imitative Measurements Matter in Food Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Decouple from human variability:** Removes fatigue, mood, and personal bias of panelists.
> - **Quality Control (QC):** Detects batch-to-batch variation in a factory (e.g., biscuit hardness, ketchup viscosity, meat tenderness).
> - **Shelf-life studies:** Monitors *temporal softening* of bread, *hardening* of cookies during storage.
> - **Product Development (R&D):** Benchmarks reformulated products (low-fat, sugar-reduced) against a reference standard.
> - **Sensory–Instrumental Correlation:** Bridges Module 2 (Sensory methods) with hard data — many KTU questions test this link.

### 1.5 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

The output is universally expressed in the **SI system**:

- **Force** → **Newtons (N)** (sometimes **kgf** in older literature; $1 \text{ kgf} \approx 9.81 \text{ N}$)
- **Distance/Deformation** → **millimetres (mm)** or **metres (m)**
- **Time** → **seconds (s)**
- **Energy/Work** → **Joules (J)** = N·m
- **Strain Rate / Test Speed** → **mm s⁻¹** (typical imitative: **0.5 – 10 mm s⁻¹**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Imitative Equipment (Block-Level View)

Imitative instruments for food texture are commonly grouped by the **type of mechanical action** they emulate:

| # | Equipment Class | Mimicked Oral Action | Typical Probe / Cell | Force Range | KTU-Relevant Foods |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Texture Analyzer (TA) / Universal Testing Machine (UTM, e.g., Instron, TA.XTplus, Lloyd)** | Generic compression / penetration | Flat plunger, cylinder, cone | 0.5 N – 5 kN | Universal |
| 2 | **Kramer Shear Press (Multi-blade Shear Cell)** | Multi-tooth chewing | 5/10/13-blade standard shear cell | Up to ~5 kN | Vegetables, meat, snacks |
| 3 | **Warner–Bratzler Shear Blade** | Single incisor bite | Triangular V-notch blade | Up to ~500 N | Cooked meat tenderness |
| 4 | **Puncture / Penetration Probe** | Front incisor puncture | Spherical, conical, needle | 0.1 N – 100 N | Fruits, gels, jellies |
| 5 | **Back/Forward Extrusion Cell** | Tongue–palate compression | Annular piston in a cylinder | 0.5 N – 200 N | Ketchup, yogurt, pastes |
| 6 | **Three-Point Bending Rig** | First incisor snap | Knife-edge supports + blade | 1 N – 200 N | Biscuits, pasta, chocolate bars |
| 7 | **Cutting Test Rig (Kramer-type or Ottawa cell)** | Shearing at the molars | Wire/grid cutters | Up to ~5 kN | Meat, cheese, fibrous foods |

> [!NOTE]
> Most modern KTU-approved labs consolidate items 1–7 into **one programmable Texture Analyzer** (e.g., **TA.XT2i, TA.HDplus, Instron 3343/3366, Lloyd LF plus**) by simply changing the load cell and probe.

## 2.2 The Generic Force–Time Curve — Common Language

Every imitative test produces a **Force (N) vs. Time (s)** or **Force vs. Distance (mm)** curve. The shape of this curve carries the *texture fingerprint* of the food.

For a **two-bite TPA**, the curve has **two prominent humps** separated by a *downward dip* during the *second approach*. This curve is the **single most important diagram** in KTU Module 3.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Texture Profile Analysis Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the **7 primary TPA parameters**, their **definition**, **unit**, and **graphical derivation**. These are recurring **14-mark questions** in KTU ESE.

| # | Parameter (Symbol) | Formal Definition | Mathematical Expression | Unit | Sensory Correlate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hardness** ($H$) | Peak force during the **first compression** | $H = F_{1,\text{peak}}$ | **N** | Force required to bite through |
| 2 | **Adhesiveness** ($A_d$) | Negative work (area) of the **first bite's withdrawal** | $A_d = \int_{\text{up-stroke 1}} F \, dt$ (signed negative area) | **N·s** or **J** | Stickiness to the palate |
| 3 | **Springiness** ($S_p$) | Distance recovered between end of 1st and peak of 2nd compression | $S_p = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ | **dimensionless (ratio)** | Spring-back after partial compression |
| 4 | **Cohesiveness** ($C_o$) | Ratio of positive areas of the 2nd and 1st compressions | $C_o = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | **dimensionless (ratio)** | How well the sample holds together |
| 5 | **Gumminess** ($G$) | Hardness × Cohesiveness | $G = H \times C_o$ | **N** | Energy needed to disintegrate a semi-solid |
| 6 | **Chewiness** ($C_w$) | Gumminess × Springiness | $C_w = H \times C_o \times S_p$ | **N** | Energy to chew a solid to swallow |
| 7 | **Resilience** ($R$) | Ratio of areas of the up-stroke to the down-stroke (1st bite only) | $R = \dfrac{A_{\text{up,1}}}{A_{\text{down,1}}}$ | **dimensionless (ratio)** | Instantaneous elastic recovery |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU BOARD NOTE:** When writing the *Adhesiveness* area in an exam, **always include the sign convention** — the curve dips below the time axis during retraction, and this *negative* area is the adhesiveness. Many students lose marks by giving only the magnitude.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Dynamic Test Parameters

Dynamic tests are single-pass, high-speed, or single-event tests (no double compression). Typical derived parameters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\max} &= \text{Peak force at fracture} \quad [\text{N}] \\
D_f &= \text{Deformation at fracture} \quad [\text{mm}] \\
E_{\text{abs}} &= \int_{0}^{D_f} F \, dD \quad [\text{J}] \;\; \text{(Work to fracture)} \\
\sigma &= \frac{F_{\max}}{A_{\text{contact}}} \quad [\text{Pa}] \;\; \text{(Apparent stress)} \\
\varepsilon &= \frac{D_f}{D_0} \quad \text{(Apparent engineering strain)} \\
\text{Firmness Index} &= \frac{F_{\max}}{D_f} \quad \left[\frac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $D_0$ is the original sample thickness/dimension and $A_{\text{contact}}$ is the projected contact area of the probe.

### 2.5 Why These Formulas Matter in Practice

- **Hardness** correlates with sensory *firmness*.
- **Springiness** correlates with *elasticity / freshness perception* (e.g., bread).
- **Cohesiveness** correlates with *crumbly vs. gummy* (cheese, cake).
- **Chewiness** is the **gold standard for meat tenderness** in QC labs.
- **Resilience** is now a regulatory parameter in **spongy-cake and bread** specifications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Generic TPA Force–Time Curve

The **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** test, originally codified by **Szczesniak, Brandt, and Friedman (1963)** and refined by **Bourne (1978)**, simulates **two successive human bites** with an inter-cycle pause.

### 3.1.1 Test Protocol (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Pre-test (Idle).**
The probe sits above the sample at a clearance $h_0$. The crosshead is stationary.
- Force reading: $F = 0$ N.

**Step 2 — First Compression (Down-stroke).**
The crosshead descends at a constant **test speed** $v$ (typical: **1.0 – 5.0 mm s⁻¹**) along the negative $z$-axis.
- The probe contacts the sample at $t = t_c$ (contact time).
- Force rises approximately linearly (Hookean region) up to a yield point, then more gradually as the cellular matrix collapses.
- Peak force is reached at $F = F_{1,\text{peak}}$ at time $t = t_{1,p}$.
- The **down-stroke area** $A_{\text{down},1} = \int_{t_c}^{t_{1,p}} F(t) \, dt$ represents the work done **on** the food.

**Step 3 — Pause (Hold) at Maximum Compression.**
The crosshead holds for a brief dwell (typically **2 – 5 s**), allowing *stress relaxation* in viscoelastic foods.
- The force **decays exponentially**:

$$
F(t) = F_{\text{hold}} \, e^{-t / \tau}
$$

where $\tau$ is the **stress-relaxation time constant** (s). A short $\tau$ implies a *more elastic* food; a long $\tau$ indicates *more viscous* (e.g., cheese vs. cracker).

**Step 4 — First Withdrawal (Up-stroke).**
The probe retracts at the same speed $v$.
- Adhesiveness, if present, **pulls** the probe down against gravity, producing a **negative-going force lobe** below the time axis.
- The **negative signed area** is:

$$
A_d = -\int_{t_{1,p}}^{t_{1,e}} F(t) \, dt \quad ; \quad F(t) < 0 \text{ in the dip}
$$

**Step 5 — Pause Between Bites.**
The crosshead pauses for the *between-bite time* $t_{bb}$ (typical: **3 – 5 s**).
- The food partially **springs back** by an amount $\Delta D$.
- Springiness is then calculated as:

$$
S_p = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{\Delta D_2}{\Delta D_1}
$$

where $D_1$ is the deformation at the end of the 1st compression and $D_2$ at the peak of the 2nd compression.

**Step 6 — Second Compression (Second Bite).**
The cycle is repeated. The peak force $F_{2,\text{peak}}$ is **almost always less than** $F_{1,\text{peak}}$ because the food's internal structure has been **broken down** by the first bite.
- The **ratio of areas** $A_2 / A_1$ defines **Cohesiveness**:

$$
C_o = \frac{A_{\text{down},2}}{A_{\text{down},1}}
$$

- $C_o = 1$ → perfectly cohesive; $C_o \to 0$ → completely crumbly.

**Step 7 — Second Withdrawal (Optional).**
The probe retracts, producing a second negative area $A_{d,2}$, used for advanced studies.

### 3.1.2 Master TPA Equation for Chewiness

Combining the three composite parameters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ch} &= H \times C_o \times S_p \\
&= (F_{1,\text{peak}}) \times \left( \frac{A_2}{A_1} \right) \times \left( \frac{D_2}{D_1} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **unit of Chewiness is Newtons (N)** because Springiness and Cohesiveness are dimensionless ratios and only Hardness carries units.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

A chewable food sample is subjected to TPA at **2 mm s⁻¹** with a 50 % strain. The force–time curve yields the following data:

| Point | Force (N) | Time (s) |
|---|---|---|
| 1st peak | 38.5 | 2.40 |
| End 1st down-stroke | 8.0 | 4.80 |
| 2nd peak | 30.2 | 8.00 |
| End 2nd down-stroke | 6.0 | 10.20 |

Measured areas: $A_1 = 95.2$ N·s, $A_2 = 71.4$ N·s.
Distances: $D_1 = 4.80$ mm, $D_2 = 4.30$ mm.

**Compute all 7 TPA parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardness, } H &= 38.5 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
\text{Cohesiveness, } C_o &= \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{71.4}{95.2} = 0.75 \;\; \text{(dimensionless)} \\[4pt]
\text{Springiness, } S_p &= \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{4.30}{4.80} = 0.896 \;\; \text{(dimensionless)} \\[4pt]
\text{Gumminess, } G &= H \times C_o = 38.5 \times 0.75 = 28.875 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
\text{Chewiness, Ch} &= H \times C_o \times S_p = 38.5 \times 0.75 \times 0.896 = 25.87 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **exact numerical answer is not asked in KTU**; the **derivation logic and units** are. State the *formula first*, then *plug in the values*, then *box the answer with units*.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Linking Test to Sensory Perception

| Instrumental Parameter | Dominant Mechanical Property | Sensory Descriptor | Typical KTU Example Food |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardness (H) | Compressive strength | Hard, firm, tough | Raw carrot, hard candy |
| Adhesiveness (A) | Tensile surface tack | Sticky, tacky, gooey | Cheese spread, honey |
| Springiness (S) | Elastic recovery | Springy, rubbery | Bread, marshmallow |
| Cohesiveness (C) | Internal bond strength | Crumbly, brittle vs. gummy | Cheese, cake |
| Gumminess (G) | Semi-solid disintegration energy | Gummy, doughy | Cheese, gummy candy |
| Chewiness (Ch) | Solid disintegration energy | Chewy, tough | Meat, toffee |
| Resilience (R) | Instant rebound | Doughy vs. springy | Sponge cake |
| Fracture Force (dynamic) | Shear strength | Tender vs. tough | Cooked meat (Warner–Bratzler) |
| Puncture Force (dynamic) | Skin rupture threshold | Crispness, pop | Apple, grape |

## 3.3 Critical Operational Notes for KTU Lab

1. **Sample Geometry Standardisation** — Cylindrical samples of identical height and diameter are mandatory; otherwise $C_o$ and $S_p$ become incomparable.
2. **Strain Choice** — Typically **25 – 75 %** engineering strain. TPA is *strain-controlled*, not load-controlled.
3. **Crosshead Speed** — Must be **constant** and within the **quasi-static regime** (no inertia heating). A KTU board favourite: "Why use low speeds?" — Answer: *to stay in the linear viscoelastic region and avoid shock waves*.
4. **Temperature Control** — At **± 0.5 °C**, since fats and ice crystals are highly temperature-sensitive.
5. **Number of Replicates** — Minimum **5**, ideally **10**, to compute mean ± SD; this satisfies the **CO4 (statistical analysis)** learning outcome.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Imitative Texture Tests

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Imitative Texture Measurement] --> B[Texture Profile Analysis TPA]
    A --> C[Dynamic Tests Single Pass]
    A --> D[Imitative Cells Multi Action]

    B --> B1[Flat Plunger Compression]
    B --> B2[Twin Bite Two Cycle]
    B1 --> B3[Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness]
    B1 --> B4[Adhesiveness, Gumminess, Chewiness, Resilience]

    C --> C1[Puncture Penetration]
    C --> C2[Extrusion Back and Forward]
    C --> C3[Cutting Warner Bratzler]
    C --> C4[Three Point Bending]
    C1 --> C5[Skin rupture force, Crispness]
    C2 --> C6[Consistency, Viscosity index]
    C3 --> C7[Meat tenderness score]
    C4 --> C8[Snappability, Break strength]

    D --> D1[Kramer Shear Press]
    D --> D2[Ottawa Cell]
    D --> D3[Loewen Press]
    D1 --> D4[Multi blade chewing simulation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — TPA Test Sequence (Engineering Workflow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Start: Sample Mounted on Base Plate] --> P1[Probe Approaches at Constant Speed v]
    P1 --> P2{Probe Contacts Sample?}
    P2 -- No --> P1
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Record Force vs Time: 1st Down Stroke]
    P3 --> P4[Detect Peak F1 peak equals Hardness]
    P4 --> P5[Hold Crosshead for Stress Relaxation]
    P5 --> P6[Retract Probe: 1st Up Stroke]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Negative Area equals Adhesiveness]
    P7 --> P8[Wait for Between Bite Time t bb]
    P8 --> P9[Descend Again: 2nd Down Stroke]
    P9 --> P10[Record Peak F2 peak and Area A2]
    P10 --> P11[Compute Cohesiveness equals A2 by A1]
    P11 --> P12[Compute Springiness equals D2 by D1]
    P12 --> P13[Compute Gumminess and Chewiness and Resilience]
    P13 --> P14[End: Report 7 TPA Parameters]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Modern Texture Analyzer

```mermaid
graph TD
    U[Universal Texture Analyzer Hardware] --> U1[Load Cell 0.5 N to 5 kN]
    U --> U2[Motorized Crosshead Speed 0.001 to 40 mm per sec]
    U --> U3[Interchangeable Probes and Cells]
    U --> U4[PC Software for Force Time Capture]
    U3 --> V1[Flat Plunger 50 mm]
    U3 --> V2[Spherical Probe 1 inch]
    U3 --> V3[Kramer Shear Cell 5 and 10 Blade]
    U3 --> V4[Warner Bratzler V Blade]
    U3 --> V5[Back Extrusion Annular Piston]
    U4 --> W1[Real Time Force Time Plot]
    U4 --> W2[Auto Computation of 7 TPA Parameters]
    U4 --> W3[Export to CSV for Statistical Analysis]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram-to-Exam Mapping:** The KTU board examiner may ask you to *"draw a labelled block diagram of a Texture Profile Analyzer showing its key components and the typical force-time curve obtained"*. The three mermaid diagrams above collectively cover this requirement — practise re-drawing them in **pen-and-paper form** (no need to include them in the answer sheet as Mermaid; convert to a clean labelled hand sketch).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define imitative measurements in food texture evaluation. Give two examples of instruments used.**

> **Model Answer (≈ 60 words, 3 marks):**
> Imitative measurements are objective instrumental methods that mechanically simulate the biting, chewing, and shearing actions of the human mouth on food, and record the force–deformation response as a quantitative texture parameter. They decouple texture assessment from human sensory variability. **Examples:** Texture Analyzer (TA.XTplus) and the Kramer Shear Press. **Marks split:** Definition 2 marks + Examples 1 mark.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three TPA parameters and state their units.**

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Hardness** — Peak force of first compression, unit: **N**.
> 2. **Adhesiveness** — Negative area of first bite, unit: **N·s** (or J).
> 3. **Chewiness** — Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness, unit: **N** (because the two ratios are dimensionless).
> **Marks split:** 1 mark per correct pair (parameter + unit).

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Long-Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the principle and procedure of **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)**. Draw a labelled force–time curve and explain how the seven primary TPA parameters are derived from it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bread sample of dimensions $25 \text{ mm} \times 25 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$ is subjected to TPA at a crosshead speed of $2 \text{ mm s}^{-1}$ and $50\%$ strain. From the force-time curve: $F_{1,\text{peak}} = 12.4 \text{ N}$, $A_1 = 30.5 \text{ N·s}$, $A_2 = 22.8 \text{ N·s}$, $D_1 = 10.0 \text{ mm}$, $D_2 = 8.2 \text{ mm}$. Calculate Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, and Chewiness, and briefly comment on whether this bread is "soft and springy" or "tough and crumbly".

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (a)

**Principle (2 marks):** TPA is a **two-cycle, strain-controlled compression test** that imitates two successive human bites on a food sample. A flat plunger descends at constant speed, compresses the sample to a target strain, retracts, pauses for a defined *between-bite time*, and repeats the cycle. The resulting force–time curve is auto-analysed for **seven primary texture parameters**.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Condition the sample (size, temperature: **20 ± 1 °C**, RH ≈ 50 %).
2. Mount on the base plate; select probe (flat cylinder, **75 mm dia** is KTU-standard).
3. Set crosshead speed (typical: **1 – 5 mm s⁻¹**) and target strain (typical: **25 – 75 %**).
4. Trigger the test; the analyser records the force–time trace.
5. The software integrates areas and extracts peak forces automatically.
6. Replicate **5–10 times**; report mean ± SD.

**Labelled Force–Time Curve (2 marks):**
The student should draw the classical *two-hump* curve, labelling: (i) First peak = Hardness, (ii) Negative lobe = Adhesiveness, (iii) Ratio of areas $A_2/A_1$ = Cohesiveness, (iv) Ratio of distances $D_2/D_1$ = Springiness, (v) Up-stroke/down-stroke ratio = Resilience.

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (b)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardness } H &= F_{1,\text{peak}} = 12.4 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
\text{Cohesiveness } C_o &= \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{22.8}{30.5} = 0.747 \\[4pt]
\text{Springiness } S_p &= \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{8.2}{10.0} = 0.820 \\[4pt]
\text{Gumminess } G &= H \times C_o = 12.4 \times 0.747 = 9.26 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
\text{Chewiness Ch} &= H \times C_o \times S_p = 12.4 \times 0.747 \times 0.820 = 7.59 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (≈ 30 words, 1 mark):** The bread is **relatively soft** (low H = 12.4 N), **fairly springy** (S_p = 0.82 > 0.7), and **moderately cohesive** (C_o = 0.75). It is best described as *"soft and springy"* — typical of a fresh, well-conditioned bread crumb.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Chewiness unit:** Many students wrongly write *Joules* for chewiness. It is **Newtons**, because the two ratios are dimensionless.
> 2. **Adhesiveness sign:** Always mention the *negative* area; do not just give the magnitude.
> 3. **Springiness vs. Resilience confusion:** Springiness uses *time/distance between the two peaks*; Resilience uses the *up-stroke/down-stroke area ratio of the first bite only*.
> 4. **Strain control vs. load control:** TPA is **strain-controlled**, not load-controlled — stating the opposite loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the units in the final answer** — instant 0.5 mark deduction in KTU board valuation.

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of **Warner–Bratzler Shear** and **Kramer Shear Press** as imitative dynamic tests. Compare their geometry, foods evaluated, and the parameters reported.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **back-extrusion** in food texture evaluation? Draw a labelled cell and the typical force–deformation curve. State the two parameters obtained and their physical meaning.

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (a)

**Warner–Bratzler Shear (WBS) (3.5 marks):**
- **Principle:** A triangular **V-notched blade** (10 mm wide, 60° included angle, 1 mm thick) shears perpendicularly through a cylindrical meat core (typically **12.7 mm dia** cooked muscle sample).
- **Mimicked action:** Incisive / molar bite.
- **Output:** Peak shear force (**N**) — single value reported as the **tenderness index**; lower = more tender.
- **Geometry:** Single blade; single shear plane.
- **Foods:** Cooked meat, fish, poultry.

**Kramer Shear Press (KSP) (3 marks):**
- **Principle:** A **multi-blade cell** (5, 10, or 13 blades, each **3 mm** thick, spaced **5 mm** apart) is driven through a known mass of food (typically **100 – 200 g**) held in a slotted bottom plate.
- **Mimicked action:** Multi-tooth chewing.
- **Output:** Peak force (**N**), **work/energy to compress** (**J**), and a **firmness index** (**N mm⁻¹**).
- **Geometry:** Multi-blade; multiple simultaneous shear planes.
- **Foods:** Vegetables (peas, beans), snacks, comminuted meats.

**Comparison (0.5 mark):**

| Aspect | Warner–Bratzler | Kramer |
|---|---|---|
| Blade | 1 V-notch | 5/10/13 parallel |
| Sample | Single core | Bulk mass |
| Primary Output | Peak force | Force + Energy |
| Best for | Tenderness | Chewiness/Crispness |

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (b)

**Back-Extrusion (BE) Principle (2.5 marks):**
- A **cylindrical container** is filled with the food; a **plunger/annular piston** descends into the food.
- The food is **forced upward** in the **annular gap** between the piston and the wall — hence "**back**-extrusion."
- The force rises to a **peak** ($F_{BE,\text{max}}$) corresponding to the point at which the bulk material first yields and reverses flow, then drops to a **steady-state plateau** ($F_{BE,\text{ss}}$) representing continuous flow.

**Labelled Curve + Cell (2.5 marks):** Draw a **cylindrical cup** with a **central piston**, showing food being squeezed into the **annular gap**, plus a **force–deformation plot** with peak + steady-state regions.

**Two Parameters (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{consistency}} &= F_{BE,\text{max}} \quad \text{(Peak force — initial resistance / firmness)} \\
F_{\text{flow-steady}} &= F_{BE,\text{ss}} \quad \text{(Steady force — flow / viscosity-related index)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **back-extrusion** with **forward-extrusion** (where food exits *through* an orifice in the bottom — used for paste viscosity). Remember: **back** = goes *back* up around the piston.

---

## 5.3 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU Module-3 revision — high-density bullet recap of *Imitative Measurements, TPA & Dynamic Tests*.**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **Imitative test:** instrumental test that mechanically copies the **human biting/chewing action** and records **force–time/deformation** data.
- **TPA:** two-cycle strain-controlled compression; gives **7 primary parameters**.
- **Dynamic test:** single-pass, often fracture-oriented (Warner–Bratzler, puncture, extrusion, 3-point bend).

### ✅ The 7 TPA Parameters — Memorise in Order
1. **Hardness** = $F_{1,\text{peak}}$ — **N**
2. **Adhesiveness** = $-\int F\,dt$ (negative area) — **N·s**
3. **Springiness** = $D_2 / D_1$ — dimensionless
4. **Cohesiveness** = $A_2 / A_1$ — dimensionless
5. **Gumminess** = $H \times C_o$ — **N**
6. **Chewiness** = $H \times C_o \times S_p$ — **N**
7. **Resilience** = $A_{\text{up},1} / A_{\text{down},1}$ — dimensionless

### ✅ Equipment Mapping
- **Multi-bite (chewing) →** Kramer Shear Cell
- **Single incisive bite →** Warner–Bratzler V-blade
- **Puncture / skin rupture →** Spherical or needle probe
- **Tongue–palate action →** Back-extrusion cell
- **Snap / break →** 3-point bending
- **Generic two-bite →** Flat-plunger TPA on a Texture Analyzer

### ✅ Numerical & Operational Points
- **Test speed:** 1 – 5 mm s⁻¹ (quasi-static).
- **Strain:** 25 – 75 %.
- **Replicates:** ≥ 5 (≥ 10 for QC).
- **Temperature control:** ± 0.5 °C.
- **Sample geometry:** identical cylinders, mounted identically.

### ✅ Sensory–Instrumental Correlations (Recall the Matrix)
- **H ↔ Firmness; A ↔ Stickiness; S_p ↔ Springiness; C_o ↔ Crumbliness; Ch ↔ Chewy; R ↔ Elastic Rebound; W-B force ↔ Tenderness.**

### ✅ High-Yield Sentence for 14-Mark Answers
> *"TPA is a two-bite imitative test performed under **strain-controlled** quasi-static compression, whose force–time curve yields **Hardness, Adhesiveness, Springiness, Cohesiveness, Gumminess, Chewiness and Resilience** — all derived as ratios or products of peak forces, integrated areas, and inter-peak distances, with **Hardness** and **Chewiness** expressed in **Newtons**."*

### ✅ Likely KTU Theory-Only 7-Mark Questions
1. "With a neat sketch, explain the TPA curve and derive all 7 parameters."
2. "Compare Warner–Bratzler and Kramer Shear Press."
3. "Describe back-extrusion with a labelled diagram."
4. "Differentiate TPA from a single-compression dynamic test."
5. "Discuss the engineering significance of imitative tests in food quality control."

### ✅ Likely Calculation 7-Mark Questions
- Always: **compute $H, C_o, S_p, G, \text{Ch}$** from given $(F_{1,\text{peak}}, A_1, A_2, D_1, D_2)$.
- Always: **comment on texture descriptor** based on the numerical result.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colour: Measuring Equipment, L*a*b System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT524 - SENSORY EVALUATION OF FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Colour: Measuring Equipment, L*a*b System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Colour** in the context of food sensory evaluation is defined as a psychophysical attribute of a food product arising from the spectral composition of visible light (wavelength range **380–780 nm**) reflected, transmitted, or emitted by the sample, as perceived by the human visual system and standardized through instruments called **colorimeters** and **spectrophotometers**.

The **CIE L\*a\*b\* colour system** (also known as **CIELAB**) is a device-independent, three-dimensional, opponent-colour model developed by the **Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE)** in **1976**. It expresses colour numerically using three orthogonal axes:

- **L\*** — Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)
- **a\*** — Red/Green axis (positive = red, negative = green)
- **b\*** — Yellow/Blue axis (positive = yellow, negative = blue)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEFTT524 — Module 3):**
> Colour is the *first* quality attribute perceived by the consumer. Instrumental colour measurement eliminates human subjectivity and provides a quantitative, reproducible, regulatory-defensible value that correlates with sensory panel scores.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chef's Colour Thermometer"

Imagine a chef tasting soup and saying, *"It needs more salt."* But how much salt? Without a salt-meter (refractometer for Brix, or salinometer), the chef can only give a subjective answer. Colour works the same way. A trained panelist may say, *"This tomato ketchup is too dark,"* but a **L\*a\*b\* colorimeter** can say, *"L\* = 24.6, a\* = 28.1, b\* = 14.3, ΔE vs reference = 3.7"* — a number you can put in a quality-control report.

Think of the **L\*a\*b\* cube** as a **3D "colour map of the world"**:
- The **vertical axis (L\*)** is the brightness ladder (a charcoal briquette at the bottom, fresh snow at the top).
- The **horizontal a\* axis** is the "ripe strawberry ↔ fresh basil leaf" axis.
- The **horizontal b\* axis** is the "lemon peel ↔ blueberry" axis.

Every perceivable colour is a single point inside this cube.

### 1.3 Why Colour Measurement Matters in Food Science

| Application | Why L*a*b* is Used |
|---|---|
| **Quality control** (e.g., coffee roasting) | Objective batch-to-batch consistency |
| **Process monitoring** (e.g., baking, frying Maillard reaction) | Track browning numerically |
| **Shelf-life studies** | Detect discoloration (ΔE) over storage |
| **Regulatory compliance** | FDA, FSSAI labelling of colour additives |
| **Correlation with sensory panels** | Link instrumental a\*/b\* with panel "redness"/"yellowness" scores |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants used in CIELAB:**
> - Illuminant **D65** (average daylight, 6504 K) — default for food
> - Observer angle **10°** (CIE 1964 supplementary standard observer)
> - Reference white point **(Xn, Yn, Zn)** defined by the illuminant
> - Illuminant **A** (tungsten, 2856 K) — for warm products
> - Illuminant **C** (6724 K) — historical reference, rarely used now
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — How the L\*a\*b\* System Works

The L\*a\*b\* system is **derived from the CIE XYZ tristimulus values** in a multi-step pipeline. The logic flow is:

1. **Light source** (standard illuminant, e.g., D65) illuminates the food sample.
2. **Sample** selectively absorbs certain wavelengths and reflects/transmits others.
3. **Detector** (photodiode array) measures the reflected spectrum **R(λ)**.
4. The instrument **integrates** R(λ) over the visible range weighted by the **CIE standard observer functions** $\bar{x}(\lambda)$, $\bar{y}(\lambda)$, $\bar{z}(\lambda)$ to give the **tristimulus values X, Y, Z**.
5. The XYZ values are **mathematically transformed** into L\*, a\*, b* using the non-linear cube-root function.
6. The L*a*b* values are then **interpreted** by comparison to a reference standard or panel data.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Coordinate

- **L\* — Lightness (0 to 100):** Why use a cube-root instead of linear Y? Because human vision perceives brightness **logarithmically** (Weber-Fechner law). The cube-root compresses Y so that *equal numerical steps in L\* correspond to equal perceptual steps.*
- **a\* — Red/Green:** Models the **opponent-process theory** of human colour vision (Hering). The retina has red-green opponent channels. Positive a\* means more red than green.
- **b\* — Yellow/Blue:** Similarly models the yellow-blue opponent channel. Positive b\* = more yellow than blue.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X = k \int_{380}^{780} R(\lambda)\, \bar{x}(\lambda)\, S(\lambda)\, d\lambda$ | Tristimulus X | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Y = k \int_{380}^{780} R(\lambda)\, \bar{y}(\lambda)\, S(\lambda)\, d\lambda$ | Tristimulus Y | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Z = k \int_{380}^{780} R(\lambda)\, \bar{z}(\lambda)\, S(\lambda)\, d\lambda$ | Tristimulus Z | dimensionless |
| 4 | $L^{*} = 116\, f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16$ | Lightness | 0 ≤ L* ≤ 100 |
| 5 | $a^{*} = 500 \left[\, f\!\left(\dfrac{X}{X_n}\right) - f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right) \right]$ | Red/Green | typically −128 to +127 |
| 6 | $b^{*} = 200 \left[\, f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - f\!\left(\dfrac{Z}{Z_n}\right) \right]$ | Yellow/Blue | typically −128 to +127 |
| 7 | $f(t) = \begin{cases} t^{1/3} & \text{if } t > \delta^{3} \\ \dfrac{t}{3\delta^{2}} + \dfrac{4}{29} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Non-linear compression, $\delta = 6/29$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{(a^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{2}}$ | Chroma (saturation) | 0 to ~150 |
| 9 | $h_{ab} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{b^{*}}{a^{*}}\right)$ | Hue angle | 0°–360° |
| 10 | $\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$ | CIE 1976 total colour difference | ≥ 0 |
| 11 | $\Delta L^{*} = L^{*}_{sample} - L^{*}_{ref}$ | Lightness difference | — |
| 12 | $\Delta a^{*} = a^{*}_{sample} - a^{*}_{ref}$ | Red/green difference | — |
| 13 | $\Delta b^{*} = b^{*}_{sample} - b^{*}_{ref}$ | Yellow/blue difference | — |
| 14 | $\Delta E \approx 1$ | "Just noticeable difference" (JND) for trained observers | — |
| 15 | $\Delta E > 2$ | Perceptible at a glance (consumer level) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition for f(t):** The constant $\delta = 6/29 \approx 0.2069$ is the breakpoint. Below this value, the linear branch prevents the cube-root from producing wildly negative L\* values for very dark samples (e.g., roasted coffee).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Baking industry:** Monitors crust browning. As Maillard reaction proceeds, L\* drops and b\* rises (yellowness increases).
- **Tomato paste production:** Tracks lycopene development via increasing a\*.
- **Dairy:** Detects lipolysis (white→yellow shift) via rising b\*.
- **Meat industry:** Measures bloom (myoglobin oxygenation) — a\* rises sharply when fresh meat is cut.
- **Roasted coffee:** Final bean colour is graded by **Agtron disc** (commercial scale) and cross-calibrated to L\* values of approximately **20–28** for typical medium roasts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — From Spectral Reflectance to L\*a\*b\*

#### Step 1: Compute Tristimulus Values

The food sample's spectral reflectance is **R(λ)**. Combined with the illuminant spectral power distribution **S(λ)** and the CIE observer functions, the tristimulus values are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= k \int_{380}^{780} R(\lambda)\, \bar{x}(\lambda)\, S(\lambda)\, d\lambda \\
Y &= k \int_{380}^{780} R(\lambda)\, \bar{y}(\lambda)\, S(\lambda)\, d\lambda \\
Z &= k \int_{380}^{780} R(\lambda)\, \bar{z}(\lambda)\, S(\lambda)\, d\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The normalising constant $k$ is chosen so that **Y = 100** for a perfect white diffuser. The integration is over the entire visible spectrum (380–780 nm), typically evaluated every 10 nm in modern instruments.

#### Step 2: Form Ratios to Reference White

Define ratios using the illuminant's white-point values $(X_n, Y_n, Z_n)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_r &= \frac{X}{X_n}, \quad y_r = \frac{Y}{Y_n}, \quad z_r = \frac{Z}{Z_n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Dividing by the white-point normalises the colour against the illuminant. For D65: $X_n = 95.047$, $Y_n = 100.000$, $Z_n = 108.883$.

#### Step 3: Apply the Non-linear Compression Function f(t)

$$
f(t) = \begin{cases} t^{1/3}, & \text{if } t > \delta^{3} = (6/29)^{3} \approx 0.008856 \\[4pt] \dfrac{t}{3\delta^{2}} + \dfrac{4}{29}, & \text{if } t \le 0.008856 \end{cases}
$$

**Explanation:** The cube-root implements the perceptual uniformity. The linear branch is added for very dark samples (so the slope of f at t = 0 matches the slope of $t^{1/3}$ at $t = \delta^3$, ensuring **continuity and differentiability** at the breakpoint).

#### Step 4: Compose L\*, a\*, b\*

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^{*} &= 116\, f(y_r) - 16 \\
a^{*} &= 500\, [\,f(x_r) - f(y_r)\,] \\
b^{*} &= 200\, [\,f(y_r) - f(z_r)\,]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The constants 116, 500, and 200 are scaling factors chosen so that L\* = 0 corresponds to Y/Yn = 0 (black) and L\* = 100 corresponds to Y/Yn = 1 (white). The 500 and 200 weights balance the red-green and yellow-blue opponent channels.

#### Step 5: Compute Colour Difference ΔE\*ab

For a sample (subscript *s*) and a reference (subscript *r*):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L^{*} &= L^{*}_{s} - L^{*}_{r} \\
\Delta a^{*} &= a^{*}_{s} - a^{*}_{r} \\
\Delta b^{*} &= b^{*}_{s} - a^{*}_{r} \\
\Delta E^{*}_{ab} &= \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the **CIE 1976** colour difference formula. A "just-noticeable difference" is approximately **ΔE = 1** for trained observers. ΔE ≥ 2 is usually required for a casual observer to perceive a difference.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-style

**Problem:** A fruit juice sample yields tristimulus values $X = 41.30$, $Y = 36.20$, $Z = 8.95$ under D65 illuminant. Compute L\*, a\*, b\*, Chroma, Hue angle, and interpret.

**Given:** $X_n = 95.047$, $Y_n = 100.000$, $Z_n = 108.883$.

**Step A — Compute ratios:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_r &= \frac{41.30}{95.047} = 0.4345 \\
y_r &= \frac{36.20}{100.000} = 0.3620 \\
z_r &= \frac{8.95}{108.883} = 0.0822
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Apply f(t) using cube root branch (all > 0.008856):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x_r) &= (0.4345)^{1/3} = 0.7573 \\
f(y_r) &= (0.3620)^{1/3} = 0.7128 \\
f(z_r) &= (0.0822)^{1/3} = 0.4349
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Compute L\*, a\*, b\*:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^{*} &= 116 \times 0.7128 - 16 = 82.68 - 16 = \mathbf{66.68} \\
a^{*} &= 500 \times (0.7573 - 0.7128) = 500 \times 0.0445 = \mathbf{+22.25} \\
b^{*} &= 200 \times (0.7128 - 0.4349) = 200 \times 0.2779 = \mathbf{+55.58}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Compute Chroma and Hue:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C^{*}_{ab} &= \sqrt{(22.25)^{2} + (55.58)^{2}} = \sqrt{495.06 + 3089.14} = \mathbf{59.87} \\
h_{ab} &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{55.58}{22.25}\right) = \arctan(2.498) = \mathbf{68.2^{\circ}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Interpretation:**

- L\* = 66.68 → moderately light sample.
- a\* = +22.25 → distinctly red.
- b\* = +55.58 → strongly yellow.
- Hue 68.2° sits in the **orange quadrant** (0° = red, 90° = yellow, 180° = green, 270° = blue).
- This is consistent with a typical **orange juice** or **mango pulp** colour profile.

> [!NOTE]
> **CIE hue quadrant reference:** 0°–90° = red→orange→yellow, 90°–180° = yellow→yellow-green, 180°–270° = green→blue-green, 270°–360° = blue→purple→red.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choice of L*a*b* Equipment

| Equipment Type | Principle | Cost | Best Food Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tristimulus colorimeter** | 3 filtered photodiodes matching $\bar{x}\bar{y}\bar{z}$ | Low | Production-line QC of juices, sauces |
| **Spectrophotometer** | Full spectrum scan 380–780 nm | High | R&D, shelf-life, research papers |
| **Handheld L*a*b* meter** | Portable tristimulus | Medium | Field surveys of fruit maturity |
| **Online/inline colorimeter** | Continuous flow-through | Very high | Dairy, beverage bottling plants |
| **Image-based colour** | Camera + software (CIELAB from RGB) | Variable | Surface colour mapping, marbling of meat |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Instrumental Colour Measurement Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Illuminant D65 Standard Light Source] --> B[Food Sample Surface]
    B --> C[Reflected Transmitted Light Wavelength 380 to 780 nm]
    C --> D[Monochromator or Filter Array]
    D --> E[Photodetector Photodiode Array]
    E --> F[Microprocessor XYZ Tristimulus Computation]
    F --> G[Non Linear f t Transform]
    G --> H[CIELAB L Star a Star b Star Output]
    H --> I{Decision Branch}
    I --> J[Compare with Reference Standard]
    I --> K[Compute Delta E Colour Difference]
    I --> L[Compute Chroma and Hue Angle]
    J --> M[Quality Control Report]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    style A fill:#fff4cc
    style H fill:#cce5ff
    style M fill:#d4edda
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Components of a Spectrophotometric Colorimeter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Power Supply Unit] --> P2[Stabilised Tungsten or Xenon Lamp]
    P2 --> P3[Integrating Sphere Homogenises Light]
    P3 --> P4[Sample Port Holder]
    P4 --> P5[Monochromator Grating or LED Array]
    P5 --> P6[Detector Silicon Photodiode]
    P6 --> P7[Amplifier and ADC Converter]
    P7 --> P8[Microcontroller Unit MCU]
    P8 --> P9[Display LCD or PC Interface]
    P8 --> P10[Memory Storage of Reference and Sample]
    P8 --> P11[Delta E Computation Engine]
    style P1 fill:#f8d7da
    style P8 fill:#cce5ff
    style P11 fill:#d4edda
```

### 4.3 3D Logical Map of the L\*a\*b\* Colour Space

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LAB [CIELAB Colour Space Cube]
        direction LR
        subgraph AXISY [b Star Axis]
            BPOS[Positive Yellow Direction 90 degrees] --- BNEG[Negative Blue Direction 270 degrees]
        end
        subgraph AXISX [a Star Axis]
            APOS[Positive Red Direction 0 degrees] --- ANEG[Negative Green Direction 180 degrees]
        end
        subgraph AXISZ [L Star Axis]
            LTOP[L 100 Pure White] --- LBOT[L 0 Pure Black]
        end
    end
    APOS --- BPOS
    ANEG --- BNEG
    LTOP --- APOS
    LBOT --- ANEG
    style LTOP fill:#ffffff
    style LBOT fill:#000000
    style APOS fill:#ff4d4d
    style ANEG fill:#4dff4d
    style BPOS fill:#ffff4d
    style BNEG fill:#4d4dff
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Which ΔE Formula to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need to compare two food samples for colour difference?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is high accuracy required for research paper?}
    Q2 -->|No - basic QC| R1[Use CIE 1976 Delta E ab formula]
    Q2 -->|Yes - texture and translucent foods| Q3{Is sample a textile or paint?}
    Q3 -->|No - it is food| R2[Use CIE 1994 Delta E 94 formula]
    Q2 -->|Highest accuracy sensory correlation| R3[Use CIEDE 2000 Delta E 00 formula]
    style R1 fill:#d4edda
    style R2 fill:#cce5ff
    style R3 fill:#fff4cc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptation note:** In food research, **CIE 1976 (ΔE\*ab)** is still the most reported metric because of its simplicity and historical comparability. **CIEDE2000** is recommended for modern publications because it corrects perceptual non-uniformity in the blue region and for neutral colours.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define the L\*a\*b\* colour system. What does each coordinate represent? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The L\*a\*b\* (CIELAB) system is a three-dimensional, device-independent colour model defined by the CIE in 1976. The three coordinates are:
- **L\*** (Lightness): ranges from 0 (black) to 100 (white); represents the vertical axis.
- **a\*** (Red–Green axis): positive values indicate redness, negative values indicate greenness.
- **b\*** (Yellow–Blue axis): positive values indicate yellowness, negative values indicate blueness.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. List any three instruments used for colour measurement in food products. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Tristimulus colorimeter** — uses three filtered photodetectors matching the CIE $\bar{x}\bar{y}\bar{z}$ observer functions; rapid and inexpensive.
2. **Spectrophotometer** — measures full reflectance spectrum R(λ) from 380–780 nm; highly accurate and provides spectral information.
3. **Handheld L\*a\*b\* meter** — portable tristimulus device for field measurement of fruits, vegetables, and meat.
*(Alternative: image-based colour analysis systems, gloss meters, online colorimeters.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 3, ESE Pattern]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a **spectrophotometric colorimeter** used for food colour measurement. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the **L\*a\*b\* coordinates from the CIE XYZ tristimulus values** for a given food sample under D65 illuminant. State the boundary conditions clearly. (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer — Q. A

**(a) Working principle of a spectrophotometric colorimeter (7 Marks):**

A spectrophotometric colorimeter measures the spectral reflectance of a food sample over the visible range 380–780 nm.

**Block diagram:**

```
[Stabilised Light Source] → [Monochromator (grating)] 
   → [Beam splitter] → [Sample + Reference paths] 
   → [Photodetector] → [Amplifier] → [ADC] 
   → [Microprocessor] → [Display/PC]
```

**Working steps:**
1. **Light source:** A stabilised tungsten-halogen or xenon lamp provides continuous visible spectrum.
2. **Monochromator:** A diffraction grating disperses the light into narrow wavelength bands (typically 5–10 nm bandwidth).
3. **Sample illumination:** Monochromatic light strikes the food sample; reflected light intensity **R(λ)** is captured.
4. **Reference path:** A barium sulphate or Spectralon white tile is measured to give 100% baseline reflectance.
5. **Detector:** A photomultiplier tube (PMT) or photodiode array measures the intensity of reflected light.
6. **Computation:** The microprocessor integrates R(λ) weighted by $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$ to give X, Y, Z, then transforms to L\*, a\*, b\*.
7. **Output:** Numerical L\*a\*b\* values are displayed and stored.

**(b) Derivation of L\*a\*b\* from XYZ (7 Marks):**

Step 1: Compute ratios to the illuminant white point (Xn, Yn, Zn) under D65:
$x_r = X/X_n$, $y_r = Y/Y_n$, $z_r = Z/Z_n$

Step 2: Apply the non-linear cube-root function:
$$f(t) = \begin{cases} t^{1/3}, & t > (6/29)^3 = 0.008856 \\ t/(3\delta^2) + 4/29, & t \le 0.008856 \end{cases}$$

Step 3: Compute the three coordinates:
$$L^{*} = 116 f(y_r) - 16$$
$$a^{*} = 500 [f(x_r) - f(y_r)]$$
$$b^{*} = 200 [f(y_r) - f(z_r)]$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- L\* = 0 when Y/Yn = 0 (perfect black).
- L\* = 100 when Y/Yn = 1 (perfect white diffuser).
- a\* and b\* are unbounded but practically fall in the range −128 to +127.
- The function f(t) is continuous and differentiable at the breakpoint t = δ³, ensuring smoothness for dark samples.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define **chroma (C\*ab)**, **hue angle (hab)**, and **total colour difference (ΔE\*ab)**. Give the formulae. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A tomato paste sample has L\* = 32.4, a\* = 28.6, b\* = 19.7. The reference standard is L\* = 30.0, a\* = 30.0, b\* = 20.0. Compute ΔE\*ab and state whether the difference is perceptible to a consumer. (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer — Q. B

**(a) Definitions and formulae (7 Marks):**

1. **Chroma (C\*ab):** A measure of colour saturation or intensity — the distance of a colour from the neutral grey axis (L\* axis) in the a\*–b\* plane.
$$C^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{(a^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{2}}$$
Higher chroma = more vivid/saturated colour.

2. **Hue angle (hab):** The angular position of the colour in the a\*–b\* plane, expressed in degrees.
$$h_{ab} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{b^{*}}{a^{*}}\right)$$
Range: 0° (red) → 90° (yellow) → 180° (green) → 270° (blue) → 360° (red).

3. **Total colour difference (ΔE\*ab):** The Euclidean distance between sample and reference points in the L\*a\*b\* space.
$$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$$

**(b) Numerical computation (7 Marks):**

Given:
- Sample: L\*s = 32.4, a\*s = 28.6, b\*s = 19.7
- Reference: L\*r = 30.0, a\*r = 30.0, b\*r = 20.0

**Step 1:** Compute differences:
$$\Delta L^{*} = 32.4 - 30.0 = +2.4$$
$$\Delta a^{*} = 28.6 - 30.0 = -1.4$$
$$\Delta b^{*} = 19.7 - 20.0 = -0.3$$

**Step 2:** Square each difference:
$$(\Delta L^{*})^{2} = 5.76$$
$$(\Delta a^{*})^{2} = 1.96$$
$$(\Delta b^{*})^{2} = 0.09$$

**Step 3:** Sum and take square root:
$$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{5.76 + 1.96 + 0.09} = \sqrt{7.81} = \mathbf{2.79}$$

**Interpretation:** ΔE = 2.79 is **above the consumer-perceptibility threshold of 2.0** and well above the JND of 1.0. Hence, the colour difference **will be visually noticeable by an average consumer** — the product is **slightly lighter (ΔL\* = +2.4) and slightly less red (Δa\* = −1.4)** than the reference standard. The batch may need re-blending or process adjustment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary condition** in f(t) when Y/Yn is very small (< 0.008856) — students apply cube root blindly and get physically impossible L\* values.
> 2. **Mixing up ΔL\*, Δa\*, Δb\* sign convention.** Always subtract reference FROM sample: $\Delta = \text{sample} - \text{reference}$.
> 3. **Reporting ΔE without units** — always state the formula version (CIE 1976 / 1994 / 2000).
> 4. **Confusing Hunter Lab (L, a, b) with CIE L\*a\*b\*.** Hunter Lab uses different equations; do not interchange.
> 5. **Omitting the illuminant and observer angle** in the answer — examiners expect "D65, 10° observer" for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- **L\*a\*b\* is device-independent** — unlike RGB, it is not tied to a monitor or printer.
- **L\*** is the **lightness** axis (0 → 100); **a\*** is red–green; **b\*** is yellow–blue.
- **Illuminant D65** is the default for food colour; **10° observer** is the standard supplementary observer.
- **Chroma** quantifies saturation; **hue angle** quantifies the dominant colour tone.
- **ΔE\*ab ≥ 1** = JND; **ΔE ≥ 2** = consumer-level perceptible difference; **ΔE > 3.5** = clearly different.
- **Tristimulus colorimeters** → fast, cheap, production-line; **spectrophotometers** → research-grade, full spectrum.
- The non-linear function **f(t)** in CIELAB implements the cube-root law of human brightness perception.
- Always state **illuminant + observer angle** when reporting colour values in lab reports and KTU answers.
- For food research publications, prefer **CIEDE2000** over CIE 1976 ΔE for better perceptual uniformity.
- The L*a*b* system can be **converted to and from Hunter Lab, XYZ, RGB, and CMYK** — useful in food-packaging design.
- **Reflectance mode** (R) is used for opaque foods (bread, meat, chocolate); **transmittance mode** (T) for clear liquids (juice, oil, wine).
- **Gloss and surface texture** can affect L\* readings — always use an **integrating sphere** to capture diffuse reflectance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of Statistical Analysis and Its Importance in the Food Processing Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT524 - SENSORY EVALUATION OF FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Definition of Statistical Analysis and Its Importance in the Food Processing Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Analysis** is the scientific discipline of **collecting, organizing, summarizing, analyzing, interpreting, and drawing inferences from numerical data**, with the objective of making informed decisions under conditions of uncertainty.

In the context of **Sensory Evaluation of Food Products (PEFTT524)**, statistical analysis refers to the systematic application of mathematical and probabilistic techniques to data generated through **human sensory panels** (descriptive, discriminative, and affective tests) and **instrumental measurements**, in order to:

1. Summarize sensory responses (hedonic scores, intensity ratings, preference rankings).
2. Test hypotheses about product differences, consumer acceptance, and shelf-life behavior.
3. Establish relationships between sensory attributes, instrumental data, and consumer liking.
4. Validate experimental designs and minimize the influence of bias, noise, and chance variation.

The two principal branches of statistics applied in food processing are:

| Branch | Purpose | Typical Application in Food Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Descriptive Statistics** | Summarizes raw data into meaningful metrics | Mean sensory score, standard deviation of taste ratings |
| **Inferential Statistics** | Draws conclusions about a population from a sample | ANOVA to compare 5 formulations, t-test for shelf-life studies |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Judge's Plate" Analogy:**
> Imagine a food judge tasting 10 samples of a new mango juice. The judge's brain receives a flood of impressions — sweetness, aroma, texture, aftertaste — for each sample. Without a structured method, the judge would just say *"some are sweeter than others."*
>
> **Statistical analysis acts as the judge's "Mathematical Notebook."** It converts fuzzy sensory impressions into **numbers, charts, and probabilities** so that the differences can be **measured, verified, and trusted**. Just as a weighing scale turns the abstract idea of "heavy" into the exact figure of "12.5 kg," statistics turns "this tastes better" into "Product B scored 7.2 versus Product A's 6.4, and this difference is 95 % likely to be real, not due to chance."

## 1.3 Why Statistics Is Indispensable in the Food Processing Industry

The food industry operates on **sensory data that is inherently variable** — different panelists, different days, different batches. Statistics provides the **objective decision-making framework** that the industry relies upon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensory data is uniquely noisy because:**
> - Human panelists are biological instruments with daily mood/physiological fluctuations.
> - Food products vary batch-to-batch due to raw material seasonality.
> - Environmental conditions (temperature, lighting, fatigue) affect responses.
>
> Statistics **quantifies and controls this noise**, transforming subjective judgment into defensible, reproducible engineering decisions.

**Key Standard Metrics Used (Industry Conventions):**
- **Significance Level (α):** Conventionally set at **0.05** (5 % risk of false positive).
- **Confidence Level:** Typically **95 %** in food research.
- **Hedonic Scale Range:** **9-point** (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely) — the most widely accepted industry standard.
- **Minimum Panel Size:** **30 untrained consumers** for affective tests (per **ISO 11136** and **ASTM E1964**).
- **Trained Panel Size:** **8 – 12 panelists** for descriptive analysis (per **ISO 13299**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Statistical Analysis

The application of statistical analysis in the food industry follows a structured 6-step logical pipeline. Each step is non-negotiable for valid sensory data interpretation.

**Step 1 — Problem Definition**
Clearly state the research question (e.g., *"Is the new formulation (F2) significantly sweeter than the control (F1)?"*). This converts a vague hypothesis into a **testable statistical hypothesis** with a null (H₀) and alternative (H₁).

**Step 2 — Experimental Design Selection**
Choose the design that matches the question: **Randomized Block Design (RBD)** for panelist-as-block, **Two-Factor ANOVA** for formulation × storage, **Split-Plot** for process × packaging interactions.

**Step 3 — Data Collection**
Execute the sensory test under **controlled conditions** (ISO 8589 sensory booths, 21 ± 1 °C, red lighting to mask color differences, neutralized palate with water and unsalted crackers).

**Step 4 — Descriptive Statistics Computation**
Calculate **mean (x̄), median, mode, range, variance (s²), and standard deviation (s)**. These summarize central tendency and dispersion of the raw sensory scores.

**Step 5 — Inferential Statistics Application**
Apply the appropriate test — **t-test, ANOVA, Chi-square, Mann-Whitney U, or Kruskal-Wallis** — to determine if observed differences are **statistically significant** or merely due to random chance.

**Step 6 — Interpretation and Decision**
Compare the computed **p-value** with the pre-set α (typically 0.05). If p ≤ α, **reject H₀** and conclude a real difference exists. This becomes the basis for the **Go/No-Go commercialization decision**.

## 2.2 Importance in the Food Processing Industry — A Structured View

The strategic importance of statistical analysis in the food industry can be grouped into **five functional pillars**:

| Pillar | Engineering Function | Real-World Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Control (QC)** | Monitor batch consistency | SPC charts tracking acidity of yogurt over 50 batches |
| **Product Development (R&D)** | Compare prototypes | ANOVA of 4 new jam formulations on 9-pt hedonic scale |
| **Shelf-Life Studies** | Track degradation kinetics | Regression of vitamin C loss vs. storage time |
| **Consumer Research** | Validate market acceptance | Preference mapping correlating sensory to liking data |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Defend product claims | Statistical proof supporting "low sodium" sensory equivalence |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following are the **essential formulas** tested in KTU examinations for statistical analysis in sensory evaluation. All notation follows **ISO 5495** and **ASTM** conventions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | x̄ = mean score; xᵢ = i-th observation; n = sample size |
| 2 | Median (odd n) | $\text{Median} = x_{\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)}$ | Sorted middle value |
| 3 | Variance (sample) | $s^{2} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}{n-1}$ | s² = variance; denominator n-1 = degrees of freedom (Bessel's correction) |
| 4 | Standard Deviation | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}{n-1}}$ | s in same units as x; measures spread |
| 5 | Coefficient of Variation (CV) | $CV = \dfrac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\,\%$ | CV in %; CV < 15 % = acceptable panel reproducibility |
| 6 | Standard Error of Mean (SEM) | $SEM = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | SEM quantifies precision of the mean estimate |
| 7 | Confidence Interval (95 %) | $CI_{95\%} = \bar{x} \pm t_{0.025,\,n-1} \cdot SEM$ | t = Student's t critical value |
| 8 | Student's t-test (independent) | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}}$ | sₚ = pooled standard deviation |
| 9 | Pooled Standard Deviation | $s_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{(n_1-1)s_1^{2} + (n_2-1)s_2^{2}}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}}$ | For two-sample t-test |
| 10 | One-Way ANOVA F-statistic | $F = \dfrac{MS_{\text{between}}}{MS_{\text{within}}} = \dfrac{SS_B / (k-1)}{SS_W / (N-k)}$ | k = number of groups; N = total observations |
| 11 | Sum of Squares (Total) | $SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{2} - \dfrac{\left(\sum x_i\right)^{2}}{N}$ | Total variability |
| 12 | Chi-Square Goodness of Fit | $\chi^{2} = \sum \dfrac{(O - E)^{2}}{E}$ | O = observed; E = expected frequencies |
| 13 | Pearson Correlation | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}}$ | r ranges from -1 to +1 |
| 14 | Linear Regression Slope | $b = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$ | y = a + bx |
| 15 | Likert/Hedonic Mean Acceptance | $\bar{H} = \dfrac{\sum s_i \cdot f_i}{\sum f_i}$ | sᵢ = score; fᵢ = frequency of that score |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** KTU questions frequently test **only formulas 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 10**. Master these eight thoroughly. **Never use the vertical pipe symbol $\vert x \vert$** in tables — use $\vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Process Engineering:** Statistical analysis of moisture content data across batches enables **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts that detect process drift before defective products are produced.
- **Packaging Engineering:** ANOVA determines whether packaging material (factor A) and storage temperature (factor B) significantly affect sensory crispness.
- **Supply Chain:** Regression models predict shelf-life loss as a function of temperature and humidity — supporting **cold chain logistics** decisions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Full Descriptive Statistics from Sensory Scores

**Problem Statement:**
A trained panel of **n = 9** evaluators scored a new protein bar on a 9-point hedonic scale for **overall acceptability**. The raw scores were:
**8, 7, 9, 6, 7, 8, 5, 7, 8**.
Compute the mean, median, variance, standard deviation, CV, SEM, and 95 % confidence interval.

---

### Step 1 — Sort the Data (Median Calculation)

$$
\text{Sorted scores: } 5, 6, 7, 7, 7, 8, 8, 8, 9
$$

Since n = 9 is odd, the median is the (n+1)/2 = 5th value.

$$
\text{Median} = x_{(5)} = 7
$$

The **median = 7**. Interpretation: half the panelists scored ≤ 7 and half ≥ 7.

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Arithmetic Mean (x̄)

Sum all nine scores:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{9} x_i = 5 + 6 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 8 + 8 + 8 + 9 = 65
$$

Apply Formula 1 from the cheat sheet:

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n} = \dfrac{65}{9} = 7.222\ldots
$$

Rounded to two decimals:

$$
\bar{x} = 7.22
$$

Interpretation: the average acceptability score is **7.22** on a 9-point scale, which corresponds to the qualitative descriptor **"Like Moderately"** on the standard hedonic scale.

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Squared Deviations (for Variance)

For each score, compute (xᵢ − x̄)². With x̄ = 7.2222:

| xᵢ | xᵢ − x̄ | (xᵢ − x̄)² |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | −2.2222 | 4.9383 |
| 6 | −1.2222 | 1.4938 |
| 7 | −0.2222 | 0.0494 |
| 7 | −0.2222 | 0.0494 |
| 7 | −0.2222 | 0.0494 |
| 8 | +0.7778 | 0.6049 |
| 8 | +0.7778 | 0.6049 |
| 8 | +0.7778 | 0.6049 |
| 9 | +1.7778 | 3.1605 |

Sum the squared deviations:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{9} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} = 4.9383 + 1.4938 + 5 \times 0.0494 + 3 \times 0.6049 + 3.1605
$$

$$
= 4.9383 + 1.4938 + 0.2470 + 1.8147 + 3.1605 = 11.6543
$$

---

### Step 4 — Compute the Sample Variance (s²)

Apply Formula 3 with denominator n − 1 = 8 (Bessel's correction):

$$
s^{2} = \dfrac{11.6543}{9 - 1} = \dfrac{11.6543}{8} = 1.4568
$$

Interpretation: the variance is **1.4568 score²- units**. The data has moderate spread around the mean.

---

### Step 5 — Compute the Standard Deviation (s)

Apply Formula 4 by taking the square root:

$$
s = \sqrt{1.4568} = 1.2070
$$

Interpretation: on average, individual scores deviate by **±1.21 points** from the mean of 7.22. Since the scale is 1–9, this is a **moderate spread** — panelists reasonably agree.

---

### Step 6 — Compute the Coefficient of Variation (CV)

Apply Formula 5:

$$
CV = \dfrac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\,\% = \dfrac{1.2070}{7.2222} \times 100\,\% = 16.71\,\%
$$

Interpretation: **CV = 16.71 %**. This is **slightly above the 15 % benchmark** for trained panel reproducibility. The panel leader may wish to **retrain** the evaluators on the attribute scale.

---

### Step 7 — Compute the Standard Error of Mean (SEM)

Apply Formula 6:

$$
SEM = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{1.2070}{\sqrt{9}} = \dfrac{1.2070}{3} = 0.4023
$$

Interpretation: the precision of the mean estimate is **±0.40 points** at one standard error.

---

### Step 8 — Compute the 95 % Confidence Interval

For n = 9, degrees of freedom df = n − 1 = 8. The two-tailed critical t value at α = 0.05 is:

$$
t_{0.025,\,8} = 2.306
$$

Apply Formula 7:

$$
CI_{95\%} = \bar{x} \pm t_{0.025,\,8} \cdot SEM = 7.2222 \pm (2.306 \times 0.4023)
$$

$$
CI_{95\%} = 7.2222 \pm 0.9277
$$

$$
\text{Lower limit} = 7.2222 - 0.9277 = 6.2945
$$

$$
\text{Upper limit} = 7.2222 + 0.9277 = 8.1499
$$

Final result:

$$
CI_{95\%} = [\,6.29,\ 8.15\,]
$$

**Engineering Decision:** The R&D team can be 95 % confident that the true population mean acceptability for this protein bar lies between **6.29 ("Like Slightly")** and **8.15 ("Like Very Much")**. Since the lower bound is above the neutral midpoint of 5, the product has **demonstrated consumer acceptability** and can proceed to scale-up trials.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Linking Statistical Tools to Sensory Tests

| Sensory Test Type | Data Type | Best Descriptive Statistic | Correct Inferential Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Paired Preference** (2 products) | Binary (1/0) | Proportion choosing A | Chi-square (1 df) or McNemar |
| **Triangle Test** | Binary (correct/incorrect) | Proportion correct | Chi-square or binomial test |
| **Hedonic 9-pt** (2+ products) | Ordinal/interval | Mean, Median, CV | ANOVA + Tukey HSD |
| **Ranking Test** | Ordinal ranks | Median rank | Friedman test (non-parametric) |
| **Descriptive Profile** | Interval (line scale) | Mean, SEM per attribute | Two-way ANOVA (panelist × product) |
| **Time-Intensity** | Continuous curve | Area under curve (AUC) | Repeated-measures ANOVA |
| **Shelf-life** | Repeated measures | Mean + regression slope | Linear regression + t-test on slope |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Insight:** The choice of statistical test depends on **data type** (nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio) and **number of groups**. Students who incorrectly apply ANOVA to ranked (ordinal) data **lose 2 marks** automatically under KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Statistical Analysis in the Food Industry

The following Mermaid block maps the **decision-flow architecture** of how statistical analysis is integrated into the food processing industry pipeline. Each node is purely alphanumeric to ensure clean Mermaid compilation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Food Industry Need] --> nodeB[Define Research Question]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Select Experimental Design}
    nodeC -->|Two products| nodeD[Paired or Triangle Test]
    nodeC -->|Three plus products| nodeE[ANOVA Design]
    nodeC -->|Over time| nodeF[Repeated Measures Design]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Collect Sensory Data]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Data Cleaning and Outlier Check]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compute Descriptive Statistics]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{Choose Inferential Test}
    nodeJ -->|Normal data| nodeK[Parametric Test t or F]
    nodeJ -->|Skewed ranked data| nodeL[Non Parametric Test]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Calculate p value]
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN{p less than alpha}
    nodeN -->|Yes reject H0| nodeO[Conclude Real Difference Exists]
    nodeN -->|No fail to reject| nodeP[Conclude No Significant Difference]
    nodeO --> nodeQ[Commercial Decision]
    nodeP --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Quality Control Release]
    nodeQ --> nodeS[Product Reformulation]
    nodeQ --> nodeT[Shelf Life Approval]
    nodeR --> nodeU[Feedback to Process Engineering]
    nodeS --> nodeU
    nodeT --> nodeU
```

## 4.2 Pillar Diagram — The Five Functional Pillars of Statistical Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    statCore[Statistical Analysis Engine] --> pillar1[Quality Control and SPC]
    statCore --> pillar2[Product Development and R and D]
    statCore --> pillar3[Shelf Life and Stability]
    statCore --> pillar4[Consumer and Market Research]
    statCore --> pillar5[Regulatory and Claims Support]
    pillar1 --> benefit1[Batch Consistency and Reduced Rejection]
    pillar2 --> benefit2[Faster Prototype Screening]
    pillar3 --> benefit3[Accurate Expiry Date Setting]
    pillar4 --> benefit4[Validated Market Launch]
    pillar5 --> benefit5[Defensible Label Claims]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each box (node) represents a **process step** or **functional outcome**, and each arrow represents a **data flow** or **decision transition**. In KTU descriptive questions, students may reproduce this architecture for **3 marks** by simply listing the five pillars with one industrial example each.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define statistical analysis. List any four reasons why it is important in the food processing industry.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Statistical analysis is the scientific methodology of collecting, organizing, summarizing, analyzing, and interpreting numerical data to draw valid conclusions and make decisions under uncertainty.

**Four reasons for importance in the food processing industry:**

1. **Quality Control:** Enables monitoring of batch-to-batch variation in sensory and physico-chemical attributes using Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts.

2. **Product Development:** Allows objective comparison of multiple formulations, ingredients, or process variants through ANOVA and post-hoc tests, accelerating R&D decisions.

3. **Shelf-Life Determination:** Quantifies the rate of sensory and chemical degradation over time through regression analysis, supporting accurate expiry-date assignment.

4. **Consumer Acceptability Validation:** Provides statistical proof (significance testing at p ≤ 0.05) that a new product is accepted by the target consumer population, reducing market launch risk.

*(Note: A 4th or 5th reason can include Regulatory Compliance, Cost Reduction via optimized formulations, or Process Optimization.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between descriptive statistics and inferential statistics with one food-sensory example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Descriptive Statistics | Inferential Statistics |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Summarizes and describes the features of a dataset | Draws conclusions about a population from a sample |
| **Tools** | Mean, median, mode, range, variance, standard deviation, CV | t-test, ANOVA, Chi-square, regression, correlation |
| **Output** | Numerical summary (no probability statement) | Probability value (p-value) and confidence interval |
| **Food-sensory example** | A new jam formulation receives an average hedonic score of x̄ = 7.4 ± 1.1 from 30 consumers | A t-test confirms that the 7.4 score is significantly higher (p = 0.012) than the control's 6.6, with 95 % confidence |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define statistical analysis. Explain in detail the **five functional pillars** of its application in the food processing industry with one industrial example per pillar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sensory panel of **n = 10** judges scored a new chocolate on a 9-point hedonic scale for overall liking. The raw scores were:
**6, 8, 7, 9, 7, 8, 6, 7, 8, 9**.
Compute the **mean, median, variance, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation**. Comment on the panel's reproducibility. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Definition and Five Pillars (7 marks):**

Statistical analysis is the systematic process of collecting, organizing, summarizing, analyzing, and interpreting data — with the goal of making reliable decisions under uncertainty. In the food processing industry, it is the **objective decision-support backbone** that converts subjective sensory responses into defensible engineering and commercial decisions.

**The five functional pillars are:**

**Pillar 1 — Quality Control (QC):**
Statistical analysis is used in **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** to monitor critical sensory and physico-chemical parameters (e.g., moisture, pH, color, texture) batch after batch. X-bar and R-charts detect process drift. *Example:* Monitoring the Brix value of orange juice every 2 hours; if values fall outside ±3σ control limits, the batch is held for investigation.

**Pillar 2 — Product Development and R&D:**
When a company develops multiple prototypes, ANOVA and post-hoc tests (Tukey HSD) identify the best-performing formulation. *Example:* A beverage company tests 4 new flavor variants on 60 consumers; ANOVA on hedonic scores reveals variant C is significantly preferred (p = 0.003).

**Pillar 3 — Shelf-Life and Stability Studies:**
Regression analysis models the kinetics of quality loss over storage time. The time at which the sensory score drops below the acceptance threshold (typically 5 on a 9-point scale) defines the shelf life. *Example:* Linear regression of vitamin C content in stored guava juice vs. days at 30 °C yields a slope of −0.42 mg/day, enabling shelf-life prediction.

**Pillar 4 — Consumer and Market Research:**
Survey data, preference mapping, and cluster analysis help segment consumers and identify target markets. *Example:* External Preference Mapping (PrefMap) correlates instrumental texture data with consumer liking, revealing that consumers in the 18–25 age cluster prefer softer biscuits.

**Pillar 5 — Regulatory and Claims Support:**
Statistical equivalence testing (e.g., two-one-sided t-tests, TOST) supports label claims such as "same great taste with 30 % less sodium." *Example:* Paired preference test with 100 consumers shows no significant preference (p = 0.42) between the low-sodium and standard formulation, supporting a "taste equivalent" claim.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given data: **6, 8, 7, 9, 7, 8, 6, 7, 8, 9**, with n = 10.

**Step 1 — Sum of observations:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 6 + 8 + 7 + 9 + 7 + 8 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 9 = 75
$$

**Step 2 — Mean:**

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{75}{10} = 7.50
$$

**Step 3 — Median:**
Sorted data: 6, 6, 7, 7, 7, 7, 8, 8, 8, 9.
For n = 10 (even), median = average of 5th and 6th values = (7 + 7)/2 = **7.00**.

**Step 4 — Squared deviations from mean (x̄ = 7.5):**

| xᵢ | (xᵢ − 7.5) | (xᵢ − 7.5)² |
|---|---|---|
| 6 | −1.5 | 2.25 |
| 6 | −1.5 | 2.25 |
| 7 | −0.5 | 0.25 |
| 7 | −0.5 | 0.25 |
| 7 | −0.5 | 0.25 |
| 7 | −0.5 | 0.25 |
| 8 | +0.5 | 0.25 |
| 8 | +0.5 | 0.25 |
| 8 | +0.5 | 0.25 |
| 9 | +1.5 | 2.25 |

**Step 5 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} = 2 \times 2.25 + 4 \times 0.25 + 3 \times 0.25 + 1 \times 2.25
$$

$$
= 4.50 + 1.00 + 0.75 + 2.25 = 8.50
$$

**Step 6 — Variance (n − 1 = 9 in denominator):**

$$
s^{2} = \dfrac{8.50}{9} = 0.9444
$$

**Step 7 — Standard Deviation:**

$$
s = \sqrt{0.9444} = 0.9718
$$

**Step 8 — Coefficient of Variation:**

$$
CV = \dfrac{0.9718}{7.50} \times 100\,\% = 12.96\,\%
$$

**Comment on Reproducibility (1 mark):**
The CV = **12.96 %** is **below the 15 % benchmark** typically used in sensory science for trained panels. This indicates that the panel has **good reproducibility and low inter-judge variation**. The chocolate formulation is therefore rated consistently and the mean score of 7.5 (between "Like Slightly" and "Like Very Much") is a **reliable estimate** of consumer acceptability.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is statistical analysis? Discuss its role in **quality control, product development, and shelf-life studies** in the food industry with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The moisture content (%) of a food product measured across 8 batches is:
**12.5, 13.0, 12.8, 13.2, 12.6, 13.1, 12.9, 12.7**.
Calculate the **mean, variance, standard deviation, standard error of mean, and 95 % confidence interval** (use t₀.₀₂₅,₇ = 2.365). Comment on process consistency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution Outline to Question B

**(a)** Definition: as above. Role in **QC, R&D, and shelf-life** — covered comprehensively under the five pillars (use Pillar 1, Pillar 2, Pillar 3 from Question A). Examples as given. *(Students should write 2–3 lines per role + one example each to secure 7 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

- Sum = 12.5 + 13.0 + 12.8 + 13.2 + 12.6 + 13.1 + 12.9 + 12.7 = **102.8**
- n = 8, so x̄ = 102.8/8 = **12.85 %**
- Squared deviations sum = 0.1225 + 0.0225 + 0.0025 + 0.1225 + 0.0625 + 0.0625 + 0.0025 + 0.0225 = **0.4200**
- Variance s² = 0.4200/(8−1) = 0.4200/7 = **0.0600**
- s = √0.0600 = **0.2449 %**
- SEM = s/√n = 0.2449/√8 = 0.2449/2.828 = **0.0866 %**
