## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
*Define "accreditation" of a sensory evaluation laboratory. Name the national accreditation body in India.*

**Model Answer:**
Accreditation is the formal, third-party recognition by an authorized national or international body that a sensory evaluation laboratory has demonstrated **technical competence** and **impartiality** to perform specific sensory tests in accordance with **ISO/IEC 17025:2017**.

In India, the national accreditation body is the **National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories (NABL)**, which operates under the Quality Council of India (QCI) and is a signatory of the **ILAC MRA** (International Laboratory Accreditation Cooperation – Mutual Recognition Arrangement).

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for ISO reference, 1 for NABL)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
*List any three environmental conditions specified by ISO 8589 for a sensory test room.*

**Model Answer:**
According to **ISO 8589:2007**, the test room environment must satisfy:

1. **Temperature:** $20 \pm 2\,°C$ (constant, no draft).
2. **Relative Humidity:** $50 \pm 10\,\%$.
3. **Lighting:** Adjustable, colour-corrected (CRI $\geq 90$), and free from extraneous odours (sound level $\leq 40\,dB$).

*(3 marks — 1 mark each)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain the **management requirements** (Clause 4) of **ISO/IEC 17025:2017** as applicable to a sensory evaluation laboratory. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for obtaining NABL accreditation** for a newly established sensory laboratory. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Management Requirements of ISO/IEC 17025:2017 for a Sensory Lab:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Heading-wise Answer (7 marks = 1 mark per sub-point + 2 marks for sensory-specific examples):**

1. **Impartiality (Clause 4.1):** The lab must operate free from commercial, financial, or other undue pressures. *Sensory example:* The panel leader must not know the brand of products being tested (double-blind protocol).

2. **Confidentiality (Clause 4.2):** Customer results must be kept confidential. *Sensory example:* Only the panel leader and designated analyst see raw data.

3. **Document Control (Clause 4.3):** All SOPs, Quality Manual, and forms must have version numbers, issue dates, and approval signatures.

4. **Records (Clause 4.4):** Technical records must be retained for a defined period (typically 5 years). *Sensory example:* Panelist screening cards, ballot sheets, calibration data.

5. **Risk and Opportunity (Clause 4.6):** Identify risks (e.g., panelist dropout) and opportunities (e.g., new method validation).

6. **Internal Audits (Clause 4.13):** Must be conducted at least once every 12 months, covering all sensory test methods.

7. **Management Review (Clause 4.14):** Top management reviews the QMS at least annually — input includes PT results, customer feedback, corrective actions.

---

**(b) Step-by-Step NABL Accreditation Procedure:**

| Step | Action | Timeline |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gap Analysis** vs ISO/IEC 17025 | 1 month |
| 2 | **Quality Manual & SOP Preparation** | 2 months |
| 3 | **Implementation & Internal Audits** | 3–6 months |
| 4 | **Proficiency Testing (PT)** participation | 2 rounds |
| 5 | **NABL Application** with scope, methods, fees | 1 month |
| 6 | **Document Review** by NABL | 1–2 months |
| 7 | **Pre-Assessment (Optional)** | 1 month |
| 8 | **Final On-Site Assessment** by NABL team | 2–3 days |
| 9 | **Closure of Non-Conformities** | 1–3 months |
| 10 | **Accreditation Granted** with scope certificate | — |
| 11 | **Surveillance Audits** | Annually |
| 12 | **Re-assessment** | Every 4 years |

> *(7 marks = 1 mark for listing the 10-step flow + 1 mark for PT emphasis + 1 mark for surveillance cycle + 1 mark each for sensory-specific tie-in: ISO 8589 room, ISO 13299 methods, panel records, NABL portal reference)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
**(a)** Discuss the **technical requirements** (Clause 5) of ISO/IEC 17025 with special reference to **personnel competence** and **environmental control** in a sensory lab. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is **proficiency testing (PT)**? Explain its role in maintaining **accreditation** of a sensory laboratory, with a sample calculation of the **z-score**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Technical Requirements — Personnel & Environment:**

**Personnel Competence (Clause 5.2):**
- The **panel leader** must have a degree in Food Science/Sensory Science with **minimum 3 years of panel management experience** and certified training in **ISO 13299 methodology**.
- **Panelists** must undergo:
  - Initial screening (ISO 8586-1): taste, smell, texture sensitivity.
  - Training (ISO 8586-2): descriptor development, scale usage.
  - Periodic re-screening every 6–12 months.
- Training records, screening scoresheets, and performance charts must be retained.

**Environmental Control (Clause 5.3) — ISO 8589 Specifications:**
- **Test Room:** Isolated from preparation area; positive air pressure to prevent odour ingress.
- **Booths:** Minimum 6 individual booths, each ~$900 \times 600\,mm$, separated by partitions (to prevent visual & verbal interaction).
- **Lighting:** D65 or incandescent with adjustable intensity; red lighting for colour-masking when required.
- **Temperature:** $20 \pm 2\,°C$.
- **Sound:** $\leq 40\,dB$; acoustic insulation.
- **Sample serving through a pass-through hatch** (one-way) to prevent panelist seeing the server.

> *(7 marks — 3 for personnel, 3 for environment, 1 for document linkage)*

---

**(b) Proficiency Testing & z-Score:**

**Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proficiency Testing (PT)** is the evaluation of a laboratory's performance by **inter-laboratory comparison**, in which multiple accredited labs test the **same homogeneous sample** and the results are statistically compared against an **assigned value**.

**Role in Accreditation:**
- Demonstrates the lab's results are **statistically equivalent** to peer labs.
- Mandatory under **ISO/IEC 17025 Clause 5.9** (Quality Assurance).
- PT participation is a **pre-condition** for NABL accreditation and must be continued throughout the accreditation cycle.
- Unsatisfactory PT ($\vert z \vert \geq 3$) triggers a **mandatory root-cause investigation** and corrective action.

**z-Score Calculation:**

$$
z = \frac{x_{lab} - x_{assigned}}{\sigma_{pt}}
$$

> **Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark question staple):**
>
> In a NABL-conducted PT round for **bitterness scoring** of a reference coffee sample:
> - Lab's reported score: $x_{lab} = 4.5$ (on 9-point scale)
> - Assigned value: $x_{assigned} = 4.2$
> - Standard deviation for PT: $\sigma_{pt} = 0.4$
>
> **Solution:**
> $$
> z = \frac{4.5 - 4.2}{0.4} = \frac{0.3}{0.4} = 0.75
> $$
> Since $\vert z \vert = 0.75 \leq 2.0$, the lab's performance is **satisfactory**. ✓

> *(7 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for role, 2 for formula, 2 for numerical example with correct interpretation)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Accreditation vs Certification** — Many students write these interchangeably. **Certification** is the lab claiming it meets a standard; **Accreditation** is a *third-party* body confirming it. Always emphasize the third-party angle.
>
> 2. **Forgetting ISO 8589** — Students often cite only ISO 17025 and miss that **sensory labs have a unique physical-environment standard (ISO 8589)**. Examiners specifically test this.
>
> 3. **Skipping the "Sensory" angle** — When asked about ISO 17025, students give generic lab answers. Always anchor to **panelists, ISO 8589 room, blinding codes, ISO 13299 methods, sensory uncertainty**.
>
> 4. **Not stating the PT requirement numerically** — Examiners want $\vert z \vert \leq 2.0$ as the pass criterion. Generic statements like "PT is done" lose marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing NABL with BIS / FSSAI** — NABL accredits labs; **BIS** sets product standards; **FSSAI** regulates food safety. Mixing these is a common error.
>
> 6. **No mention of ILAC MRA** — For global acceptance, the accreditation body must be an **ILAC MRA signatory**. Missing this loses a mark in long answers.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Pin This:**

- ✅ **Core standard:** ISO/IEC 17025:2017 — competence of testing labs.
- ✅ **Sensory-specific room standard:** ISO 8589:2007.
- ✅ **Methodology standard:** ISO 13299.
- ✅ **Discriminative test standard:** ISO 5495 (triangle test).
- ✅ **Affective test standard:** ISO 8587.
- ✅ **Panel screening standards:** ISO 8586-1 (screening), ISO 8586-2 (training).
- ✅ **PT statistics standard:** ISO 13528.
- ✅ **Indian accreditation body:** **NABL** (under QCI).
- ✅ **Global mutual recognition:** **ILAC MRA**.
- ✅ **Test room conditions:** $T = 20 \pm 2\,°C$, $RH = 50 \pm 10\,\%$, sound $\leq 40\,dB$, $CRI \geq 90$.
- ✅ **Panel size:** Trained descriptive = 8–15; Consumer hedonic = $\geq 75$; Triangle test = $\geq 30$.
- ✅ **PT pass criterion:** $\vert z \vert \leq 2.0$.
- ✅ **Expanded uncertainty formula:** $U = 2 \cdot u_c$ (95% confidence).
- ✅ **Sample coding:** Always **3-digit random codes** to prevent bias.
- ✅ **Accreditation cycle:** Annual surveillance + Re-assessment every 4 years (NABL).
- ✅ **Two pillars of ISO 17025:** **Management (Clause 4)** + **Technical (Clause 5)**.
- ✅ **Most critical audit checkpoint:** **Panelist competence documentation** + **ISO 8589 test room compliance**.
- ✅ **Real-world anchors:** Kerala spice export, EU/US market access, Codex Alimentarius.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT524 - SENSORY EVALUATION OF FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of Sensory Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensory Evaluation** is the scientific discipline used to evoke, measure, analyze, and interpret human responses to food and other materials as perceived through the five senses — **sight, smell, taste, touch, and hearing**. When applied in real-world industrial, research, and commercial contexts, the *application of sensory evaluation* refers to the systematic deployment of this discipline for **decision-making in product development, quality control, marketing, and shelf-life studies** within the food and allied industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> "The application of sensory evaluation encompasses its use as a decision-making tool in new product development (NPD), quality assurance (QA), quality control (QC), shelf-life determination, cost reduction, market research, and specification development. The selection of an appropriate test (discriminative, descriptive, or affective) is dictated by the specific application objective."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef designing a new biryani** for a restaurant chain. Before launching it nationwide, you must answer several real questions:

- Is the new masala blend *better* than the old one? → **Discriminative Test** (Triangle Test)
- What exactly does the new blend *taste like*? → **Descriptive Test** (Flavor Profile)
- Will customers *buy and prefer* it? → **Affective Test** (Hedonic / Preference Test)
- Will it stay tasty after 3 months on the shelf? → **Shelf-life / Time-Intensity Test**

Sensory evaluation, in its applied form, is the **structured decision-making toolkit** that lets a food scientist answer each of these questions rigorously, just as a thermometer answers "how hot is it?" — with **numbers, statistics, and reproducible procedures** rather than guesswork.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Critic's Toolkit:**
> A trained sensory panel is to a food company what a team of expert medical diagnosticians is to a hospital — they use calibrated instruments (their senses), standardized procedures (protocols), and quantitative scales (score sheets) to translate a *subjective perception* into an *objective, actionable measurement* that drives business and engineering decisions.

## 1.3 Core Sensory Attributes (Standard KTU Metrics)

The standard sensory attributes evaluated across all food applications are:

| Sense | Attribute | Example in Food |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Sight (Visual)** | Color, Appearance, Size, Shape | Brown crust on bread |
| **Smell (Olfactory)** | Aroma, Fragrance, Off-odors | Fruity aroma of jam |
| **Taste (Gustatory)** | Sweet, Sour, Salty, Bitter, Umami | Saltiness of chips |
| **Touch (Tactile/Texture)** | Hardness, Crispness, Creaminess, Viscosity | Crunch of a biscuit |
| **Hearing (Auditory)** | Crunch, Snap, Sizzle | Snap of fresh chips |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardization Body:** The sensory protocols discussed in this module are aligned with the practices recommended by the **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM)**, **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**, and the **Food Technology & Standards Authority** methodologies followed in food industry R&D.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Application Areas of Sensory Evaluation

The applications of sensory evaluation can be classified into **eight major functional domains** within the food industry. Each domain has a specific objective, a recommended test type, and a corresponding panel composition.

### Application 1: New Product Development (NPD)
- **Objective:** Identify consumer-acceptable concepts and formulations.
- **Recommended Test:** Affective tests (9-point Hedonic, Just-About-Right (JAR) scaling, CATA — Check-All-That-Apply).
- **Panel:** Consumers (n ≥ 75–150) of the target demographic.
- **Why it matters:** ~**80–90%** of new food products fail in the marketplace. Sensory-driven NPD significantly reduces this failure rate.

### Application 2: Product Reformulation
- **Objective:** Modify existing products (e.g., reduce sugar, sodium, fat) without compromising acceptability.
- **Recommended Test:** Descriptive analysis (QDA — Quantitative Descriptive Analysis) + Affective validation.
- **Why it matters:** Driven by regulatory pressures (e.g., FSSAI sugar-cap rules) and consumer health trends.

### Application 3: Quality Control (QC) and Quality Assurance (QA)
- **Objective:** Ensure batch-to-batch consistency and conformance to specification.
- **Recommended Test:** Difference-from-control tests, Descriptive analysis against a reference standard.
- **Panel:** Trained in-house panel (8–12 panelists) or quality graders.
- **Why it matters:** A reference product acts as the "gold standard," and every production batch is compared to it.

### Application 4: Shelf-Life and Stability Studies
- **Objective:** Determine how long a product retains acceptable sensory quality under defined storage conditions.
- **Recommended Test:** Time-intensity tests, repeated descriptive profiling across storage intervals.
- **Why it matters:** Sensory shelf-life is often **shorter** than chemical/microbial shelf-life — the product is "safe" but no longer "liked."

### Application 5: Cost Reduction / Ingredient Substitution
- **Objective:** Replace an expensive ingredient with a cheaper alternative without perceptible quality loss.
- **Recommended Test:** Triangle test (ISO 4120) or paired comparison test.
- **Why it matters:** Direct impact on profit margins in B2B food manufacturing.

### Application 6: Competitive / Benchmarking Analysis
- **Objective:** Position a company's product against competitors.
- **Recommended Test:** External preference mapping (PrefMap) using both descriptive and consumer data.
- **Why it matters:** Strategic marketing and R&D direction.

### Application 7: Process Optimization
- **Objective:** Determine optimal processing parameters (time, temperature, pressure) for best sensory outcome.
- **Recommended Test:** Descriptive analysis across a process-variable matrix.
- **Why it matters:** Links engineering parameters to consumer-perceived quality.

### Application 8: Specification & Procurement Standards
- **Objective:** Define sensory acceptance criteria for raw materials and finished goods.
- **Recommended Test:** Attribute-specific rating scales anchored with reference standards.
- **Why it matters:** Enables objective purchasing and supplier rejection decisions.

## 2.2 Mapping Applications to Sensory Test Types

The **golden rule of sensory test selection** is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Match the test to the question — not the question to the test."**
> - Want to know if a difference exists? → **Discriminative Test**
> - Want to know what the difference is? → **Descriptive Test**
> - Want to know how much the difference is liked/preferred? → **Affective Test**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Sensory evaluation is largely non-mathematical, but the statistical interpretation of test results uses well-defined formulas. KTU 2024 frequently tests the statistical significance tables and decision criteria.

| Test Type | Statistic Used | Formula | Decision Rule |
|-----------|---------------|---------|---------------|
| **Triangle Test** | Number of correct responses (x) | Look up critical value table (n, α) | If x ≥ critical value → significant difference |
| **Paired Preference** | Number preferring A (x) | Binomial table lookup | If x ≥ critical value → A is significantly preferred |
| **Duo-Trio Test** | Number correct (x) | Binomial table lookup | If x ≥ critical value → significant difference |
| **Hedonic (9-pt)** | Mean score (x̄) | $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | Higher mean → greater liking |
| **Two-tailed t-test** | t-statistic | $t = \frac{\bar{x}_A - \bar{x}_B}{s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{n}}}$ | If t > t_critical (df, α) → significant |
| **ANOVA** | F-statistic | $F = \frac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}}$ | If F > F_critical → significant group effect |
| **Rank Test (Friedman)** | χ²_r statistic | $\chi_r^2 = \frac{12}{nk(k+1)} \sum R_j^2 - 3n(k+1)$ | Compare to χ² critical value |
| **Shelf-life (QDA)** | Mean panel score | Tracked vs. time | Shelf-life = time to reach rejection threshold |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Rule:** For **two-tailed triangle tests** (most common), at **α = 0.05** with n = 30 panelists, you need **≥ 13 correct responses** to claim a significant difference. With n = 50, you need **≥ 19 correct responses**. KTU examiners expect you to quote these values precisely.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

For a **Food Technologist / Food Process Engineer**, sensory evaluation applications translate directly into:

- **Process Engineering:** Choosing optimal pasteurization temperature based on sensory (not just microbial) endpoints.
- **Packaging Engineering:** Comparing barrier films for sensory impact on oxygen-sensitive products.
- **Supply Chain:** Vendor acceptance criteria for spices, oils, dairy powders.
- **Marketing & Sales:** Sensory claims substantiation on packaging ("real fruit chunks" must be sensorially provable).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 The Five-Stage Application Framework (KTU Recommended Approach)

When applying sensory evaluation to solve an industrial problem, the process follows a structured five-stage framework. This is the most frequently tested "process" question in KTU Module 4.

### Stage 1: Problem Definition
- Clearly articulate the business/research question.
- Example: *"Is the new low-fat ice cream sensorially equivalent to the full-fat version?"*
- **Output:** A one-sentence problem statement.

### Stage 2: Test Selection
- Map the problem to the correct test type using the decision matrix in §2.2.
- For the ice cream example above → **Triangle Test (ISO 4120)** or **Difference-from-Control test**.

### Stage 3: Panel Selection
- **Trained Panel** (8–12 panelists): For discriminative and descriptive tests.
- **Consumer Panel** (75–150+ untrained panelists): For affective/preference tests.
- Panelists must be **screened, trained, and calibrated**.

### Stage 4: Test Execution
- Conducted in a **controlled sensory booth** (ISO 8589 standard) under:
  - **Red lighting** (to mask color differences when testing flavor/texture)
  - **Temperature-controlled environment** (20–22 °C, ±1 °C)
  - **Neutral odor-free room** (positive air pressure, activated carbon filtration)
  - **Sample presentation:** Random 3-digit codes, balanced/randomized order to avoid positional bias.

### Stage 5: Data Analysis & Decision
- Apply the appropriate statistical test (formula sheet §2.3).
- Decide: Accept / Reject / Reformulate / Re-test.
- Document and communicate the decision.

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Triangle Test for Ingredient Substitution

**Problem:** A company wants to substitute cocoa butter with a cocoa butter equivalent (CBE) at 5% level in a chocolate bar. Will consumers notice the difference?

**Step 1 — Hypothesis Setup**

$$H_0: \text{No sensory difference between control and reformulated sample}$$
$$H_1: \text{Significant sensory difference exists}$$

**Step 2 — Sample Preparation**

$$n = 60 \text{ panelists (per ISO 4120 recommendation for triangle)}$$
$$\text{Each panelist receives 3 coded samples: 2 identical + 1 different}$$

**Step 3 — Conduct the Test**

$$\text{Panelists identify the "odd one out" and record on score sheet}$$

**Step 4 — Count Correct Responses**

$$\text{Suppose } x = 26 \text{ correct out of } n = 60$$

**Step 5 — Statistical Decision**

$$\text{Critical value at } \alpha = 0.05, \, n = 60 \text{ (triangle, two-tailed)} = 23$$

Since 26 ≥ 23, **reject H₀**. A significant difference exists.

**Step 6 — Business Decision**

> The reformulation **cannot** be implemented at the 5% CBE level. The R&D team must either:
> - Reduce CBE to <2% and re-test, OR
> - Conduct descriptive analysis to identify *which* attribute changed, OR
> - Run an affective test to determine if the difference actually matters to consumers.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Application ↔ Test ↔ Panel ↔ Output

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is the **single most important tabular answer** for any 14-mark KTU Module 4 question on applications of sensory evaluation. Memorize this structure.

| Application Area | Objective | Recommended Test | Panel Type | Sample Size | Key Output |
|------------------|-----------|------------------|------------|-------------|------------|
| **NPD** | Concept screening | Affective (Hedonic) | Consumer | 75–150 | Mean liking score |
| **NPD — Refinement** | Optimize formulation | Descriptive (QDA) + JAR | Trained + Consumer | 10–12 + 75+ | Attribute profile + penalty analysis |
| **Reformulation** | Match original profile | Difference-from-control | Trained | 20–30 | Difference score |
| **Quality Control** | Batch consistency | Triangle vs. reference | Trained | 20–30 | Pass/Fail vs. spec |
| **Shelf-life** | End of sensory acceptability | Descriptive over time | Trained | 10–12 | Time vs. quality curve |
| **Cost Reduction** | Detect substitution | Triangle / Paired | Trained | 30–60 | Significance decision |
| **Competitive Analysis** | Market positioning | Preference Mapping | Trained + Consumer | 10 + 100+ | PrefMap plot |
| **Process Optimization** | Best process variable | Descriptive + Design of Experiments | Trained | 10–12 | Optimal parameter set |
| **Procurement** | Spec compliance | Attribute rating | Trained/QC panel | 10–20 | Accept/Reject |
| **Sensory Claims** | Substantiation | Descriptive + Consumer | Trained + Consumer | 10 + 100+ | Validated claim |

## 3.4 Penalty Analysis (JAR) — Step-by-Step

Just-About-Right (JAR) scaling is a critical affective technique for NPD.

**Step 1:** Each consumer rates an attribute (e.g., sweetness) on a 5-point JAR scale:

$$\text{1 = Much too low} \quad 2 = \text{Too low} \quad 3 = \text{JAR} \quad 4 = \text{Too high} \quad 5 = \text{Much too high}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the **Percentage of JAR responses** per attribute:

$$\% JAR = \frac{\text{Number rating 3}}{\text{Total respondents}} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the **Mean Drop (Penalty)** for non-JAR responses:

$$\text{Penalty} = \text{Mean liking of non-JAR group} - \text{Mean liking of JAR group}$$

**Step 4:** Decision: If % JAR > **70%** and Penalty is small, the attribute is optimized. If Penalty > 1.0, reformulation is required.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: List any six major applications of sensory evaluation in the food industry.**

**Model Answer (6 points):**

1. **New Product Development (NPD):** Screening and finalizing product concepts before launch.
2. **Product Reformulation:** Modifying recipes (e.g., sugar/sodium reduction) while maintaining sensory quality.
3. **Quality Control and Assurance:** Ensuring batch-to-batch consistency against a reference standard.
4. **Shelf-Life Studies:** Determining the sensory end-point of a product under defined storage.
5. **Cost Reduction / Ingredient Substitution:** Detecting perceptible changes when cheaper ingredients replace expensive ones.
6. **Competitive / Benchmarking Analysis:** Comparing the company's product against market competitors.
7. **Process Optimization:** Identifying the best processing parameters (time, temperature) for sensory quality.
8. **Procurement Specification:** Defining acceptance criteria for raw materials.

*(Any six well-explained applications fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the difference between a discriminative test and an affective test? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Discriminative Test | Affective Test |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------|
| **Objective** | Detect if a difference exists | Measure preference / liking / acceptance |
| **Panel** | Trained panelists (10–30) | Consumers (75–150+) |
| **Question asked** | "Which sample is different?" | "Which do you prefer / how much do you like it?" |
| **Statistics** | Binomial tables (correct responses) | Mean hedonic score, ANOVA, preference mapping |
| **Example** | **Triangle Test (ISO 4120)** — used for ingredient substitution | **9-Point Hedonic Test** — used for consumer acceptance in NPD |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *preference* (which is an affective test) with *difference* (which is a discriminative test). Remember: preference tests assume the panelist *can* perceive the difference and then *chooses* which they prefer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: Discuss in detail the major applications of sensory evaluation in the food industry. For each application, state the test type, panel type, and expected output.**

**Model Answer (Comprehensive):**

Sensory evaluation is a **scientific decision-making tool** with applications across the entire food product lifecycle — from concept screening in R&D to consumer acceptance testing in marketing. The major applications are:

**(1) New Product Development (NPD):**
- **Test Type:** Affective tests — 9-point Hedonic, Just-About-Right (JAR), CATA (Check-All-That-Apply).
- **Panel:** 75–150+ consumers of the target demographic.
- **Output:** Mean liking scores, JAR penalty analysis, and final concept acceptance.

**(2) Product Reformulation:**
- **Test Type:** Descriptive (QDA) + Affective validation.
- **Panel:** 10–12 trained panelists for QDA, 75+ consumers for liking.
- **Output:** Optimized formula that retains the original sensory fingerprint.

**(3) Quality Control and Assurance:**
- **Test Type:** Difference-from-control test, Triangle test vs. reference.
- **Panel:** 8–12 trained in-house QC panelists.
- **Output:** Batch pass/fail decision against a defined sensory specification.

**(4) Shelf-Life and Stability Studies:**
- **Test Type:** Time-intensity tests and repeated descriptive profiling over storage intervals.
- **Panel:** 10–12 trained panelists.
- **Output:** Sensory shelf-life curve and recommended expiry date.

**(5) Cost Reduction / Ingredient Substitution:**
- **Test Type:** Triangle test (ISO 4120) or Paired Comparison.
- **Panel:** 30–60 trained panelists.
- **Output:** Statistical decision on whether the substitution is sensorially detectable.

**(6) Competitive / Benchmarking Analysis:**
- **Test Type:** External Preference Mapping combining descriptive and consumer data.
- **Panel:** 10 trained + 100+ consumer panelists.
- **Output:** PrefMap plot showing product positioning in the sensory space.

**(7) Process Optimization:**
- **Test Type:** Descriptive analysis within a Design of Experiments (DoE) matrix.
- **Panel:** 10–12 trained panelists.
- **Output:** Optimal process parameters (e.g., pasteurization temperature, baking time).

**(8) Procurement / Specification Standards:**
- **Test Type:** Attribute-specific rating scales anchored with reference standards.
- **Panel:** 10–20 trained QC graders.
- **Output:** Vendor acceptance/rejection and a written sensory specification.

> All these applications collectively support the **commercial success, regulatory compliance, and consumer satisfaction** of food products.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) Part (b)
**Q: A food company is reformulating a biscuit to reduce sugar by 30%. Outline the step-by-step sensory protocol they should follow.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Problem Definition (1 mark):**
Clearly state the objective: *"Determine if the 30%-sugar-reduced biscuit is sensorially acceptable and indistinguishable (or, if distinguishable, equally liked) compared to the original."*

**Step 2 — Test Selection (1 mark):**
Conduct a **Triangle test (ISO 4120)** first to detect difference, followed by a **9-Point Hedonic test** with 100 consumers to measure liking.

**Step 3 — Panel Recruitment (1 mark):**
- Triangle test: 30–60 trained panelists.
- Hedonic test: 100+ consumer panelists representative of the target market.

**Step 4 — Sample Preparation & Presentation (2 marks):**
- Prepare both formulations under identical processing conditions.
- Use **3-digit random codes**, randomized serving order.
- Conduct tests in **ISO 8589 sensory booths** (20–22 °C, red lighting optional, neutral odor).
- Use water and unsalted crackers as palate cleansers.

**Step 5 — Data Analysis & Decision (2 marks):**
- Triangle test: Count correct responses. Compare with critical value table (α = 0.05, n = 50 → critical value = 19).
- Hedonic test: Compute mean liking scores and apply ANOVA / t-test.
- If a significant difference exists but liking is still ≥ 7 on the 9-point scale → **accept the reformulation**.
- If liking drops below 6 → **reformulate further** (e.g., use a natural sweetener as sugar replacer) and re-test.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the **palate cleansing** step, **3-digit coding** of samples, and the need to **balance/randomize** serving order. These are procedural essentials worth 2–3 marks in any 14-mark answer. Also, you must state the **critical value** for the chosen test — examiners expect numeric answers, not just "statistically significant."

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Q-A) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: Explain the role of sensory evaluation in (i) Quality Control, (ii) Shelf-life studies, and (iii) Cost-reduction through ingredient substitution. Compare the panel requirements for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Quality Control (4 marks):**
Sensory evaluation in QC ensures that every production batch matches a **pre-defined reference (gold) standard**. The procedure involves:
- A trained in-house panel of 8–12 members conducts **triangle tests** or **difference-from-control** evaluations on every production batch.
- Results are compared against a **sensory specification** (e.g., "biscuit must score ≥ 7/10 for crispness, 6/10 for sweetness").
- A batch failing the sensory spec is rejected or reworked, even if it passes chemical/microbial tests.

**(ii) Shelf-Life Studies (4 marks):**
Sensory shelf-life is the period during which a product retains its **desired sensory characteristics** under specified storage. The procedure involves:
- Storing samples under **real or accelerated conditions** (e.g., 25 °C, 35 °C, 45 °C).
- Conducting **descriptive analysis** at fixed intervals (0, 1, 2, 3, 6 months).
- Plotting **mean sensory scores vs. time** to identify the rejection threshold (typically a 1-point drop on a 9-point scale).
- The time at which the threshold is crossed is the **sensory shelf-life**, which is often shorter than the microbial shelf-life.

**(iii) Cost Reduction through Ingredient Substitution (3 marks):**
- The objective is to replace an **expensive ingredient** (e.g., cocoa butter, vanilla extract) with a **cheaper alternative** (CBE, synthetic vanillin) without consumer-perceptible quality loss.
- **Triangle tests (ISO 4120)** with 30–60 trained panelists are the industry standard.
- If a significant difference is detected, the cheaper alternative is rejected or used at a lower inclusion rate.
- Descriptive profiling is then used to identify *which* attribute changed, guiding further R&D.

**Panel Requirement Comparison (3 marks):**

| Application | Panel Size | Panel Type | Training Level |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| **QC** | 8–12 | In-house trained | Highly trained, calibrated on reference |
| **Shelf-life** | 10–12 | Trained sensory panel | Trained, calibrated over multiple sessions |
| **Cost Reduction** | 30–60 | Trained or semi-trained | Moderately trained |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is stating "consumers" as panelists for QC and shelf-life studies. These applications require **trained panels** — not consumers. Only **affective tests** (NPD, market research) use consumer panels.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) Part (b)
**Q: What is a sensory booth? List the essential features as per ISO 8589.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A **sensory booth** is a **standardized, controlled, individual testing compartment** used to isolate panelists from external distractions and biases while evaluating food samples. It is the cornerstone of any reproducible sensory test.

**Essential Features as per ISO 8589:**

1. **Physical Separation (1 mark):** Individual booths separated by partitions (≥ 1 m apart) to prevent panelist-to-panelist communication.

2. **Lighting Control (1 mark):** Adjustable, color-corrected lighting (e.g., D65 daylight). For color-masking tests, **red lighting** is used to eliminate visual differences.

3. **Temperature & Ventilation Control (1 mark):** Maintained at **20–22 °C ± 1 °C**, with **positive air pressure** and **activated carbon filtration** to remove odors.

4. **Sample Server Window (1 mark):** A hatch through which the server passes coded samples to the panelist, preventing server bias.

5. **Neutral Color Scheme (1 mark):** Walls, ceiling, and surfaces in **white, gray, or beige** to avoid color-induced bias.

6. **Data Entry System (1 mark):** Computerized or paper-based score sheets at each booth for direct, blind data capture.

7. **Noise Control (1 mark):** Soundproof or low-noise environment to prevent auditory bias (e.g., masking the crunch sound when testing flavor).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **red lighting option** for color-masking, the **positive air pressure** for odor control, and the **sample server window** for unbiased sample delivery. Each of these is a 1-mark item that examiners specifically look for.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in Module 4 (Applications) Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "difference" with "preference":** A triangle test detects a difference; it does NOT tell you which sample is preferred. KTU examiners routinely check this distinction.
>
> 2. **Quoting "consumers" when "trained panelists" are required:** For QC, shelf-life, and reformulation studies, only trained panels are used. Saying "100 consumers" for a QC test loses 2–3 marks immediately.
>
> 3. **Forgetting critical statistical values:** When discussing triangle/paired tests, you must quote the **critical value from the binomial table** (e.g., "for n=30, α=0.05, two-tailed, critical value = 13"). Generic statements like "the result was significant" are insufficient.
>
> 4. **Missing procedural controls:** Always mention **3-digit coding, randomization, palate cleansers, and ISO 8589 booth conditions** when describing a sensory protocol.
>
> 5. **Ignoring business context:** Sensory evaluation is a *decision-making tool*. Always end your answer with a **business outcome** (accept / reject / reformulate / re-test) — not just a statistical conclusion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Application of Sensory Evaluation**

- ☐ **Sensory evaluation** is a *scientific decision-making tool*, not a subjective opinion.

- ☐ The **three classes** of sensory tests are **Discriminative, Descriptive, and Affective** — each suited to a specific question.

- ☐ **Eight major applications:** NPD, Reformulation, QC/QA, Shelf-life, Cost Reduction, Competitive Analysis, Process Optimization, Procurement.

- ☐ **Discriminative tests** (Triangle, Paired, Duo-Trio, Ranking) → answer *"Is there a difference?"* — use **trained panels**.

- ☐ **Descriptive tests** (QDA, Spectrum, Free Choice Profiling) → answer *"What is the difference?"* — use **trained panels** (10–12).

- ☐ **Affective tests** (9-pt Hedonic, JAR, Preference, CATA) → answer *"How much is it liked/preferred?"* — use **consumer panels** (75–150+).

- ☐ **Critical values for triangle test (α=0.05, two-tailed):** n=20→9, n=30→13, n=50→19, n=60→23.

- ☐ **Sensory booth (ISO 8589):** 20–22 °C, neutral color, red-lighting option, positive air pressure, server window, individual partitions.

- ☐ **Penalty analysis (JAR):** %JAR > 70% and small penalty → attribute optimized; penalty > 1.0 → reformulation needed.

- ☐ **Sensory shelf-life** is often **shorter than microbial shelf-life** — the product may be "safe" but no longer "liked."

- ☐ **Three-digit coding, randomization, palate cleansers** are **non-negotiable** procedural controls.

- ☐ Always end a sensory application answer with a **business decision** — accept, reject, reformulate, or re-test.

- ☐ Memorize the **Application ↔ Test ↔ Panel ↔ Output** matrix (§3.3) — it is the single most valuable tabular answer for any 14-mark question.

- ☐ **KTU Examiner's favorite questions:**
  1. List applications with test types and panels. *(7 marks)*
  2. Design a sensory protocol for a given industrial scenario (reformulation, substitution, shelf-life). *(7 marks)*
  3. Explain sensory booth / ISO 8589 features. *(5–7 marks)*
  4. Discuss QC and shelf-life applications. *(7–10 marks)*
